Ayahs

يسٓ (١)
Ya Seen! (1)
وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
By the Wise Quran, (2)
إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٣)
you are indeed one of the apostles, (3)
عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤)
on a straight path. (4)
تَنزِيلَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٥)
[It is a scripture] sent down gradually from the All-mighty, the All-merciful (5)
لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أُنذِرَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٦)
that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, so they are oblivious. (6)
لَقَدْ حَقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَىٰٓ أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٧)
The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not have faith. (7)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ أَغْلَـٰلًا فَهِىَ إِلَى ٱلْأَذْقَانِ فَهُم مُّقْمَحُونَ (٨)
Indeed We have put iron collars around their necks, which are up to the chins, so their heads are upturned. (8)
وَجَعَلْنَا مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٩)
And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, then We have blind-folded them, so they do not see. (9)
وَسَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠)
It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. (10)

Content of Surah "Ya-Sin"

As we know, this Surah was revealed in "Makkah"; therefore, its general content aligns with the universal themes of Makkan Surahs, which discuss Monotheism (Tawhid), the Hereafter (Ma'ad), Revelation, the Quran, warnings, and glad tidings. Four major sections are specifically observed in this Surah:

  1. First, it speaks of the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the Holy Quran, the purpose of the revelation of this great Divine Book, and those who believe in it; this continues until verse eleven.

  2. Another section of this Surah speaks of the mission of three Divine prophets, how they called people toward Monotheism, and their persistent and grueling struggle against polytheism. This serves as a form of consolation and encouragement for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and provides a roadmap for fulfilling his great mission.

  3. A section of this Surah, starting from verse 33 and continuing to verse 44, is filled with fascinating points regarding Monotheism and eloquent descriptions of the signs and manifestations of the Lord's greatness in the universe. The latter parts of the Surah also return to this discussion of Monotheism and Divine signs.

  4. Another important section of this Surah discusses matters related to the "Hereafter," its various proofs, the nature of the Resurrection and Gathering, the questioning and answering on the Day of Judgment, the end of the world, and Paradise and Hell. Very important and subtle points are hidden within this section.

Interspersed throughout these four sections are soul-shaking verses intended to awaken and alert the heedless and the unaware, which have a powerful impact on hearts and souls.

In summary: In this Surah, man encounters various scenes of creation, resurrection, life, death, warnings, and glad tidings, which together form an awakening collection and a healing prescription.

The Virtue of Surah "Ya-Sin"

Surah "Ya-Sin," according to numerous hadiths narrated on this subject, is one of the most important Surahs of the Quran, to the extent that it has been called the "Heart of the Quran" in traditions.

In a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, everything has a heart, and the heart of the Quran is Ya-Sin." [^1]

The same meaning is narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, which adds: "Whoever recites Ya-Sin in his day before evening shall be among the protected and the sustained during his day until evening; and whoever recites it at night before sleeping, Allah appoints a thousand angels to protect him from every accursed devil and every affliction..." and he continues to mention other significant virtues. [^1]

Again, in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Surah Ya-Sin is called 'The Universalizer' (al-Mu'immah) in the Torah!" It was asked, "What is 'The Universalizer'?" He replied, "It encompasses its companion with the good of this world and the Hereafter..." [^2]

Other narrations on this subject exist in both Shi'a and Sunni books, which would be lengthy to quote in full.

Thus, it must be admitted that perhaps few Surahs in the Holy Quran possess such immense virtue.

And as we have said many times, this virtue is not for those who merely recite its words and cast its concepts into the arch of oblivion; rather, this greatness is due to the magnificent content of this Surah.

A content that awakens, grants faith, creates responsibility, and fosters piety—which, when a person reflects upon it and this reflection shines upon his actions, brings him the good of this world and the Hereafter.

For example, in verse 60 of this Surah, it speaks of a covenant that Allah took from all the children of Adam not to worship Satan, for Satan is a manifest enemy: "أَ لَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ".

Clearly, whenever a person adheres to this Divine covenant, as mentioned in the aforementioned hadiths, he will be safe from every accursed devil. But if he recites this verse superficially while in practice being among the sincere friends and loyal allies of Satan, he cannot attain this great honor. This same calculation must be applied to every single verse and word of this Surah.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

Ya-Sin (1) By the wise Quran (2) Indeed, you are among the messengers (3) On a straight path (4) A revelation of the Almighty, the Merciful (5) That you may warn a people whose forefathers were not warned, so they are heedless (6) Already the word has come true against most of them, so they do not believe (7) Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks, reaching to the chins, so their heads are forced up (8) And We have set a barrier before them and a barrier behind them and covered them, so they do not see (9) And it is all the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them—they will not believe (10)

Commentary

The Beginning of the "Heart of the Quran"

This Surah, like 28 other Surahs of the Holy Quran, begins with "Disconnected Letters" (Ya and Sin).

Regarding the interpretation of the Disconnected Letters at the beginning of Surahs "Al-Baqarah," "Al-Imran," and "Al-A'raf," we have had extensive discussions [^1], but specifically regarding Surah "Ya-Sin," other interpretations exist for these letters:

Among them is that this word is composed of "Ya" (a vocative particle) and "Sin," referring to the person of the Prophet of Islam himself. Thus, it addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to state the subsequent matters.

It is also mentioned in various hadiths that this word is one of the names of the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. [^2] Another view is that the addressee here is "Man" (Insan), and "Sin" refers to him, but this possibility does not align with the subsequent verses, as the address in those verses is solely to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Therefore, we read in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Ya-Sin is a name of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and the proof for that is His saying: 'Indeed, you are among the messengers, on a straight path.'" (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 375).

Following these Disconnected Letters—as in many Surahs that begin with them—it speaks of the Holy Quran, except that here He swears by it, saying: "By the wise Quran" (وَ الْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ).

Interestingly, He describes the "Quran" as "Wise" (Hakim), whereas wisdom is usually an attribute of a living, rational being. It is as if He introduces the Quran as a living, rational entity, a leader and a guide that can open the doors of wisdom to humanity and guide them to the "Straight Path" mentioned in the following verses.

Of course, Allah has no need for an oath, but the oaths of the Quran always serve two important purposes: first, to emphasize the matter, and second, to express the greatness of the thing sworn by, for no one swears by insignificant things.

The next verse states the purpose for which the oath in the previous verse was taken, saying: "Indeed, you are among the messengers of Allah" (إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

"A mission accompanied by truth, and your being on a straight path" (عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ). [^1]

Then He adds: "This is a Quran revealed from the Almighty, the Merciful" (تَنْزِيلَ الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ). [^2] The emphasis on Allah being "Almighty" (Al-Aziz) is to express His power over such a great, invincible Book that remains a perpetual miracle throughout all ages and centuries, and no power can erase its greatness from the pages of hearts.

And the emphasis on Allah's "Mercifulness" (Al-Rahim) is to express the reality that His mercy necessitated placing such a great blessing at the disposal of humanity.

Some commentators believe these two descriptions represent two different types of reactions people might show toward the revelation of this Divine Book and the sending of this Messenger:

If they rise to deny and reject, Allah threatens them with His Might and Power; and if they enter through submission and acceptance, Allah gives them glad tidings with His Mercy. [^1] Therefore, His Might and Mercy—one being the manifestation of "Warning" (Indhar) and the other the manifestation of "Glad Tidings" (Bisharat)—are blended together as He provides this great Divine Book to mankind.

A question arises here: Can the truth of a Prophet or a Divine Book be proven by oaths and emphasis?

The answer to this question is hidden within the verses themselves. On one hand, He describes the Quran as "Wise," indicating that its wisdom is hidden from no one and is itself a proof of its own truth.

On the other hand, He describes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as traveling upon the "Straight Path," meaning the content of his call itself demonstrates that his path is a straight one, and his life's history also shows that he has no path other than the straight one.

We have noted in discussions on the proofs of the truth of prophets that one of the best ways to realize their truth is to carefully examine the content of their call. Whenever it is consistent with nature (fitrah), reason, and conscience, and is at a level impossible for a human with mere human power—and furthermore, the Prophet's life history showed him to be a man of integrity and honesty, not lies and deceit—these matters become living evidence that he is a messenger of God. The above verses, in reality, point to both these matters; therefore, the oath and the claim are by no means without proof.

Furthermore, from the perspective of the art of debate, to influence the hearts of stubborn deniers, the firmer, more decisive, and more emphasized the expressions used, the more they affect such individuals.

Another question arises: Why is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself the addressee in this sentence, rather than the polytheists or the general public?

The answer is that the goal was to emphasize: "You are on the truth and on the straight path, whether they accept it or not; therefore, be diligent in your heavy mission and do not allow the slightest weakness to enter you due to the rejection of the opponents."

The next verse explains the primary goal of the Quran's revelation as follows: "We revealed the Quran to you so that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, and for this reason, they are plunged into heedlessness" (لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أُنْذِرَ آبائهم فهم غافلون). [^1]

Certainly, the "people" referred to are the Arab polytheists. If it is said that according to our belief, no nation has been without a warner, and the earth will never be empty of Allah's Proof (Hujjah)—furthermore, we read in Surah Fatir, verse 24: "And there was no nation but that there had passed within it a warner" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ).

In response, we say that the verse under discussion refers to a manifest warner and a great Prophet whose fame spreads everywhere. Otherwise, in every age, a Divine Proof exists for those who seek and desire it. If we see that the period between Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and the rise of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is considered a "period of interval" (fatrah), it does not mean that Divine Proof absolutely did not exist for them; rather, the interval is in terms of the rise of the great "Arch-Prophets" (Ulul-'Azm).

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in this regard: "Indeed, Allah sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) when none of the Arabs read a book or claimed prophethood!" (Nahj al-Balagha, Sermons 33 and 104).

In any case, the goal of the Quran's revelation was to alert the heedless, awaken those overtaken by sleep, and remind them of the dangers surrounding them, the sins they are plunged into, and the polytheism and corruption they are tainted with. Yes, the Quran is the foundation of awareness and awakening, and the book for purifying the heart and soul.

Then the Quran, as a prophecy regarding the leaders of disbelief and the heads of polytheism, says: "The Divine word and promise has been realized against most of them, and for this reason, they do not believe" (لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى‌ أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Commentators have offered several possibilities for what "the word" (al-qawl) refers to here, but it apparently refers to the promise of the punishment of Hell for the followers of devils, as stated in Surah As-Sajdah, verse 13: "But the word from Me will come true that I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together" (وَ لكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ). And in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 71, we read: "But the word of punishment has come true against the disbelievers" (وَ لكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ).

In any case, this applies to those who have cut all their lines of communication with Allah, severed all ties, closed all windows of guidance upon themselves, and reached the peak of stubbornness, enmity, and defiance. Yes, these will never believe and have no way back because they have destroyed all bridges behind them.

The truth is that a person is reformable and capable of being guided only as long as he has not completely trampled his monotheistic nature (fitrah) with his evil deeds and tainted character; otherwise, absolute darkness will overcome his heart, and all openings of hope will be closed to him.

Incidentally, it becomes clear from this statement that the "majority" who will never believe refers to the leaders of polytheism and disbelief. And so it was that a group was killed in the Islamic wars while in a state of polytheism and idolatry, and some who remained did not have faith in their hearts until the end. Otherwise, the majority of Arab polytheists entered Islam in groups after the conquest of Makkah, according to the verse: "Entering the religion of Allah in multitudes" (Surah An-Nasr, verse 2).

The subsequent verses, which speak of a barrier before them and behind them, describe their eyes as blind, and state that warning or not warning them is the same, serve as evidence for this same meaning. [^1]

In any case, the next verse continues the description of this impenetrable group. In the first description, it says: "Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks reaching to their chins, and their heads are forced up" (إِنَّا جَعَلْنا فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ أَغْلالًا فَهِيَ إِلَى الْأَذْقانِ فَهُمْ مُقْمَحُونَ).

"Aghlal" is the plural of "ghull," which originally comes from the root "ghall," meaning something placed in the middle of things; for example, flowing water passing through trees is called "ghalal." A "ghull" was a ring placed on the neck or hands, then fastened with a chain. Since the neck or hand is placed inside it, this word is used for it. Sometimes the shackles on the neck were chained separately, and those on the hands were separate, but sometimes the hands were placed in shackles and tied to the ring on the neck, placing the prisoner or captive under severe restriction, pressure, and torture.

If the state of thirst or intense grief and anger is called "ghullah," it is also because of the penetration of this state into the heart and body of man. Fundamentally, the root "ghall" means both to enter and to make enter; hence, the income from a house or crop is called "ghallah." [^2]

In any case, sometimes the collar of the "shackle" placed on the neck would extend to the chin, keeping the head held high, and while the captive suffered extraordinary torture from this, he was prevented from observing his surroundings.

How striking is the comparison made between the state of stubborn idolaters and such people! They have bound the collar of "imitation" and the chain of "superstitious customs and traditions" around their necks, hands, and feet, and their shackles are so wide and expansive that they keep their heads held high and deprive them of seeing the truths. They are captives who have neither the power of activity and movement nor the power of sight! [^1]

In any case, the above verse can be a depiction of the state of this faithless group in this world, as well as a description of their state in the Hereafter, which is a manifestation of the issues of this world. If this sentence is mentioned in the past tense, it poses no problem, as in many verses of the Holy Quran, certain future events are expressed with the past tense verb form; this is what is known among linguists as "the future whose occurrence is certain is expressed in the past tense." It can also refer to both meanings: their state in this world and their state in the next.

A group of commentators has mentioned "reasons for revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for the above verse and the one following it, stating they were revealed regarding "Abu Jahl" or a man from the tribe of "Bani Makhzum" or the "Quraysh" tribe. They repeatedly decided to kill the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but Allah prevented them through miracles. At that sensitive moment when they reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and wanted to strike a fatal blow, their eyes failed or the power of movement was taken from them. [^1] However, these reasons for revelation do not prevent the generality of the verse's concept and the breadth of its meaning regarding all leaders of disbelief and stubborn bigots. Furthermore, it confirms what we stated earlier in the interpretation of the phrase "so they do not believe"—that it does not refer to the majority of polytheists, but to the majority of the leaders of polytheism, disbelief, and hypocrisy.

The next verse is another description of these same individuals, providing a vivid depiction of the factors behind their impenetrability. It says: "And We have set a barrier before them and a barrier behind them" (وجَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا).

They are so surrounded by these two barriers that they have neither a way forward nor a way back! And at the same time, "We covered their eyes, so they do not see" (فَأَغْشَيْناهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُبْصِرُونَ).

What a vivid depiction! On one hand, they are like captives in shackles and chains.

On the other hand, the ring of the shackle is so wide and expansive that it has turned their heads toward the sky, and they see absolutely nothing of their surroundings!

From a third perspective, a barrier from before them and behind them has besieged them, closing the way forward and backward.

From a fourth perspective, their eyes are closed, and their power of sight and vision has completely failed.

Think well: what can someone with such attributes do? What does he understand? What can he see? And how can he take a step? Such is the state of the arrogant, self-centered, and self-conceited, the blind and deaf followers, and the stubborn bigots in the face of the countenance of truths!

For this reason, in the last verse under discussion, it explicitly says: "It is all the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them—they will not believe!" (وَ سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَ أَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

No matter how influential your speech and how effective the Divine revelation, it will not take effect unless it enters a prepared ground. If the world-illuminating sun shines on salt marshes for thousands of years, and blessed rains fall upon them, and the spring breeze regularly passes over them, they will produce nothing but weeds and thorns; for the receptivity of the recipient is a condition alongside the efficacy of the agent.

Key Points

1- The Failure of the Tools of Cognition!

To become aware of the world outside himself, man utilizes means and tools called "tools of cognition."

Some of them are "intrinsic," and some are "extrinsic." Reason, intellect, conscience, and nature (fitrah) are among the intrinsic tools of cognition, while man's external senses, such as sight and hearing, are extrinsic tools of cognition.

If these God-given means are utilized correctly, they become stronger and more powerful day by day, showing truths better and more accurately.

But if they are placed in deviant paths for a while, or not used at all, they gradually atrophy or change completely, showing truths in reverse. It is just like a clear mirror covered by thick dust or deep scratches, which can no longer show anything, and if it does, it never corresponds to reality.

It is man's own incorrect actions and deviant stances that deprive him of the great blessing of the tools of cognition; therefore, the primary culprit is he himself, and the sin lies upon his own neck.

The above verses are a vivid depiction of this important and decisive issue. They compare the arrogant pleasure-seekers, the stubborn self-centered ones, and the unconditional followers to those who, on one hand, are trapped in shackles and chains—these are the very chains of desire, pride, arrogance, and blind imitation that they themselves have placed on their own hands and necks—and compare them to those besieged by two powerful and impassable barriers.

On the other hand, their eyes are also closed and blind.

Shackles and chains alone are enough to prevent their movement; those two massive barriers alone prevent their activity; and the lack of eyes and blindness is itself an independent factor.

It is as if these two barriers are so high and close that they alone take away their power of sight, just as they deprive them of the power of movement.

We have repeatedly said that man's capacity for guidance remains as long as he has not reached this stage. But once he reaches this stage, even if all the prophets and saints were to gather and recite all the Divine Books to him, it would not be effective!

The emphasis in Islamic narrations and Quranic verses that if a slip occurs for a person or a sin is committed, he should immediately repent and return to Allah, and avoid "procrastination," "delay," "persistence," and "repetition," is so that he does not reach this stage. He should wash away the rust, destroy small obstacles before they turn into a large barrier, keep the path of progress and movement open, and settle the dust from before his eyes "so that he may be able to see."

2- Barriers from Before and Behind!

A question has been raised by some commentators: the main obstacle to continuing movement is the barrier in front; what is the meaning of the barrier behind?

Some have answered: Man possesses two types of guidance: theoretical and deductive guidance, and natural and intuitive guidance. The barrier in front indicates that he is deprived of theoretical guidance; he wants to turn back and look at natural guidance, but the barrier and obstacle behind prevents him from returning to his nature (fitrah). [^1]

Others have said the barrier in front refers to obstacles that prevent him from reaching the Hereafter and eternal happiness, and the barrier behind is something that prevents him even from reaching happiness and peace in this world. [^2]

The possibility also exists in the interpretation of the verse that when a person encounters an obstacle on the path to a destination, he turns back to find another way to the destination. But when a barrier is created on both sides, he is deprived of finding a way to the destination in any case.

Furthermore, the answer to why no mention was made of barriers on the right and left also became clear: because going to the right or left never brings a person to the destination; he must open a way forward.

Additionally, a barrier is usually created in a place where the right and left sides are closed, and only a passage exists between them; by creating a barrier, that passage is also closed, and practically, the person is besieged.

3- Deprivation of Horizons and Souls To know Allah, there are usually two paths for study: studying the signs of Allah within the human body and soul, called "Signs within the Souls" (Ayat al-Anfusi), and studying the signs that exist outside his being in the earth, the heavens, the fixed stars, the planets, the mountains, and the seas, called "Signs in the Horizons" (Ayat al-Afaqi). The Holy Quran refers to this in Surah Fussilat, verse 53, saying: "We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves until it becomes clear to them that He is the Truth" (سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ).

When man's power of cognition fails, both the path of observing the signs within the soul and the path of observing the signs in the horizons are closed to him.

In the above verses, the phrase "Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks reaching to the chins, so their heads are forced up" refers to the first meaning, because the shackles keep their heads held so high that they do not even have the power to see themselves. And the barriers before and behind so prevent their eyes from observing their surroundings that whatever they look at, they see nothing but the wall of the barrier, and they are deprived of observing the signs in the horizons.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَخَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَأَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ (١١)
You can only warn someone who follows the Reminder and fears the All-beneficent in secret; so give him the good news of forgiveness and a noble reward. (11)
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا۟ وَءَاثَـٰرَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ فِىٓ إِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٢)
Indeed it is We who revive the dead and write what they have sent ahead and their effects [which they left behind], and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam. (12)

Commentary

Who Will Accept Your Warning?

Who Will Accept Your Warning?

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on a group who are in no way prepared to accept divine warnings, and to whom warning or lack thereof is the same. However, the verses under discussion speak of another group who stand at the exact opposite pole, so that by comparing the two—as is the Quranic method of discussion—the matter becomes clearer.

It says: "You can only warn him who follows the 'Reminder' (the Dhikr) and fears the Rahman (the All-Merciful) in secret" (إِنَّما تُنْذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِّكْرَ وَ خَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ).

"And whoever is such, give him the glad tidings of forgiveness and a noble reward" (فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَ أَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ).

Here, several points should be noted

  1. In this verse, two descriptions are mentioned for those upon whom the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) "warning" and "admonition" are effective: following the Reminder, and fearing Allah in secret. Of course, the purpose of stating these two descriptions is to highlight their readiness and "potential" aspect; that is, the warning only becomes effective in those who have a listening ear and a prepared heart. The warning produces two effects in them: first, following the Reminder and the Quran, and second, a sense of fear regarding the Lord and one's responsibilities.

In other words, these two states exist potentially within them, but they reach actuality after the warning, unlike the stubborn and heedless blind-hearted who neither have a listening ear nor are ready for fear (khashyah).

This verse is exactly like the opening verses of Surah Al-Baqarah which say: ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah."

  1. According to many commentators, the "Reminder" (Dhikr) refers to the Holy Quran, as this word has been used repeatedly in the Quran in this same sense [1]. However, there is no obstacle to it referring to its literal meaning, i.e., any kind of reminder,

[1]: An-Nahl: 44, Fussilat: 41, Az-Zukhruf: 44, Al-Qamar: 25; at the same time, the word "Dhikr" in the sense of absolute remembrance has also been used frequently in the Quran.

which would include the verses of the Quran and all other warnings of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and divine leaders.

  1. "Khashyah", as we have mentioned previously, refers to a fear that is mixed with a sense of awe and greatness. The use of the name "Rahman," which is the manifestation of Allah's general mercy, contains a subtle point here: while fearing the greatness of Allah, they must also have hope in His mercy, so that the two scales of "fear" (khawf) and "hope" (raja)—which are the factors for continuous evolutionary movement—remain balanced. It is interesting that in some verses of the Quran regarding "hope," the name "Allah" is mentioned, which is the manifestation of awe and majesty (لِمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا اللَّهَ وَ الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ - Al-Ahzab: 21), indicating that hope must be mixed with fear, and fear with hope (reflect on this).
  1. The expression "in secret" (bil-ghayb) here refers to knowing Allah through reasoning and proof, since His Pure Essence is hidden from human senses; His beauty and glory can only be observed with the eye of the heart and through His signs. There is also the possibility that "ghayb" here means hidden from the eyes of the people; that is, the state of "khashyah" and fear of Him should not be for show or in the presence of people, but rather he should possess fear even in private.

Some have also interpreted it as "the Resurrection," as it is one of the clear instances of matters hidden from our senses, but the first meaning seems most appropriate of all.

  1. The phrase "give him glad tidings" (fabashshirhu) is in fact the completion of the "warning," because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)

warns at the beginning, and when following Allah's command and fear mixed with awe toward Him is established and its effects appear in the "speech" and "action" of the human, he gives glad tidings.

Of what does he give glad tidings? First, of something that occupies the human mind more than any other subject: the slips that may have occasionally occurred. He gives him the glad tidings that the Great God has forgiven all of them. Then, of a "noble reward" (ajrin karim) and a highly valuable prize whose dimensions no one but Allah knows. It is interesting that both "forgiveness" (maghfirah) and "noble reward" are mentioned in the indefinite form (nakirah), and we know that using the indefinite form in such cases is to express greatness.

  1. Some commentators believe that the "fa" in the phrase "fabashshirhu," which is for derivation (tafri'), indicates that those two actions (following the Reminder and fearing the Lord) result in these two effects: forgiveness and a noble reward—the first stemming from the first, and the second from the second.

Then, in accordance with the discussion in the previous verses regarding the valuable reward of the believers and those who accept the warnings of the prophets, the next verse points to the issue of "the Hereafter, the Resurrection, and the recording and preservation of deeds for accounting and retribution," saying: "Indeed, it is We who bring the dead to life" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌). The emphasis on the pronoun "We" (Nahnu) indicates that with the Great Power that everyone recognizes in Us, there is no longer room for debate or discussion about how decayed bones and dust will take life anew and don the garment of existence.

Not only do We bring the dead to life, but "We record what they have sent before

and their traces" (وَ نَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَ آثارَهُمْ‌).

Therefore, nothing will be omitted; rather, everything will be preserved in the record of deeds for the Day of Reckoning. The phrase "what they have sent before" (ma qaddamu) refers to the deeds they performed of which no trace remains, while the expression "and their traces" (wa atharahum) refers to the deeds that remain after the person and whose effects are reflected in the environment, such as ongoing charities (sadaqah jariyah)—buildings, endowments, and centers that remain after a person and from which people benefit.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "ma qaddamu" refers to deeds that are personal in nature, and "atharahum" refers to actions that become a tradition (sunnah) and continue to cause goodness and blessing, or evil, harm, and sin after the person is gone. Of course, the concept of the verse is broad and it is possible for both interpretations to be included in the meaning.

And at the end of the verse, for further emphasis, it adds: "And We have enumerated everything in a clear register" (وَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

Most commentators have interpreted "Imamum Mubin" (Clear Register) here as the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh al-Mahfuz)—the very book in which all deeds, all beings, and all events of this world are recorded and preserved. The use of the term "Imam" may be because this book on the Day of Resurrection is a leader and guide for all the agents of reward and punishment, and a criterion for measuring the value of human deeds and their reward and retribution.

It is interesting that this term ("Imam") has been used in some other verses of the Quran regarding the "Torah," where it says: أَ فَمَنْ كانَ عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَ يَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ وَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى‌ إِماماً وَ رَحْمَةً: "Is he then [like] one who is on a clear proof from his Lord, and a witness from Him follows him, and before it was the Book of Moses as a guide (Imam) and a mercy?"

The application of the word "Imam" in this verse to the Torah is because of its knowledge, laws, and commands, and also because of the signs of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) mentioned therein; in all these matters, it could be a leader and guide for the creation. Therefore, the said word in each case has a meaning appropriate to it.

Key Points

1. Types of Books for Recording Deeds

From the verses of the Holy Quran, it is understood that human deeds are recorded and preserved in several books, so that at the time of accounting, no excuse or pretext remains for anyone.

First is the "personal record of deeds," which records all the actions of an individual throughout his entire life. The Quran says: On the Day of Resurrection, everyone will be told: اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى‌ بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً: "Read your record. Sufficient is yourself against you this Day as accountant" (Al-Isra: 14).

It is here that the cry of the criminals rises, saying: "Woe to us! What is this book that leaves nothing small or great except that it has enumerated it?" يَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابُ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها (Al-Kahf: 49).

This is the same book that "the righteous hold in their right hand and the wicked

in their left hand" (Al-Haqqah: 19 and 25).

Second is the book which is the "record of deeds of nations," expressing the social lines of their lives, as the Quran says: كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى‌ إِلى‌ كِتابِهَا: "Every nation will be called to its record" (Al-Jathiyah: 28).

And the third book is the "comprehensive and general record, the Preserved Tablet," in which not only the deeds of all humans from the first to the last, but all the events of the world are recorded together. It is another witness in that great scene over the deeds of man, and in reality, it is an Imam and leader for the angels of accounting and the angels of reward and punishment [1].

2. Everything is Recorded, Even...

In an eloquent and awakening Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) stopped at a barren land and said to his companions: "Bring firewood." They said: "O Messenger of Allah! We are in a barren land!" He said: "Let every person bring whatever he can." So they brought it until they threw it before him, some on top of others. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "This is how sins accumulate." Then he said: "Beware of the belittled sins, for everything has a seeker. Behold, the seeker of them writes what they have sent before and their traces, and everything We have enumerated in a clear register."

This shaking Hadith is a vivid depiction of the accumulation of seemingly insignificant sins and the great fire that blazes from their sum.

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that the tribe of "Banu Salimah" were located in a distant part of the city of Medina. They decided to move near the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mosque. The above verse was revealed: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌... and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to them:

"Indeed, your traces are being written."

"Your traces (your steps toward the mosque) will be written in your record of deeds (and you will be rewarded)." When Banu Salimah heard this, they reconsidered and stayed in their place [2]. It is clear that the verse has a broad and expansive meaning, of which each of these matters is an instance.

What might at first glance seem inconsistent with the above interpretation are narrations from the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) in which "Imamum Mubin" is interpreted as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام).

Among them is a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) from his father from his grandfather (عليه السلام) who said: "When this verse وَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ was revealed,

[1]: Regarding the "Preserved Tablet," we have discussed it in Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 241 (under verse 39 of Surah Ar-Ra'd) and also in Volume 5, page 268 (under verse 59 of Al-An'am).

Abu Bakr and Umar stood up and said: 'O Prophet! Does it refer to the Torah?' He said: 'No.' They said: 'Is it the Gospel?' He said: 'No.' They said: 'Does it refer to the Quran?' He said: 'No.' At that moment, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), came toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). When the eyes of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) fell upon him, he said:

'It is this man! He is the Imam in whom Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has enumerated the knowledge of everything!'" [1] In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is also narrated from Ibn Abbas from the Commander of the Faithful himself that he said:

"By Allah, I am the Clear Imam (Imamum Mubin); I distinguish truth from falsehood. I inherited and learned this from the Messenger of Allah." [2]

Although some commentators like Alusi have been alarmed by the citation of such narrations through Shia channels and attributed it to ignorance of the verse's interpretation, with a little reflection, it becomes clear that these types of narrations do not contradict the interpretation of "Imamum Mubin" as the "Preserved Tablet." This is because the pure heart of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the first degree, and the vast heart of his successor in the second degree, are mirrors that reflect the Preserved Tablet, and a great portion of what is in the Preserved Tablet is inspired to them by Allah. In this way, they are an example of the "Preserved Tablet," and therefore, applying "Imamum Mubin" to them is not a strange matter, as it is a branch derived from that root and a part that returns to that whole.

Beyond this, the existence of the Perfect Man—as we know—is a microcosm (alam saghir) that summarizes the macrocosm (alam kabir) within itself, according to the famous poem attributed

to Ali (عليه السلام): "Do you think you are a small body, while the greater world is folded within you?" We also know that the universe, from one perspective, is the page of Allah's knowledge and the Preserved Tablet.

It is surprising that Alusi, while strongly denying the above narrations, did not consider this latter interpretation very far-fetched. In any case, there is no doubt that "Imamum Mubin" refers to the "Preserved Tablet," and the above narrations are also applicable to it (reflect on this).

[1]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar by Saduq, Chapter on the Meaning of Imamum Mubin, page 95.

[2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 4, page 379.

Ayahs

وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ إِذْ جَآءَهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (١٣)
Cite for them the example of the inhabitants of the town when the apostles came to it. (13)
إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱثْنَيْنِ فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَعَزَّزْنَا بِثَالِثٍ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُم مُّرْسَلُونَ (١٤)
When We sent to them two [apostles], they impugned both of them. Then We reinforced them with a third, and they said, ‘We have indeed been sent to you.’ (14)
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ (١٥)
They said, ‘You are no other than human beings like us, and the All-beneficent has not sent down anything, and you are only lying.’ (15)
قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا يَعْلَمُ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ (١٦)
They said, ‘Our Lord knows that we have indeed been sent to you, (16)
وَمَا عَلَيْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٧)
and our duty is only to communicate in clear terms.’ (17)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنَا بِكُمْ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهُوا۟ لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٨)
They said, ‘Indeed we take you for a bad omen. If you do not desist we will stone you, and surely a painful punishment will visit you from us.’ (18)
قَالُوا۟ طَـٰٓئِرُكُم مَّعَكُمْ ۚ أَئِن ذُكِّرْتُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ (١٩)
They said, ‘Your bad omens attend you. What! If you are admonished.... Indeed, you are an unrestrained lot.’ (19)

Commentary

The Story of the "People of the Town" is a Lesson for Them

Following the discussions regarding the Quran, the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the true believers, and the stubborn deniers, the verses under discussion present an example of the state of past nations in this regard. Within these verses and the several following ones—totaling 18 verses—the Quran recounts the story of several ancient messengers commissioned to guide a polytheistic and idol-worshipping people, whom the Quran refers to as the "People of the Town" (Ashab al-Qaryah). These people rose in opposition, denied them, and were ultimately afflicted by a painful punishment. This serves both as a warning to the polytheists of Mecca and as a source of solace and comfort for the Prophet and the few believers of that time.

In any case, the emphasis on this story in the heart of this Surah—which itself is the "Heart of the Quran"—is due to its complete similarity to the situation of the Muslims at that time.

First, He says: "And present to them an example: the people of the town, when the messengers came to it" (WA-DRIB LAHUM MATHALAN ASHABA AL-QARYATI IDH JAAHA AL-MURSALUN) [1]. The word Qaryah originally refers to a place where people gather, and sometimes it is applied to the people themselves; therefore, it has a broad meaning that includes both cities and villages. Although in common Persian it is only applied to villages, in the Arabic language and the Holy Quran, it is repeatedly applied to important and major cities such as "Egypt," "Mecca," and the like.

Regarding which city this was, the well-known and famous view among commentators is that it was "Antioch" (Antakya), one of the cities of the Levant (Shamat). This city was one of the very famous cities of ancient Rome and is currently geographically part of the territory of Turkey; we will provide further details about it in the "Key Points."

In any case, it is clearly evident from the verses of this Surah that the people of this city were idolaters, and these messengers had come to invite them toward monotheism and to combat polytheism.

Then, after this brief and general statement, the Quran proceeds to explain the event, saying: "When We sent to them two [messengers],"

[1]: Some believe that Ashab al-Qaryah is the first object of idrib and mathalan is its second object which has been placed before the first, while others take it as an appositive (badal) for mathalan; however, the first possibility seems more appropriate.

"but they denied them; so We strengthened them with a third, and they said, 'Indeed, we are messengers to you'" (IDH ARSALNA ILAYHIMU-THNAYNI FA-KADHDHABUHUMA FA-AZZAZNA BI-THALITHIN FA-QALU INNA ILAYKUM MURSALUN) [1]. In this way, three messengers of the Lord (two at the beginning and one in the middle to strengthen them) came to this misguided people.

There is discussion among commentators as to who these messengers were. A group has said their names were "Simon" and "John," and the name of the third was "Paul," while others have mentioned different names for them.

Furthermore, there is debate among commentators as to whether they were prophets and messengers of Allah, or messengers of Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام) (and if Allah says "We sent them," it is because the messengers of Jesus are also His messengers). Although the literal appearance of the above verses favors the first interpretation, it makes no difference to the conclusion the Quran intends to draw.

Now let us see what reaction that misguided people showed toward the invitation of the messengers. The Quran says they brought forward the same excuse that many rebellious disbelievers raised against the Divine prophets: "They said, 'You are but human beings like ourselves, and the Most Merciful has not revealed anything. You are only telling lies!'" (QALU MA ANTUM ILLA BASHARUN MITHLUNA WA MA ANZALA AR-RAHMANU MIN SHAY'IN IN ANTUM ILLA TAKDHIBUN).

They reasoned that if a messenger were to come from Allah, he should be a high-ranking angel, not a human being like them. They considered this a reason to deny the messengers and reject the revelation of the Divine command.

[1]: Some commentators consider the word idh here as an appositive for Ashab al-Qaryah, while others consider it related to a hidden verb, namely udhkur (remember).

Yet, perhaps they themselves knew that throughout history, all prophets were from the progeny of Adam; for instance, Abraham, whom everyone recognized as a messenger, was certainly human. Furthermore, who can understand the needs, problems, and pains of humans other than a human? [1] Regarding why the attribute of "Mercy" (Rahmaniyyah) of Allah is emphasized in the verse, it may be from the perspective that Allah, while quoting their words, specifically emphasizes this attribute so that their answer is hidden within their own statement. For how is it possible that a God whose general mercy has encompassed the entire world would not send prophets for the cultivation of souls and to invite humans toward growth and perfection?

It is also possible that they specifically emphasized the description "Most Merciful" (Rahman) to say that a Merciful God does not make things difficult for His servants by sending prophets and establishing obligations! He leaves them free! This weak and baseless logic was consistent with the level of thinking of this group.

In any case, these prophets did not despair at the stubborn opposition of that misguided people and did not allow weakness or lethargy to overcome them. In response to them, they "said, 'Our Lord knows that we are indeed messengers to you,'" (QALU RABBUNA YA'LAMU INNA ILAYKUM LA-MURSALUN).

"And our duty is only to convey [the message] clearly" (WA MA 'ALAYNA ILLA AL-BALAGH AL-MUBIN).

[1]: Regarding the philosophy of the similarity of prophets to their nations, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 12, page 289 (under verse 94 of Surah al-Isra).

Certainly, they did not rely solely on a claim, nor did they suffice with an oath; rather, from the expression "clear conveyance" (balagh mubin), it is generally understood that they showed proofs and miracles. Otherwise, their conveyance would not be an instance of "clear conveyance," for "clear conveyance" must be such that it delivers the reality to everyone, and this is not possible except with the help of solid proofs and eloquent miracles.

It is also mentioned in some narrations that, like Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام), they healed some incurable diseases by the command of Allah.

But these blind-hearted people did not submit to that clear logic and miracles; instead, they increased their violence. Moving beyond the stage of denial, they stepped into the stage of threats and severe action. "They said, 'Indeed, we consider you an evil omen. Your presence is unlucky and a cause of misfortune for our city and land!'" (QALU INNA TATAYYARNA BIKUM) [1]. It is possible that coinciding with the arrival of these Divine prophets, some problems arose in the lives of the people of that land due to their sins or as a Divine warning. As some commentators have narrated, the rainfall ceased for a period [2]. But they not only failed to take a lesson but linked this event to the invitation of the messengers. Again, they did not stop there, but manifested their sinister and ugly intentions with an explicit and clear threat, saying: "If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and there will surely touch you from us a painful punishment!"

[1]: Regarding tatayyur and taking evil omens and the original root of this word, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 6, page 317 under verse 131 of al-A'raf, and Volume 15, page 491 under verse 47 of an-Naml.

[2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verses in question.

"(LA'IN LAM TANTAHU LA-NARJUMANNAKUM WA LA-YAMASSANNAKUM MINNA 'ADHABUN ALIM)."

Is the "painful punishment" ('adhabun alim) an emphasis on the issue of stoning (rajm), or is it a punishment in addition to it? Two possibilities exist.

The second possibility seems closer, because stoning—which is one of the worst types of punishment and torture that sometimes leads to death—might be mentioned alongside "painful punishment" to indicate that "we will continue stoning you until it causes your death," or that "in addition to stoning, we will perform other types of torture that previous criminals practiced, such as thrusting hot irons into the eyes or pouring molten metal down the throat and the like upon you."

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that stoning was a physical punishment, while "painful punishment" was a spiritual and psychological punishment [1]. However, the first interpretation seems closer.

Yes, the supporters of falsehood and the defenders of oppression and corruption, because they have no presentable logic, always rely on threats, pressure, and violence, unaware that the wayfarers of the path of "Allah" will never submit to these threats; rather, it will increase their steadfastness. On the day they stepped into this field, they took their lives in their hands and were ready for sacrifice.

It was here that the Divine messengers, with their eloquent logic, began to answer their ravings and "said, 'Your evil omen is with yourselves. Is it because you are reminded?'" (QALU TA'IRUKUM MA'AKUM A-IN DHUKKIRTUM).

If misfortune, misery, and sinister events have encompassed your social environment, and Divine blessings have departed from among you, seek the cause within your own souls, in your degraded thoughts and your ugly and sinister deeds, not in our invitation. It is you who, through idolatry, following whims, injustice, and sensuality, have made the atmosphere of your life dark and gloomy, and have cut off Allah's blessings from yourselves.

A group of commentators has considered the phrase "Is it because you are reminded?" as referring to an independent point, saying its meaning is: "If Divine prophets come and remind and warn you, is the reward for that to threaten them with punishment and torture and consider their existence an evil omen? They have brought you light, guidance, goodness, and blessing; is the response to such service those threats and ugly words?" [1] Finally, the last word of these messengers of the Lord to them was: "Rather, you are a transgressing people" (BAL ANTUM QAWMUN MUSRIFUN).

Your main pain is that very extravagance and transgression of yours. If you deny monotheism and turn to polytheism, the reason is extravagance and transgressing the truth. And if your society is afflicted with a sinister fate, the cause is also extravagance in sin and being tainted with lusts. Ultimately, if you threaten those who wish you well with death in the face of their goodwill, this too is because of your transgression! We will speak in detail about the historical event of these messengers and the location of these incidents after the commentary on the remaining verses of this story.

[1]: This is in the case that la-narjumannakum is from the root rajm meaning insult, abuse, and slander.

[1]: In any case, the result of the "conditional clause" is omitted, and it is estimated as: "Is it because you are reminded that you confront us with these matters?" or "Is it because you are reminded that you have known the truth of what we said?"

Ayahs

وَجَآءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢٠)
There came a man hurrying from the city outskirts. He said, ‘O my people! Follow the apostles! (20)
ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَن لَّا يَسْـَٔلُكُمْ أَجْرًا وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٢١)
Follow them who do not ask you any reward and they are rightly guided. (21)
وَمَا لِىَ لَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٢)
Why should I not worship Him who has originated me, and to whom you shall be brought back? (22)
ءَأَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً إِن يُرِدْنِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ بِضُرٍّ لَّا تُغْنِ عَنِّى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُنقِذُونِ (٢٣)
Shall I take gods besides Him? If the All-beneficent desired to cause me any distress, their intercession will not avail me in any way, nor will they rescue me. (23)
إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٤)
Indeed, then I would be in manifest error. (24)
إِنِّىٓ ءَامَنتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَٱسْمَعُونِ (٢٥)
Indeed I have faith in your Lord, so listen to me.’ (25)
قِيلَ ٱدْخُلِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ قَوْمِى يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
He was told, ‘Enter paradise!’ He said, ‘Alas! Had my people only known (26)
بِمَا غَفَرَ لِى رَبِّى وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ (٢٧)
for what my Lord forgave me and made me one of the honoured ones!’ (27)
۞ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِن جُندٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا كُنَّا مُنزِلِينَ (٢٨)
After him We did not send down on his people a host from the sky, nor We would have sent down. (28)
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ خَـٰمِدُونَ (٢٩)
It was but a single Cry, and, behold, they were stilled [like burnt ashes]! (29)
يَـٰحَسْرَةً عَلَى ٱلْعِبَادِ ۚ مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٠)
How regrettable of the servants! There did not come to them any apostle but that they used to deride him. (30)

Commentary

A Devoted Mujahid!

In the verses under discussion, another part of the struggles of the messengers mentioned in this story is recounted. This pertains to the calculated and courageous support provided by a few believers who stood against a disbelieving, polytheistic, and stubborn majority, defending the Divine Prophets to the extent of giving their lives.

First, He says: "And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running. He said, 'O my people, follow the messengers'" (وَ جاءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا الْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعى‌ قالَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُوا الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

This man, whom most commentators have identified as "Habib al-Najjar," was among those who, in their initial encounters with the messengers of the Lord, recognized the truth of their call and the depth of their teachings. He emerged as a steadfast and determined believer. When news reached him that in the heart of the city, the people had revolted against these Divine Prophets and perhaps intended to martyr them, he did not consider silence permissible. As implied by the word "yas'a" (running/striving), he rushed to the center of the city and spared no effort in defending the truth.

The use of the indefinite term "rajul" (a man) perhaps points to the fact that he was an ordinary individual, possessing no worldly power or grandeur, and was alone on his path. Nevertheless, the light and heat of faith had so enlightened and warmed him that, indifferent to the consequences of this fierce defense of the warriors of monotheism, he entered the fray. This serves as an example for the believers of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) era at the dawn of Islam—who were but a small number—to know that even a single lone believer bears responsibility, and silence is not permissible for him.

The expression "the farthest end of the city" (aqsa al-madinah) shows that the call of these messengers had reached even the remote parts of the city and had influenced receptive hearts. Furthermore, the outskirts of cities are often the centers for the oppressed (mustad'afin) who are more prepared to accept the truth; conversely, in the heart of cities live the affluent, whose attraction to the truth is not easily achieved.

The address "O my people" (ya qawm) expresses this man's compassion toward the inhabitants of the city and his fellow citizens. His call to follow the messengers is a sincere invitation in which no personal benefit for himself is involved.

Now let us see what logic and reasoning this mujahid believer resorted to in order to attract the attention of his fellow citizens. First, he approached from this angle: "Follow those who do not ask of you any wage for their call" (اتَّبِعُوا مَنْ لا يَسْئَلُكُمْ أَجْراً).

This is the first sign of their sincerity—that they have no material interest in their call. They ask of you neither wealth, nor status, nor even thanks and appreciation, nor any other reward.

This is the same point that has been emphasized many times in the verses of the Quran regarding the great Prophets as a sign of their sincerity, impartiality, and purity of heart. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara alone, this sentence ("And I do not ask you for it any reward") is repeated five times [1]. Then he adds: Furthermore, "these messengers, as is evident from the content of their call and their words, are guided individuals" (وَ هُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ‌).

[1]: Verses 109, 127, 145, 164, and 180 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara.

This indicates that refusing to submit to someone's call is either because the call is not true and leads to deviation and misguidance, or it is true but those presenting it seek specific benefits under its shadow, which itself causes suspicion toward such a call. But when neither this nor that is the case, what room is there for hesitation and doubt?!

Then he proceeds to another argument and goes to the principle of monotheism (Tawhid), which was the core point of these messengers' call, saying: "And why should I not worship Him who created me?" (وَ ما لِيَ لا أَعْبُدُ الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي‌) [1]. Only He is worthy of worship who is the Creator, the Owner, and the Bestower of blessings—not these idols that can do nothing. Sound primordial nature (fitrah) dictates that one must worship the Creator, not these worthless creatures! The emphasis on "fatara-ni" (created me) may also point to the fact that when I return to my original nature and true essence, I clearly see a loud and eloquent cry rising from within me, inviting me to worship my Creator—an invitation that is in harmony with intellect and reason. How can I ignore this dual invitation of "nature" and "intellect"? Interestingly, he does not say: "Why do you not worship the One who created you?" but says: "Why should I not do so?" That is, in fact, he starts with himself so that it may be more effective.

[1]: The sentence "And why should I not worship..." has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "What [excuse] would I have if I did not worship my Creator?" (Majma' al-Bayan). Some commentators have also taken "ma liya" to mean "lim" (why) (Tafsir al-Tibyan under the verse).

Following that, he warns them to be careful: "And to Him you will all be returned" (وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌).

Meaning, not only is your affair in the life of this world with Him, but in the other world as well, your entire destiny is in the hand of His power. Yes, go toward the One who has taken hold of your destiny in both worlds.

In his third argument, he addresses the state of the idols and completes the proof of servitude to Allah by negating servitude to idols, saying: "Should I take besides Him gods [whose] intercession, if the Most Merciful (al-Rahman) intends me some harm, will not avail me at all, nor can they save me from His punishment?" (أَ أَتَّخِذُ مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً إِنْ يُرِدْنِ الرَّحْمنُ بِضُرٍّ لا تُغْنِ عَنِّي شَفاعَتُهُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لا يُنْقِذُونِ‌). Again, here he speaks about himself so as not to sound authoritarian or commanding, allowing others to reflect on their own situation.

He actually puts his finger on the main excuse of the idolaters who used to say: "We worship these so that they may be our intercessors before Allah." He says: What intercession? And what help and salvation? They themselves are in need of your help and support; in the straits of events, what can they do for you?! The use of the name "al-Rahman" (the Most Merciful) here, besides pointing to the vastness of Allah's mercy and the return of all blessings and bounties to Him—which is itself a proof for the monotheism of worship—expresses the point that the Merciful God does not want harm or loss for anyone, unless a person's wrongdoing reaches its ultimate degree, distancing him from the vast Divine environment and trapping him in the valley of His wrath.

Then this mujahid believer added for further emphasis and clarification: "If I were to worship such idols and associate them with the Lord, I would then be in manifest error" (إِنِّي إِذاً لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

What error is more manifest than for a rational and conscious human being to kneel before these unconscious beings and place them alongside the Creator of the heavens and the earth?

After these effective and captivating arguments and preaching, this striving and struggling believer declared with a loud voice in the presence of the assembly: "Everyone know, indeed I have believed in your Lord, and I have accepted the call of these messengers" (إِنِّي آمَنْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ‌). "Therefore, listen to me" (فَاسْمَعُونِ‌) and know that I am a believer in the call of these messengers, and act upon my words, for it is to your benefit.

Regarding who is addressed in this sentence, as well as the sentence "Indeed I have believed in your Lord": The appearance of the preceding verses indicates that they are the same group of polytheists and idolaters who were in that land. The expression "your Lord" (rabbikum) does not contradict this meaning, as this expression appears in many verses of the Holy Quran addressed to disbelievers when stating monotheistic arguments [1]. Likewise, the phrase "fasma'un" (so listen to me) does not conflict with what was said, as he mentioned this to invite them to follow his words, just as it appears in the story of the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh (where he says to the people of Pharaoh: "O my people, follow me; I will guide you to the way of right conduct" (Ghafir: 38)).

[1]: Refer to verses: Yunus 3, Yunus 32, Hud 3, Hud 52, An-Nahl 24, Al-Kahf 29, and others.

From here it becomes clear that what some commentators have said—that the addressees in this sentence are the messengers themselves who had come from Allah to invite this people, taking the expression "your Lord" and the phrase "so listen to me" as evidence for it—has no proof to support it.

But let us see what the reaction of this stubborn people was toward this selfless believer. The Quran does not mention it explicitly, but from the tone of the subsequent verses, it is understood that they rose against him and martyred him. Yes, his passionate and exciting words, accompanied by strong and powerful arguments and interesting and heart-warming points, not only failed to leave a positive effect on those black hearts and heads full of deceit and pride, but rather kindled such a fire of malice and enmity in their hearts that they rose up and fell upon this brave believing man with the utmost cruelty and ruthlessness. According to one narration, they stoned him and made his body a target for stones until he fell to the ground and surrendered his soul to the Creator, while he constantly had these words on his lips: "O Allah, guide my people, for they do not know" [1]. According to another narration, they trampled him under their feet until his soul flew to the heavens [2]. But the Quran expresses this reality with a beautiful and concise sentence, saying:

"It was said, 'Enter Paradise'" (قِيلَ ادْخُلِ الْجَنَّةَ).

This is an expression used regarding the martyrs in the way of Allah in other verses of the Quran: "And never think of those who have been killed in the cause of Allah as dead. Rather, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision" (Aal-Imran: 169).

Interestingly, this expression shows that the martyrdom of this believing man and his entry into Paradise were one and the same; the distance between the two was so small and short that the Holy Quran, in its subtle expression, mentioned his entry into Paradise instead of mentioning his martyrdom. And how close is the path for the martyrs—the path to Paradise and eternal happiness! It is clear that "Paradise" here refers to the Purgatory (Barzakh) Paradise, because it is understood from both verses and narrations that the eternal Paradise will be the lot of the believers on the Day of Resurrection, just as Hell is for the evildoers.

Therefore, there is another Paradise and Hell in the world of Barzakh, which is a sample of the Paradise and Hell of the Resurrection. As it has been narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), regarding the grave: "The grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of the Fires!" [1] And the possibility suggested by some that this sentence refers to the address that will be made to this courageous and self-sacrificing believer on the Day of Resurrection—being future-oriented rather than present—is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse.

In any case, the pure soul of this man hastened to the heavens, to the proximity of Divine mercy and heavenly bliss, and there his only wish was: "He said, 'I wish my people knew'" (قالَ يا لَيْتَ قَوْمِي يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

I wish they knew that "my Lord has forgiven me and placed me among the honored" (بِما غَفَرَ لِي رَبِّي وَ جَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ‌) [1]. I wish they had a truth-seeing eye—an eye not veiled by the thick and heavy veils of the material world—to see what is behind this curtain; that is, to behold all this bounty, honor, and respect from Allah and know what grace Allah has bestowed upon me in exchange for their insults. I wish they could see and believe, but alas! It is mentioned in a hadith that the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "He was sincere to them in his life and after his death." "This man of faith was a well-wisher for his people both during his life and after his death, wishing for their guidance" [2]. It is noteworthy that he first emphasizes the gift of Divine forgiveness, then honor, because first the soul and spirit of a human must be cleansed from the pollution of sins with the water of forgiveness, and once purified, take a place on the carpet of Divine proximity and honor.

This point is also worthy of attention: although Divine honor, respect, and glorification are granted to many servants—and fundamentally "piety" (taqwa) and "honor" (ikram) go hand in hand: "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you" (Al-Hujurat: 13)—"honor" (ikram) is mentioned in the Holy Quran completely and without any condition regarding two groups: First, the "near-stationed angels of Allah," about whom the Quran says: "Rather, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command" (Al-Anbiya: 26-27).

[1]: Regarding whether "ma" in the phrase "bi-ma ghafara li rabbi" is infinitive (masdariyah), relative (mawsulah), or interrogative (istifhamiyah), three possibilities have been given. However, the possibility of it being interrogative seems remote, and between the other two, the relative (mawsulah) is closer, although there is not much difference in meaning. [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5464.

And the other group is the servants perfect in faith whom the Quran refers to as the "sincere ones" (mukhlasin), saying about them: "Those will be in gardens, honored" (Al-Ma'arij: 35) [1].

In any case, this was the end of the affair of this man of faith and true mujahid, who did not fall short in fulfilling his mission and supporting the Divine Prophets, and finally drank the nectar of martyrdom and found his way to the proximity of Allah's mercy. But let us see where the fate of that rebellious and oppressive people led.

Although the Quran does not mention the end of the three Prophets sent to this people, a group of commentators have written that the people, in addition to killing that believing man, also murdered their Prophets. Meanwhile, others have stated that the man of faith kept the crowd occupied with himself so that the Prophets could escape the plot hatched against them and move to a safer location. However, the descent of painful Divine punishment upon them, mentioned in the following verses, is a clue favoring the first view, although the expression "after him" (after the martyrdom of that man of faith) regarding the descent of punishment suggests the second view is more accurate (reflect on this).

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 82 (under the verses discussed).

Beginning of Juz 23 of the Holy Quran

We saw how the people of the city of Antioch rose in opposition to the Divine Prophets; now let us see what became of their end.

The Quran says in this regard: "And We did not send down upon his people after him any army from the heaven, nor would We [ever] send [one]. It is not Our sunnah (way) to resort to such things to destroy these rebellious nations" (وَ ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى‌ قَوْمِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ مِنْ جُنْدٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ ما كُنَّا مُنْزِلِينَ‌).

We have no need for these things; a single sign is enough to extinguish them all, send them to the land of non-existence, and crush their entire lives. A single sign is enough for the factors of their life to turn into the factor of their death, and in a short and fleeting moment, fold up the scroll of their existence!

Then He adds: "It was not but one shout (sayhah), a shocking and deadly cry; then suddenly they were extinguished!" (إِنْ كانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً فَإِذا هُمْ خامِدُونَ‌). Was this shout the sound of a thunderbolt that rose from a cloud and struck the earth, casting a tremor over everything and destroying all buildings, so that they surrendered to death out of intense terror? Or was it a shout that rose from the heart of the earth due to a severe earthquake and echoed in the atmosphere, its blast wave dragging everyone into the jaws of death?!

Whatever it was, it was one shout, and that too in a fleeting moment, nothing more. It was a cry that silenced all cries, and a tremor that made everyone motionless. Such is the power of Allah, and such is the fate of a misguided and fruitless people! Let the wood of fruitless trees burn, This is the very desert for the one who rejects the Educator!

In the last verse under discussion, with a very captivating and effective tone, the reaction of all the rebels of history toward the call of Allah's Prophets is discussed collectively: "How great is the regret for the servants! No messenger came to them but they used to ridicule him" (يا حَسْرَةً عَلَى الْعِبادِ ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ‌). Woe to them who closed the windows of Allah's mercy upon themselves! Alas for them who broke the lamps of their own guidance! Wretched and deprived of happiness is that group who not only do not lend an ear of wisdom to the call of the leaders but rise to mock and ridicule them, then put them to the sword, while they had seen the ill fate of the faithless rebels before them and heard of their painful end with their ears or read it in the pages of history, yet they took not the slightest lesson, and stepped into the very same valley and met the same fate! It is clear that this sentence is the word of Allah, as all these verses are expressed by Him. However, the word "hasrah" (regret) in the sense of internal distress toward events about which a person can do nothing does not apply to Allah, just as "anger," "wrath," and the like do not exist in their literal sense regarding Him. Rather, the meaning is that the state of these wretched ones was such that any human aware of their situation would become sorrowful and affected as to why, with all these means of salvation, they should drown in this terrifying whirlpool?! [1] The use of "ibad" (servants of Allah) points to the fact that the wonder is that the servants of Allah, who are immersed in His blessings, committed such crimes.

Key Points

The Story of the Messengers of "Antioch"

"Antioch" is one of the oldest cities of the Levant (Sham), which some say was built three hundred years before Christ (عليه السلام). In ancient times, this city was considered one of the three great cities of the Roman Empire in terms of wealth, science, and trade. The city of Antioch is less than a hundred kilometers from Aleppo and about sixty kilometers from Alexandria. This city was conquered during the time of the second Caliph by Abu Ubaidah al-Jarrah and taken from the Romans. Its people, who were Christians, accepted the payment of jizya and remained on their faith [2]. After World War I, this city came under French occupation. When the French wanted to leave the Levant, and since most of the people of Antioch were Christians and co-religionists with the French, and they did not want the Christians to be harmed in the turmoil that would occur in this country after their departure, they gave it to Turkey! For Christians, Antioch is considered the second religious city, like Medina for Muslims, and their first city is Jerusalem, where Christ (عليه السلام) began his call. Later, a group of believers in Christ (عليه السلام) migrated to Antioch, and Paul and Barnabas [1] went to that city and called the people to this faith, and from there the religion of Christ (عليه السلام) spread. For this reason, this city is specifically mentioned in the Holy Quran (in the verses under discussion) [2].

The noble commentator Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: Christ (عليه السلام) sent two messengers from the disciples (Hawariyyun) to the city of Antioch. When they approached the city, they saw an old man grazing some sheep; this was Habib, the "Man of Ya-Sin." They greeted him; the old man replied and asked, "Who are you?" They said, "We are the messengers of Jesus; we have come to invite you from the worship of idols to the worship of the Merciful God." The old man asked, "Do you have a miracle or a sign?" They said, "Yes, we heal the sick, and we cure the blind from birth and the leper by the permission of Allah." The old man said, "I have a sick son who has been bedridden for years." They said, "Come with us to your house so we may see his condition." The old man went with them, and they stroked his son's body; by Allah's command, he rose up healthy! This news spread in the city, and subsequently, Allah healed a large group of sick people at their hands.

[1]: Paul is a famous Christian missionary who made great efforts to spread Christianity after Jesus. Barnabas (with a fatha on the 'b') was originally named Joseph and was a companion of Paul and Mark; he has a famous Gospel in which many glad tidings of the appearance of the Prophet of Islam are seen, but Christians consider it apocryphal and say it was written by a Muslim. [2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, footnotes by the late great scholar Sha'rani.

They had a pagan king; news reached him, and he summoned them and asked, "Who are you?" They said, "We are the messengers of Jesus; we have come to invite you from the worship of beings that neither hear nor see, to the worship of One who is both All-Hearing and All-Seeing." The king said, "Is there any deity other than our gods?" They said, "Yes, the One who created you and your gods!" The king said, "Stand up! Until I reflect upon you" (and this was a threat toward them). Then the people seized the two in the market and beat them.

However, in another narration, it is stated that the two messengers of Jesus (عليه السلام) did not reach the king and stayed in that city for a while. One day the king came out of his palace; they raised their voices with Takbir and mentioned the name of "Allah" with greatness. The king became angry and ordered their imprisonment, and each was given a hundred lashes. When these two messengers of Christ were denied and beaten, Christ (عليه السلام) sent Simon Peter (Sham'un al-Safa), who was the chief of the disciples, after them. Simon entered the city incognito and struck up a friendship with the king's courtiers. They enjoyed his friendship and reported the news to the king. He also invited him, made him one of his companions, and respected him. One day Simon said, "O King! I have heard that two people have been imprisoned in your jail, and when they called you to other than your religion, you beat them? Have you ever listened to their words?!" The king said, "My anger prevented this." Simon said, "If the king deems it appropriate, let him summon them so we may see what they have in their bag."

The king summoned them. Simon (as if he did not know them at all) said to them, "Who sent you here?!" They said, "The God who created everything and has no partner." He said, "What is your sign and miracle?" They said, "Whatever you wish!" The king ordered a blind slave to be brought, and they healed him by Allah's command. The king was astonished. Here, Simon spoke up and said to the king, "If you had made such a request from your gods, would they also have been capable of such a thing?" The king said, "To be honest with you, the gods we worship have neither harm, nor benefit, nor any effect!" Then the king said to the two, "If your God can revive a dead person, we will believe in Him and in you." They said, "Our God is capable of all things." The king said, "There is a dead person here who has been dead for seven days; we have not buried him yet, waiting for his father to return from a journey." The dead person was brought; the two were praying openly and Simon secretly.
