Suddenly the dead person moved and rose up, saying, "I have been dead for seven days and have seen the fire of Hell with my own eyes, and I warn you all to believe in the One God." The king was amazed. When Simon became certain that his words had influenced him, he invited him to the One God and he believed, and the people of his country joined him, although a group remained in their disbelief."

A similar narration is reported in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (عليهما السلام), although there are differences among them [1]. However, considering the apparent meaning of the previous verses, the belief of the people of that city seems very unlikely, as the Quran says they were destroyed by a heavenly shout. It is possible that in this part of the narration, an error was made by the narrator. It is also noteworthy that the expression "mursalun" (messengers) in the above verses shows that they were Prophets and messengers of Allah. Furthermore, the Quran says the people of the city said to them, "You are but humans like us, and Allah has not sent down anything." Such expressions in the Holy Quran usually appear regarding Divine Prophets, unless it is said that the messengers of Prophets are also messengers of Allah, but this justification seems remote.

2- Instructive Points of This Story

From what we read in the above verses regarding this story, many lessons can be learned, including the following:

A- Believing individuals never fear loneliness in the way of Allah, just as a single believer like "Habib al-Najjar" did not fear the multitude of polytheists in the city. Ali (عليه السلام) says: "O people, do not be lonely on the path of guidance due to the small number of its followers" [2].

B- A believer is a lover of people's guidance and suffers from their misguidance, even after his martyrdom, wishing that others could see his status and believe!

C- The content of the Prophets' call is itself the best witness to their guidance and truthfulness ("and they are guided").

D- The call toward Allah must be free from any expectation of reward to be effective.

E- Sometimes misguidance has no hidden or secret factor but is "manifest error" (dalal mubin), and idol worship and polytheism are clear examples of "manifest error."

F- Men of truth rely on realities, while the misguided rely on illusions and fancies.

G- If there is any ill omen or misfortune, its source is the human himself and his actions.

H- "Extravagance" (israf) is the factor behind many misfortunes and deviations.

I- The duty of Prophets and those who follow their path is "clear delivery" (balagh mubin) and open invitation in all fields, whether people accept or not.

J- Congregation and numbers are among the important factors of victory, honor, and strength ("and We strengthened them with a third").

K- Allah does not mobilize the vast armies of heaven and earth to crush rebellious tyrants; rather, with a single sign, He crushes everything they have.

L- There is no distance between martyrdom and Paradise, and the martyr, before falling from his mount to the ground, is embraced by the Houri [1].

[1]: In this regard, a detailed narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam is reported, which we brought in Vol. 3, p. 172 (under verse 169 of Aal-Imran).

M- Allah first cleanses a person from sin and then places him in the proximity of His mercy ("because my Lord has forgiven me and placed me among the honored").

N- One should not fear the opposition and stubbornness of the enemies of truth, as this has been their constant program throughout history ("How great is the regret for the servants! No messenger came to them but they used to ridicule him"). What regret is greater and higher than for a person to close the doors of guidance upon himself due to prejudice, stubbornness, and pride, and not see the world-illuminating sun of truth.

O- Those who believed in the Prophets were primarily the oppressed of society ("And there came a man from the farthest end of the city").

P- It was they who never grew tired in the way of seeking, and their effort and striving were not limited by any boundary ("yas'a" - running/striving).

Q- The method of preaching should be learned from the Divine messengers, who used all effective methods and tactics to penetrate the hearts of the unaware, an example of which is seen in the above verse and the narrations mentioned in its commentary.

3- Reward and Punishment of Barzakh

In the above verses, it was mentioned that the said "believer" was placed in Divine Paradise after martyrdom and wished that those left behind would know of his fate. Certainly, these verses, like the verses regarding martyrs, do not refer to the eternal Paradise of the Resurrection, which according to the verses, entry into will occur after the resurrection of the dead and the accounting of the Gathering. From here it becomes clear that we also have a Paradise and Hell in Barzakh (Purgatory), where martyrs are blessed, and tyrants like the "Family of Pharaoh" are placed before its fire morning and evening. With this point in mind, many issues mentioned regarding Paradise and Hell, such as what appears in the narrations of the Mi'raj (Ascension) and the like, are resolved.

4- The Foremost of the Nations!

In the Tafsir of Tha'labi, it is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "The foremost of the nations are three who did not disbelieve in Allah for even the blink of an eye: Ali bin Abi Talib (عليه السلام), the Man of Ya-Sin, and the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh. They are the truthful ones (Siddiqun), and Ali is the best of them" [1]. The same meaning is narrated in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur with another wording from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), who said: "The truthful ones (Siddiqun) are three: Habib al-Najjar, the believer of the Family of Ya-Sin who said, 'O my people, follow the messengers'; Ezekiel (Hizqil), the believer of the Family of Pharaoh who said (while defending Moses against the murder plot arranged by the Pharaohs), 'Do you kill a man because he says, "My Lord is Allah"?'; and Ali bin Abi Talib (عليه السلام), and he is the best of them" [2].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, Al-Mizan, and Nur al-Thaqalayn. [2]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 86.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٣١)
Have they not regarded how many generations We have destroyed before them who will not come back to them? (31)
وَإِن كُلٌّ لَّمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (٣٢)
And all of them will indeed be presented before Us. (32)

Commentary

Perpetual Negligence

In these two verses, following the discussion in the final preceding verses regarding the continuous negligence of a vast group of people throughout past ages and centuries, He says: "Did they not see how many generations and nations before them We destroyed because of their rebellion and oppression?!" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ) [^1] These are not the first group to walk upon the earth; before them, other rebellious nations lived in this world, and their painful fate—recorded on the pages of history—and their sorrowful remains—left in the ruins of their once-prosperous cities—stand before their eyes. Is this not sufficient as a lesson for reflection?

Regarding whom the plural pronoun in "أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا" (Did they not see) refers to, commentators have offered several possibilities: First, that it refers back to the "People of the Town" (Ashab al-Qaryah) who were discussed in the previous verses. Second, that it refers to the "People of Makkah," for whom these verses were revealed as a warning.

However, considering the previous verse (يا حَسْرَةً عَلَى الْعِبادِ ...), it indicates that the address is to all of humanity. This is because "the servants" (al-ibad) in the aforementioned verse encompasses all human beings throughout history who rose to deny and mock the Divine messengers. In any case, it is an invitation to all people of the world to study the history of their predecessors meticulously, to look upon their remaining traces, and to send forth a discerning heart through their eyes to observe well, viewing the ruined palace porticos of the tyrants as a mirror of warning.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "They shall never return to them" (أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ) [^2]. That is, the great tragedy lies here: they have no possibility of returning to this world to compensate for their past sins and misfortunes. The bridges behind them have been so thoroughly destroyed that their return is never possible! This interpretation is exactly like the words spoken by Ali عليه السلام regarding taking lessons from the dead in one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha:

“They can neither move away from their ugly deeds, nor are they able to increase their good deeds.” (Because the path of return is closed and compensation is impossible—Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 188).

In the next verse, He adds: "And all of them, without exception, shall be brought before Us on the Day of Resurrection" (وَ إِنْ كُلٌّ لَمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنا مُحْضَرُونَ) [^3]. This means it is not the case that if they are destroyed and cannot return to this world, the matter is finished. No, death is in fact the beginning of the matter, not the end. Soon, all will be gathered in the arena of the Mahshar (Gathering) for accounting, and after that, a painful Divine punishment—a continuous and relentless punishment—awaits them.

Under such circumstances, is it not appropriate for them to take a lesson from their state, not to afflict themselves with the same fate, and to step away from this terrifying whirlpool while opportunity remains?

Indeed, if death were the end of everything, it might be possible to say it is the beginning of our rest. But alas, it is not so, and as the poet says:

If it were that when we died, we were left alone, Death would be a relief for every living being. But when we die, we are resurrected, And after that, we are questioned about everything!

[^1]: The interrogation in the above verse is a rhetorical question (istifham taqriri), and "kam" is informative (khabariyyah), used here to denote "many" and serving as the object of "yaraw." "Min al-qurun" clarifies it. "Qurun," as we have previously stated, is the plural of "qarn," which refers both to a long period of time and to the people living in a single era.

[^2]: This phrase is a substitute (badal) for "kam ahlakna," and the implied meaning is: "Did they not see that they shall not return to them?" Some have also suggested it is a circumstantial clause (hal) for those who were destroyed.

[^3]: The well-known view among grammarians regarding the structure of this verse is that "in" is negating (some say it is lightened/mukhaffafah, which is why it does not govern the following noun in the accusative), and "lamma" means "illa" (except), as the use of "lamma" to mean "illa" is explicitly mentioned in the works of Arabic linguists. Therefore, the disagreement of al-Kisa'i poses no problem. "Jami'" meaning "all together" is the predicate (khabar) of "kull" (the nunation on "kull" replaces a deleted possessive, originally being "kulluhum"), and "muhdarun" is either a second predicate or an adjective for "jami'." Thus, the meaning of the sentence becomes: "And all of them are nothing but gathered on the Day of Resurrection, brought before Us."

Ayahs

وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْنَـٰهَا وَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهَا حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ (٣٣)
A sign for them is the dead earth, which We revive and bring forth grain out of it, so they eat of it. (33)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ وَفَجَّرْنَا فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْعُيُونِ (٣٤)
We make in it orchards of date palms and vines, and We cause springs to gush forth in it, (34)
لِيَأْكُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦ وَمَا عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٥)
so that they may eat of its fruit and what their hands have cultivated. Will they not then give thanks? (35)
سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٦)
Immaculate is He who has created all the kinds of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know. (36)

Commentary

These are Other Signs

Since the discussion in the preceding verses revolved around the struggle of the Lord's messengers against polytheism and idolatry, and the final verse alluded to the issue of the Resurrection, the verses under discussion present signs of both Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection simultaneously, serving as a means to awaken the deniers and foster faith in the Origin and the Return.

First, discussing the revival of dead lands and the blessings that reach humanity from them, He says: "A sign for them is the dead earth. We gave it life and brought forth from it grain, so they eat thereof" (وَ آيَةٌ لَهُمُ الْأَرْضُ الْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْناها وَ أَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ) [1]. The phenomenon of life is among the most significant proofs of Monotheism; it is an extraordinarily mysterious, complex, and wondrous matter that has left the intellects of all scientists in awe. Despite the vast advancements in science and knowledge achieved by mankind, no one has yet solved its enigma. No one yet knows exactly under what factors inanimate matter was transformed into living cells on the first day.

No one yet knows precisely how the seeds of plants and their various layers are constructed, or what mysterious laws govern them such that when favorable conditions arise, they stir into motion, begin to grow and develop, and absorb the particles of the dead earth into their own being, thereby transforming dead matter into the tissues of a living organism, displaying a new manifestation of life every day.

The phenomenon of life in the world of plants and animals and the revival of dead lands is, on one hand, a clear proof that immense knowledge and wisdom were employed in the creation of this world, and on the other hand, it is a manifest sign of the Resurrection.

It is clear that the pronoun in "for them" (لهم) refers to the "servants" (عباد) mentioned in the previous verses. By "servants" here, it refers to all those who are entangled in deviation or error regarding matters of the Origin and the Return, whose state the Quran considers a cause for sighing and regret.

The use of the word "sign" (آية) in its indefinite form (nakirah) points to the greatness, importance, and clarity of this monotheistic evidence.

The phrase "so they eat thereof" (فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ) indicates, on one hand, that humans consume a portion of plant grains, while others are not edible for humans but serve other purposes, such as animal feed, or the production of dyes, medicines, and other matters fully utilized in human life.

On the other hand, by placing "thereof" (منه) before "they eat" (ياكلون)—which usually denotes restriction (hasr)—it highlights the point that the majority (and also the best) of human nutrition comes from plant materials, as if they constitute the entirety of human food!

The next verse is an elaboration and explanation of the previous one, describing how the dead lands are brought to life. It says: "And We placed therein gardens of palm trees and grapevines and caused springs to gush forth therein" (وَ جَعَلْنا فِيها جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَ أَعْنابٍ وَ فَجَّرْنا فِيها مِنَ الْعُيُونِ).

In the previous verse, the discussion was about food grains, but here it speaks of strengthening and nutritious fruits, of which two prominent and perfect examples are "dates" and "grapes," each considered a complete food.

As we have previously explained in detail, scientific studies show that these two fruits in particular contain various necessary vitamins and different vital substances for the human body. Furthermore, these two fruits can be preserved and used for nutrition throughout the year, whether fresh or dried.

"A'nab" (أعناب) is the plural of "Inab" (عنب), and "Nakhil" (نخيل)—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—is the plural of "Nakhl" (نخل). However, the difference is that "Inab" is usually applied to the "grapes" themselves and rarely to the "vineyard," whereas "Nakhl" is the name for the tree, and its fruit is called "Rutab" and "Tamr" (fresh and dried dates) [1]. Some believe this variation in expression—speaking of the tree in one instance and the fruit in the other—is because every part of the date palm is useful: its trunk, branches, and leaves are all used for various purposes, with its fruit being the pinnacle of them all. In contrast, the grapevine is usually desired for its fruit, and its stem and branches do not have as much utility.

The fact that both are mentioned in the plural form may refer to the various types of these two fruits, as each has dozens of varieties with different characteristics, catering to all tastes and palates! It is also noteworthy that the previous verse only mentioned the revival of dead lands, which in the Holy Quran is usually associated with the falling of rain, but this verse mentions flowing springs of water, because for many crops, rainwater alone is sufficient, whereas fruit trees usually also require flowing water.

"Fajjarna" (فجرنا) is derived from the root "tafjir," meaning "to create a wide breach." Since springs gush out by the cleaving of the earth, this expression is used for the emergence of springs from the ground [1].

The following verse states the purpose of creating these fruitful trees: "That they may eat of His fruit, while their hands did not make it. Will they not then be grateful?" (لِيَأْكُلُوا مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ وَ ما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَ فَلا يَشْكُرُونَ). Indeed, the fruits that appear as complete food on the branches of trees, without the slightest need for cooking or other changes, are ready for use as soon as they are picked from the tree. This demonstrates the ultimate grace and greatness of the Lord toward humanity.

He has even packaged this ready and delicious food in such a way that it can be preserved for a long time without losing its nutritional value, unlike the foods that humans make with their own hands from God-given natural materials, which often spoil quickly! There is another interpretation regarding the meaning of the verse that is also noteworthy:

The Quran intends to refer both to the fruits that are used without change and to the various types of food obtained by performing some action upon these fruits (in the first interpretation, "ma" in the phrase "ma amilat-hu aydihim" is negative, and in the second case, it is a relative pronoun).

In any case, the goal is to stimulate the sense of appreciation and gratitude in humans so that through gratitude, they may step into the stage of knowing the Lord, for thanking the Benefactor is the first step in knowing the Creator.

The last verse under discussion speaks of the glorification and sanctification of the Lord, drawing a line of nullification over the polytheism of the pagans mentioned in the previous verses, and showing the path of Monotheism and the worship of the One to everyone. It says: "Glory be to Him who created all pairs, of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know!" (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ وَ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ مِمَّا لا يَعْلَمُونَ) [1]. Indeed, the God who created all these "pairs" across the expanse of existence possesses infinite knowledge and power; no defect or deficiency finds its way into His being. Therefore, there is no partner, likeness, or peer for Him. If a group has considered lifeless stones, wood, or other creatures as His equals, no dust from these improper attributions settles upon the hem of His Majesty! It is obvious that Allah does not need to glorify and sanctify Himself; this is a teaching for the servants and a set of instructions for traversing the path of perfection.

Regarding what is meant by "pairs" (azwaj) here, commentators have much to say:

What is certain is that "azwaj," the plural of "zawj," is usually applied to the two sexes, male and female, whether in the animal kingdom or otherwise. Subsequently, it was expanded, and "zawj" is applied to any two entities that are counterparts or even opposites. The word is even used for two similar rooms in a house, two leaves of a door, or two colleagues and companions. In this way, a pair can be envisioned for every entity in the universe.

In any case, it is not unlikely that "pairing" (zawjiyyat) here carries that specific meaning, namely "male" and "female" genders, and the Holy Quran in this verse informs of the existence of pairing throughout the world of plants, humans, and other entities of which people have no knowledge.

These entities might be plants, the extent of whose pairing had not yet been discovered in those days.

Or it could refer to animals in the depths of the seas that no one was aware of then, and today a portion of that has been discovered by humans.

Or it could refer to other beings living on other celestial bodies.

Or microscopic living organisms; although scientists today may not recognize male and female among them, the world of these living beings is so mysterious and covered in enigmas that human science may not yet have found its way to this part of it. Even the existence of pairing in the plant world, as we said, was unknown at the time of the Quran's revelation except in specific cases like date palms and the like, and the Quran unveiled it. In recent centuries, it has been scientifically proven that the issue of pairing in the plant kingdom is a universal and general phenomenon.

The possibility has also been raised that "pairing" here refers to the existence of positive and negative particles within all atoms, as we know that all things in this world are composed of "atoms," and the atom is effectively like a brick for the grand construction of this great palace of the material world.

Until the day the atom was split, there was no news of the existence of this pairing, but afterward, the existence of negative and positive pairs in the atomic nucleus and the electrons orbiting it was proven.

Some have also considered it an allusion to the composition of things from "matter" and "form" or "substance" and "accident," while others consider it a metaphor for the "various sorts and types" of plants, humans, animals, and other creatures of the world.

However, it is clear that when we can apply these words to their literal meaning (male and female genders) and there is no evidence to the contrary, there is no reason to seek metaphorical meanings. As we have seen, there are several interesting interpretations for the literal meaning of pairing here.

In any case, this verse is another of the verses that state the limitation of human knowledge and show that in this world, there are many truths hidden from our science and knowledge [1].

[^1]: Regarding the "pairing of the entities of the world" and especially in the plant kingdom, we have discussed this in Volume 10, page 115, and Volume 15, page 190, under verse 7 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara.

Ayahs

وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فَإِذَا هُم مُّظْلِمُونَ (٣٧)
A sign for them is the night, which We strip of daylight, and, behold, they find themselves in the dark! (37)
وَٱلشَّمْسُ تَجْرِى لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَّهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٣٨)
The sun runs on to its place of rest: That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (38)
وَٱلْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْنَـٰهُ مَنَازِلَ حَتَّىٰ عَادَ كَٱلْعُرْجُونِ ٱلْقَدِيمِ (٣٩)
As for the moon, We have ordained its phases, until it becomes like an old palm leaf. (39)
لَا ٱلشَّمْسُ يَنۢبَغِى لَهَآ أَن تُدْرِكَ ٱلْقَمَرَ وَلَا ٱلَّيْلُ سَابِقُ ٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ (٤٠)
Neither it behooves the sun to overtake the moon, nor may the night outrun the day, and each swims in an orbit. (40)

Commentary

Each of the Sun and the Moon is a Sign

The verses under discussion describe another part of the signs of Allah’s greatness in the universe. They present another link in the chain of Monotheism (Tawhid) following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the Resurrection, the revival of dead lands, and the cultivation of plants and trees.

First, He says: "The night is a sign for them" of the greatness of Allah (وَ آيَةٌ لَهُمُ اللَّيْلُ‌).

While the sunlight has encompassed everywhere and the army of darkness has been pushed back, "We strip the day from it, and suddenly they are in darkness" (نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ النَّهارَ فَإِذا هُمْ مُظْلِمُونَ‌).

The expression "naslakhu" (نَسْلَخُ) from the root salkh (rhyming with Balkh), which originally means to skin an animal, is a subtle metaphor. It is as if the light of day is like a white garment worn by the night; at sunset, this garment is stripped from its body like a skin, so that its inner essence and core become manifest.

A careful look at this expression reveals the point that the original nature of the Earth is darkness; light and brightness are accidental attributes given to it from another source, much like a garment put on someone—whenever that garment is removed, the natural color of the body appears! [^1]

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Salkh" means skinning an animal, and it is also applied to removing "armor" from the body and the ending of a month. However, some commentators say this is the case when salkh is transitive with "'an" (عن), and if it is transitive with "min" (من), it means "extracting." Yet, we did not find a clear reason for this distinction in linguistic books, although it is mentioned in Lisan al-Arab: "The day stripped (insalakha) from the night, meaning it exited from it," but it appears this is derived from the first meaning.

Here, the Holy Quran points to the darkness of night, seemingly intending—after explaining the revival of dead lands as a sign of Allah’s signs—to describe the transformation of daylight into the darkness of night as an example of death after life.

In any case, when man is submerged in the darkness of night, he remembers light and its blessings, light and its excitements, light and its source of existence, and through a comparison, he becomes acquainted with the "Creator of light and darkness."

The third sign mentioned after the sign of the night is the sign of light, brightness, and the sun. It says: "And the sun runs its course toward its stopping point" (وَ الشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها) [^1]. This verse clearly states the continuous movement of the sun. However, commentators have many discussions regarding what is meant by this movement:

A group considers it a reference to the apparent movement of the sun around the Earth, which continues until the end of the world, which is in reality the "stopping point" of the sun and the end of its life.

Others have considered it a reference to the inclination of the sun in summer and winter toward the north and south of the Earth. For we know that from the beginning of spring, the sun inclines from the equatorial line toward the north until it reaches the 23rd degree northern latitude, and from the beginning of summer, it moves back until it reaches the equatorial line again at the beginning of autumn, continuing this path toward the south until the beginning of winter, and from the beginning of winter, it moves toward the equatorial line, reaching it at the beginning of spring.

Of course, all these movements actually result from the Earth's motion and the tilt of its axis relative to its orbital plane, although apparently and according to the senses, it pertains to the movement of the sun.

Others have considered it a reference to the rotational movement of the "solar sphere" around its own axis, as scientific studies have definitively proven that the sun rotates around itself. [^1] The latest and most modern interpretation for the above verse is what scientists have recently discovered: the movement of the sun along with the entire solar system within our galaxy toward a specific direction and a distant star named "Vega."

These meanings are not contradictory, and it is possible that the phrase "runs" (tajri) refers to all these movements and other movements that our science has not yet reached and may be discovered in the future.

In any case, moving the sun—this massive sphere that is one million and three hundred thousand times larger than the Earth—with such a calculated motion in this boundless space is possible for no one except Allah, whose power is above all powers and whose knowledge is infinite. For this reason, at the end of the verse, He says: "That is the decree of the All-Mighty, the All-Knowing" (ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ‌).

[^1]: In the syntax of this sentence, there are two possibilities: first, that it is conjoined to "the night" (al-layl), meaning "And a sign for them is the sun"; second, that "the sun" (ash-shams) is the subject and "runs" (tajri) is its predicate. We have chosen the first possibility. [^1]: According to this interpretation, the "lam" in "li-mustaqarrin laha" means "in" (fi).

The final word regarding this verse is that its expressions contain a meaningful reference to the solar calendar system resulting from the sun's movement through the zodiac signs—a system that gives order and planning to human life and regulates its various aspects.

Therefore, in the next verse, to complete this discussion, it speaks of the movement of the moon and its phases, which regulate the days of the month, saying: "And for the moon We have ordained phases, until it becomes like an old, curved, yellow date-stalk" (وَ الْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْناهُ مَنازِلَ حَتَّى عادَ كَالْعُرْجُونِ الْقَدِيمِ‌).

The "phases" (manazil) refer to the twenty-eight stations that the moon passes through before the "conjunction" (muhaq) and absolute darkness. For when a month is a full thirty days, the moon is visible in the sky until the twenty-eighth night, but on this twenty-eighth night, it appears as a very thin, yellow, dim, and faint crescent, and it is not visible during the remaining two nights, which is called muhaq. Of course, in months that are twenty-nine days, the moon is usually seen until the twenty-seventh night, and the remaining two nights are muhaq.

These stations are perfectly precise and calculated, such that astronomers can predict them hundreds of years in advance based on accurate calculations! This wondrous system brings order to human life and is a natural celestial calendar that both the literate and illiterate can read. If a person pays a little attention and practices observing the state of the moon on different nights, they can, by looking at its condition, know exactly or approximately which night of the month it is (and we have tested this).

For at the beginning of the month, the tips of the crescent point upward, and gradually the volume of the moon increases until the seventh, when half of the full circle of the moon appears, and it continues to increase until the fourteenth, when it becomes a full moon (badr).

From then on, the moon decreases from the bottom side until the twenty-first, when it again becomes a semi-circle; it continues to decrease until the twenty-eighth night, when it becomes a weak, pale crescent whose tips point downward.

Yes, the foundation of human life is formed by order, and order is impossible without the precise determination of time, and Allah has placed this accurate monthly and yearly calendar in the sky for this very purpose.

From here, the meaning of the subtle expression "like an old date-stalk" (kal-'urjun al-qadim) [^1] becomes clear.

For "urjun", as most commentators and linguists have said, is that part of the date cluster that connects to the tree. To explain: dates appear as a cluster on the tree; the base of this cluster is a curved, yellow wooden stalk connected to the tree, and its tip is like a broom, with the date fruits connected to its strands like grapes. When the palm cluster is cut, that curved base remains on the tree; when it dries and withers, it looks exactly like the crescent before the conjunction, because just as the crescent of the end of the month—which appears in the eastern side of the sky near dawn—is curved, withered, and yellow with its tips pointing downward, the "old date-stalk" is the same.

In fact, this resemblance appears in various aspects: In terms of being crescent-shaped like the wood of the date cluster, in terms of being yellow, in terms of withering, in terms of the downward inclination of the tips of its arc, and in terms of being situated amidst the green mass of the palm branches, which is not unlike the placement of the last night's crescent in the dark sky.

Describing it as "old" (qadim) refers to its age, for the older these branches become, the thinner, more withered, and yellower they get, bearing a greater resemblance to the crescent of the end of the month. Glory be to Allah! What subtleties and beauties are hidden in such a short expression?

The last verse under discussion speaks of the stability and permanence of this order of the year, month, night, and day. The Lord has regulated such a program for them that not the slightest alteration occurs in their state, and human history is perfectly organized because of this stability.

He says: "It is not for the sun to overtake the moon, nor does the night outstrip the day, and each is swimming in an orbit" (لَا الشَّمْسُ يَنْبَغِي لَها أَنْ تُدْرِكَ الْقَمَرَ وَ لَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ وَ كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌).

We know that the sun completes its cycle through the twelve zodiac signs in one year, while the moon completes its phases in one month.

Therefore, the rotational movement of the moon in its path is twelve times faster than the movement of the sun in its orbit; thus, He says the sun never catches up to the moon in its movement so as to perform its one-year journey in one month and disrupt the annual system.

Likewise, the night does not outstrip the day to swallow a part of it and disrupt the existing order; rather, all of these continue their paths for millions of years without the slightest change.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the "movement of the sun" in this discussion refers to its movement according to our senses. Interestingly, this expression is used even after it was proven that the sun is stationary in its place and the Earth revolves around it once a year. For example, today people say "the sun's entry into Aries" (reaching the beginning of Farvardin) or "the sun reaching the meridian," or "reaching the total declination" (meaning the sun reaching its highest point of altitude in the Northern Hemisphere at the start of summer or, conversely, the lowest point of depression at the start of winter).

These expressions all show that even after the discovery of the Earth's movement around the sun and its relative stillness, the old expressions regarding the sun's movement are used because, from a sensory perspective, it appears that the sun is moving, and these expressions are derived from this.

In this way, the "swimming" of the sun and the moon in their orbits (كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌) also stems from this.

There is also the possibility that the "swimming" of the sun in its orbit refers to its movement along with the solar system and along with the galaxy we are in, as it is proven today that our solar system is part of a massive galaxy that is itself rotating. [^1]

[^1]: According to a group of linguists, "urjun" is derived from the root "in'iraj" meaning "crookedness" and "bending," and thus its "nun" is extra, following the pattern of "fu'lun." However, according to another group, it is derived from the root "arjana" and its "nun" is original, meaning the base of a branch that bends and remains on the date tree. "Qadim" means anything old that has passed through time. [^1]: This movement is different from the movement of the entire solar system within the galaxy toward the star "Vega," which we recently mentioned.

For "falak", as linguists have said, originally means the swelling of a girl's breasts and taking on a circular shape; then it was applied to circular pieces of land or other circular objects, and for this reason, it is applied to the circular path of the planets.

The phrase "each is swimming in an orbit" (كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌), according to many commentators, refers to each of the sun, moon, and stars, which have their own paths and orbits. Although the names of the stars were not mentioned previously in the verses, given the mention of "night" (layl) and the association of stars with the moon and sun, understanding this meaning from the said phrase is not far-fetched, especially since "swimming" (yasbahun) appears in the plural form.

There is also the interpretation that this phrase refers to each of the sun, moon, night, and day, because night and day each have their own "orbit" and revolve precisely around the Earth; darkness always covers half of the Earth, and light the other half, and these two complete one full rotation around the Earth in twenty-four hours.

The expression "swimming" (yasbahun) from the root siyahah, which according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat originally means "rapid" movement in water or air, [^1] refers to the rapid movement of celestial bodies, and compares them to rational beings that continue their rotation with speed. Today, this fact is also proven: that celestial bodies move in their paths with very strange and sometimes staggering speeds.

[^1]: The fact that the remembrance of Allah and His worship is called "tasbih" also stems from this, as it is a kind of rapid movement on the path of obedience and worship of the Lord (Al-Mufradat by Raghib, root s-b-h).

Key Points

1- The "Rotational" and "Flowing" Movement of the Sun:

In the Arabic language, "dawaran" is used for circle-like movement, while "jarayan" refers to longitudinal movements. Interestingly, in the above verses, both a flowing movement and a rotational one are attributed to the sun; in one place it says: "And the sun runs (tajri)..."

And in another place, it speaks of the sun "swimming" in an orbit (a circle-like path) (كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌).

On the day these verses were revealed, the "Ptolemaic" hypothesis ruled scientific circles with full force. According to this hypothesis, celestial bodies did not have rotations of their own; rather, they were fixed within the "spheres" (aflak), which were crystalline and dense bodies layered like the skins of an onion, and their movement was dependent on the movement of their spheres. Therefore, in those days, neither the "swimming" of the sun had any meaning, nor its longitudinal and flowing movement.

However, after the foundations of the Ptolemaic hypothesis collapsed in the light of the discoveries of recent centuries, and celestial bodies were freed from the constraints of crystalline spheres, the theory gained strength that the sun is stationary and motionless at the center of the solar system, and the entire solar system revolves around it like moths.

Here again, the meaning of the expressions in the above verses, which attributed longitudinal and rotational movement to the sun, was not clear.

Until science continued its progress, and recently several types of movement were proven for the sun:

  1. Its rotational movement around itself.
  2. Its longitudinal movement along with the solar system toward a specific point in the sky.
  3. Its rotational movement along with the galactic cluster of which it is a part.

In this way, another scientific miracle of the Quran was proven.

To clarify this issue further, we bring here a part of the discussion regarding the sun's movement found in one of the encyclopedias:

The sun has "apparent" movements (daily movement and annual movement) and "real" movements.

The sun participates in the daily and apparent movement of the celestial sphere; in our hemisphere, it rises from the east, passes through the local meridian in the south, and sets in the west. Its passage through the meridian marks true noon.

The sun also has an annual (apparent) movement around the Earth, which moves it nearly one degree from west to east every "day." In this movement, the sun passes in front of the zodiac signs once a year. The orbit of this movement lies in the plane of the "ecliptic." This movement has had great importance in the history of astronomy; the "equinoxes," "solstices," and "total declination" are related to it, and the solar year is based on it.

In addition to these apparent movements, the rotational movement of the galaxy carries the sun through space at a speed of about one million and one hundred and thirty thousand kilometers per hour! However, even within the galaxy, the sun is not stationary; rather, it moves at a speed of nearly seventy-two thousand four hundred kilometers per hour toward the constellation "Hercules" (Jathi 'ala Rukbatayh). [^1]

The reason we are unaware of this rapid movement of the sun in space is the distance of the celestial bodies that serve as the reference for identifying this specific rotational movement.

The period of the sun's rotational movement at its equator is about twenty-five days. [^1] [^2]

[^1]: "Hercules" (Jathi 'ala Rukbatayh) is a group of stars forming a constellation that resembles someone kneeling on their knees, ready to rise; this expression is taken from this meaning. [^1]: Meaning the sun rotates once around itself in twenty-five of our days. Scientists obtained this from studying sunspots, as they saw these spots shift and return exactly to their place after twenty-five days. [^2]: Quoted from Dehkhoda Encyclopedia, entry "Sun" (Vol. 22).

2- The Expressions "Overtake" and "Outstrip":

The expressions of the Quran are so calculated that their subtleties and precisions are innumerable. In the above verses, when speaking of the apparent movement of the moon and sun in their monthly and annual paths, it says: "It is not for the sun to overtake the moon" (because the moon completes its path in one month and the sun in one year; this difference in speed is such that it expresses that the sun never catches up to it (لَا الشَّمْسُ يَنْبَغِي لَها أَنْ تُدْرِكَ الْقَمَرَ).

But regarding night and day, because they do not have much distance from each other and are placed exactly one after another, it says "the night does not outstrip the day." We see that these two expressions here are very calculated.

3- The System of Light and Darkness in Human Life:

In the above verses, two subjects that are among the most important issues of human life are mentioned as two signs of the Divine signs: the subject of the darkness of night, and the subject of the sun and sunlight.

We have said before that light is the most subtle and blessed of all entities in the material world. Not only our illumination and life, but every movement and activity depends on sunlight: the falling of raindrops, the growth of plants, the blooming of buds, the ripening of fruits, the murmur of streams, the coloring of human tables with various food items, and even the movement of massive factory wheels and the production of electricity and various industrial products—all return to this great source of energy, which is sunlight.

In short, all energies on Earth (except energy resulting from nuclear fission) all draw help from sunlight; if it were not for it, everywhere would be silent and soulless, lightless, motionless, and dead.

The darkness of night, although it carries the scent of death and annihilation, is also considered a vital matter for humans in terms of moderating sunlight and its deep impact on the tranquility of body and soul, and preventing the dangers of uniform solar radiation. If it were not for the alternation of night and day, the heat on Earth would rise so high that it would burn everything, just as on the moon, which has long nights and days (each equal to fifteen Earth days), its days have killing heat and its nights have destroying cold.

Therefore, each of these two (light and darkness) is a great sign among the Divine signs.

Beyond this, the very precise order governing these two creates the organized history of human life—a history without which social relations would collapse and life would become very difficult for man. From this perspective as well, these two are among the Divine signs.

Interestingly, the Quran says in these verses: "The night does not outstrip the day." This expression indicates that the day was created before the night, and the night follows it.

It is true that if someone looks at the Earth from the outside, they see these two like two black and white entities constantly revolving around the Earth, and in this circular motion, "before" and "after" are not imagined.

However, if we consider that our Earth was originally part of the sun, and on that day it was day everywhere and no night existed, but as soon as it separated and moved away, and its cone-shaped shadow fell in the direction opposite to the sunlight, night was born—a night that moves following the day—the precision and subtlety of this expression become clear.

And as we said, not only are the sun and moon swimming in this boundless space, but night and day also swim in this space around the Earth, and each has its own orbit and rotational path.

In several narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), it is explicitly stated that Allah created the day before the night.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "He created the day before the night." [^1]

And in another narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated: "The day was created before the night." Then the Imam عليه السلام argued using the verse: "It is not for the sun to overtake the moon, nor does the night outstrip the day" in this regard. [^2]

The same meaning is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام as follows: "Indeed, Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, created the sun before the moon, and created light before darkness." [^3]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 387). [^3]: Rawdat al-Kafi—as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 387.

Ayahs

وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (٤١)
A sign for them is that We carried their progeny in the laden ship, (41)
وَخَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ مَا يَرْكَبُونَ (٤٢)
and We have created for them what is similar to it, which they ride. (42)
وَإِن نَّشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلَا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنقَذُونَ (٤٣)
And if We like We drown them, whereat they have no one to call for help, nor are they rescued (43)
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٤٤)
except by a mercy from Us and for an enjoyment until some time. (44)

Commentary

The Movement of Ships in the Seas is Also a Sign

Although some commentators, such as al-Qurtubi, have considered the first verse under discussion to be among the most complex verses of this Surah, a careful examination of these verses and their connection to the preceding ones shows that there is no particular complexity in their interpretation. In the previous verses, the discussion focused on the signs of the Lord in the creation of the sun, the moon, the night, and the day, as well as the earth and its blessings. In the verses under discussion, the focus shifts to the seas and a portion of the blessings and bounties of the sea—namely, the movement of commercial and passenger ships upon their surface.

Furthermore, the movement of these ships in the heart of the oceans is not unlike the movement of the celestial bodies in the ocean of space.

Thus, He first says: "And a sign for them is that We carried their forefathers (or offspring) in the laden ship" (وَ آيَةٌ لَهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ).

The pronoun "for them" (لهم) refers not only to the polytheists of Mecca but to all the servants and bondsmen of Allah who were mentioned in the previous verses.

"Dhurriyyah" (ذرية), as Raghib mentions in Al-Mufradat, originally means small children, although in common usage it is sometimes applied to all offspring, whether young or old. This word is used both as a singular and a collective noun.

The fact that He says, "We carried their offspring (or their small children) on these ships," without mentioning the adults themselves, might be because children have a greater need for this easy means of transport, as adults are more capable of walking and traversing the seashores.

Moreover, this expression is more suitable for stirring their emotions.

The term "mashhun" (مشحون - laden/full) indicates that not only do they board the ship themselves, but their merchandise and necessary provisions are also transported with them.

The interpretation by some that "fulk" (فلك) in the above verse refers specifically to the Ark of Noah, and "dhurriyyah" refers to ancestors and fathers (derived from the root dhara'a meaning creation), seems unlikely, unless it is intended to highlight a prominent example.

In any case, the movement of ships—which are the largest and most important means of human transportation, performing tasks thousands of times greater than other mounts—is the result of the specific properties of water, the specific gravity of the materials from which the ship is built, the property of winds (in sailing ships), steam power (in motorized ships), and atomic energy (in ships powered by nuclear energy).

All of these are forces and powers that Allah has subjected to man, and each of them, as well as their collective whole, is a sign among the Divine signs.

To prevent the misconception that ships are the only God-given means of transport, He adds in the next verse: "And We created for them the like of it, that which they ride" (وَ خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ). These are mounts that travel on land or in the air and space, carrying humans and their belongings on their backs.

Although some have interpreted this verse as referring specifically to the camel, which is called the "ship of the desert," and some to all four-legged animals, and others to airplanes and spacecraft invented in our era (the expression "We created" in their case being because their materials and means were created beforehand).

However, the general nature of the verse's expression depicts a broad concept that encompasses all of these and more.

Of course, in some verses of the Quran, "an'am" (livestock/four-legged animals) are repeatedly placed alongside "fulk" (ships), such as: "And He has made for you of ships and livestock that which you ride" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَ الْأَنْعامِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ) (Az-Zukhruf: 12). Also, in verse 80 of Surah Al-Mu'min, we read: "And upon them and upon ships you are carried" (وَ عَلَيْها وَ عَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ).

Yet, these verses do not contradict the generality of the concept in the verse under discussion.

The next verse, to further clarify this great blessing, describes the state that arises when this blessing is altered, saying: "And if We will, We drown them; then there is no helper for them, nor are they rescued" (وَ إِنْ نَشأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْقَذُونَ).

We could command a massive wave to overturn their ship! Or We could commission a whirlpool to swallow them! Or We could order a storm to pick them up like a piece of straw and toss them into the midst of the waves! And if We willed, We could disrupt the properties of water, the ship, the order of the winds, and the calm of the sea so that everything of theirs falls apart. It is We who sustain this system so that they may benefit, and if We occasionally send such incidents, it is so that they may realize the importance of the blessing in which they are immersed.

"Sariikh" (صريخ) is derived from surakh, meaning a helper who responds to a cry for help.

"Yunqadhun" (ينقذون) is from inqadh, meaning to take out and rescue.

Finally, the last verse under discussion adds to complete this discourse: "Except as a mercy from Us and enjoyment for a while" (إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَ مَتاعاً إِلى‌ حِينٍ). Indeed, they cannot be saved by any means unless the breeze of Our mercy blows and Our grace hastens to their aid.

"Hiin" (حين), meaning time, in the above verse refers to the end of human life and his appointed term, and some have interpreted it as the end of the world.

Indeed, those who have boarded ships (whether the small sailing ships of old or the mountain-like ocean liners of today) have well understood the depth of this verse's expression—that the world's largest ships are like a piece of straw against the massive waves of the sea and the terrifying storms of the oceans. If Divine mercy does not encompass humans, their rescue is impossible.

He wishes to show His great power to humans in this narrow margin between life and death, so that perhaps those who have lost their way may come to their senses and find a path toward Him through this.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَمَا خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٤٥)
And when they are told, ‘Beware of that which is before you and that which is behind you, so that you may receive [His] mercy…’ (45)
وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٤٦)
There does not come to them any sign from among the signs of their Lord but that they have been disregarding it. (46)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنُطْعِمُ مَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُۥٓ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٧)
When they are told, ‘Spend out of what Allah has provided you,’ the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Shall we feed [someone] whom Allah would feed, if He wished? You are only in manifest error.’ (47)

Commentary

They Ignore All Divine Signs

Since the previous verses discussed important signs of the Lord across the expanse of the universe, the verses under discussion describe the reaction of the stubborn disbelievers toward the Divine signs, as well as the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) invitation and his warning of the Lord's punishment.

In the first verse, He says: "When it is said to them: 'Beware of what is before you and what is behind you of Divine punishments, so that you may receive mercy,' they turn away and disregard it" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَ ما خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ) [1]. Regarding what is meant by "what is before you" (ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ) and "what is behind you" (ما خلفكم), commentators have offered many interpretations:

Among them is that "what is before you" refers to the punishments of this world, examples of which were mentioned in the preceding verses, and "what is behind you" refers to the punishments of the Hereafter which lie ahead of them. The expression "behind" (خلف) is used because it has not yet arrived; it is as if it is moving behind a person and will eventually reach him one day and seize him. The meaning of "bewaring" of these punishments is not to create the causes for them—in other words, not to do anything that necessitates these penalties.

Evidence for this is that the expression "beware" (اتقوا) in Quranic verses is used either in relation to Allah or in relation to the Day of Resurrection and Divine punishment, both of which essentially return to the same meaning, for fearing Allah is fearing His punishment.

[1]: وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ... is a conditional sentence whose result (jaza) is omitted, which can be understood from the following verse; the implied meaning is: "And when it is said to them: 'Beware...' they turned away from it."

This itself is proof that in the verse under discussion, the intent is to beware of Divine punishment and penalty in this world and the next.

Some have interpreted the verse with the opposite meaning: "what is before you" as the punishment of the Hereafter and "what is behind you" as the punishment of this world, because the Hereafter lies ahead of us (this interpretation does not differ much from the first in terms of the final result).

However, some have said that "before you" refers to sins previously committed, where bewaring of them means repentance and compensation, and "behind you" refers to sins that will be committed later.

Others believe that "before you" refers to manifest sins and "behind you" means hidden sins.

Others consider "what is before you" as an allusion to various worldly punishments and "what is behind you" as an allusion to death (whereas death is not something that can be "bewared" of).

Some, like the author of Fi Zilal, consider these two expressions as metaphors for the encompassing nature of the causes of Divine wrath and punishment that surround the disbelievers from every side.

Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani and Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir have each provided several possibilities, some of which have been mentioned. Allameh Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan considers "what is before you" as an allusion to polytheism and sins in this world, and "what is behind you" as an allusion to punishment in the Hereafter [1], whereas the apparent meaning of the verse is that both are of the same genus and only differ in time, not that one refers to polytheism and sin and the other to their punishment.

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 96 (under the verses discussed).

In any case, the best interpretation for this phrase is what was stated at the beginning, and various verses of the Quran also testify to it: that "what is before you" refers to the punishments of this world and "what is behind you" refers to the punishments of the Hereafter.

In the following verse, He emphasizes this same meaning once more and highlights the stubbornness and persistence of these blind-hearted people in ignoring Divine signs and the teachings of the Prophets, saying: "And no sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord but that they turn away from it" (وَ ما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ). Neither the explanation of the signs within the soul (anfusi) affects them, nor the description of the signs in the horizons (afaqi); neither threats and warnings, nor glad tidings and promises of Divine mercy. They neither accept the logic of reason and intellect, nor the command of emotions and fitrah (innate nature). They are like the blind who do not see the closest objects around them and do not even distinguish the light of the sun from the gloom and darkness of the night!

Then the Quran points to one of the significant instances of their stubbornness and disregard, saying: "And when it is said to them: 'Spend of that which Allah has provided for you,' the disbelievers say to those who believe: 'Shall we feed those whom, if Allah willed, He would have fed? You are only in manifest error!'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَ نُطْعِمُ مَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ). This is the same very superficial logic that is raised in every age and time by selfish and stingy individuals who say: "If so-and-so is poor, he must have done something that Allah wants him to remain poor, and if we are wealthy, we must have done something to deserve Allah's grace; therefore, neither their poverty nor our wealth is without wisdom!!" They are oblivious to the fact that the world is a field of trial and examination. Allah tests one with poverty and another with wealth, and sometimes He puts one person through both at different times to see: does he maintain integrity, dignity, and gratitude during poverty? Or does he trample them all?

And during wealth, does he spend from what he has in His way or not?

Although some have applied the above verse to specific groups, such as the Jews or the Arab polytheists or a group of atheists and deniers of the Prophets' religions, it appears that the verse has a general concept for which examples can be found in every age and time, even if its examples at the time of revelation were individuals from the Jews or polytheists. This has been a common excuse throughout the ages and centuries: they say if the Provider is Allah, then why do you ask us to provide for the poor? And if Allah has willed for them to remain deprived, then why should we benefit someone whom Allah has deprived?

They are unaware that sometimes the system of creation (takwin) necessitates one thing and the system of legislation (tashri') necessitates another.

The system of creation has necessitated that Allah places the earth with all its blessings at the disposal of mankind and leaves them free in their actions to traverse the path of perfection, while simultaneously creating instincts in them that drive them in various directions.

And the system of legislation has necessitated establishing laws to control instincts, purify souls, and train humans through altruism, sacrifice, forgiveness, and spending (infaq), so that man, who has the potential to reach the status of "Vicegerent of Allah," may reach that lofty station through this path. Through Zakat, He purifies souls; through infaq, He removes stinginess from hearts; and He eliminates the class divide which is the source of thousands of corruptions in human life.

This is exactly like people saying: "What necessity is there for us to study or teach others? If Allah willed, He would have given us all knowledge so that no one would need to learn." Would any rational person accept this logic? [1] The phrase "the disbelievers say" (قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا), which emphasizes their title of "disbelief" when a pronoun could have been used instead, indicates that these superstitious logics and excuses stem from "disbelief"!

And the believers' expression "Spend of that which Allah has provided for you" (أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ) points to the fact that in reality, the original owner is Allah, even though this trust has been handed to us and you for a few days. How stingy are those who are not willing to give even someone else's property to another by His command?! Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "You are only in manifest error" (إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ), three possibilities exist:

[1]: A group of commentators have also suggested the possibility that the Arabs at that time were known for hospitality and did not refrain from spending; the goal of the disbelievers was to mock the believers because they attributed everything to Allah's will. They said mockingly: "If Allah willed, He would have made the poor self-sufficient; there is no need for our spending." However, the interpretation mentioned above seems more appropriate (refer to Tafsir al-Tibyan, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses discussed).

First, it is the continuation of the disbelievers' speech addressed to the believers.

Second, it is Allah's address to the disbelievers.

Third, it is the speech of the believers in response to the disbelievers.

However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all because it is connected and joined to the words of the disbelievers; in fact, they wanted to retaliate against the believers and attribute "manifest error" to them!

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٨)
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, should you be truthful?’ (48)
مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ (٤٩)
They do not await but a single Cry that will seize them as they wrangle. (49)
فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٥٠)
Then they will not be able to make any will, nor will they return to their folks. (50)
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنسِلُونَ (٥١)
And when the Trumpet is blown, behold, there they will be, scrambling towards their Lord from their graves! (51)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا مَنۢ بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا ۜ ۗ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَصَدَقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٥٢)
They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep?’ ‘This is what the All-beneficent had promised, and the apostles had spoken the truth!’ (52)
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (٥٣)
It will be but a single Cry, and, behold, they will all be presented before Us! (53)

Commentary

The Cries of Resurrection!

Following the mention of the weak and excuse-seeking logic of the disbelievers regarding charity in the previous verses, the verses under discussion begin by addressing their mockery of the onset of the Day of Resurrection, crushing their decayed logic regarding the denial of the Hereafter with a decisive response.

Furthermore, the discussions presented in the preceding verses concerning Monotheism (Tawhid) are completed with discussions on the Hereafter (Ma'ad).

First, He says: "And they say: 'When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?'" (وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

Their argument is that your inability to determine a specific date for the occurrence of Resurrection is proof that you are not truthful in your speech!

The next verse gives a firm and serious answer to this mocking question, saying:

The onset of Resurrection and the end of this world is not a complex matter or a difficult task for Allah: "They await not but a single mighty Blast (Shout) which will seize them while they are disputing [about their worldly affairs]!" (مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ).

This single great celestial cry is sufficient to take the souls of everyone in a brief moment, each in the same place and state they are in. Their clamorous material life—which is their arena of constant disputes and battlefield—will give way to a silent world, void of any noise.

In Islamic narrations from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated that this celestial cry is so sudden that "two people who have spread out a piece of cloth and are busy trading will find the world ending before they can fold it up! And there are those who, at that moment, have lifted a morsel of food from a dish, but before it reaches their mouth, the celestial cry arrives and the world ends. There are those busy repairing and plastering a trough to water their livestock, but before the livestock are quenched, the Resurrection begins!"

(تقوم الساعة و الرجلان قد نشرا ثوبهما يتبايعانه فما يطويانه حتى تقوم!، و الرجل يرفع اكلته الى فيه فما تصل الى فيه حتى تقوم!، و الرجل يليط حوضه ليسقى ماشيته فما يسقيها حتى تقوم!) [1]

The phrase "ما ينظرون" here means "they do not wait," because the root "نظر" (as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat) means the movement of thought

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed—the same narration appears with slight variations in other commentaries such as Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and others.

or reflection to observe or perceive something, and sometimes it means contemplation and searching, as well as the knowledge resulting from searching.

"صيحة" (Cry/Blast) originally means the splitting of wood or clothing and the sound arising from it; subsequently, it was used for any loud, shout-like sound. Sometimes it is used to mean height of stature; for example, it is said: Barzu fulan shajar qad saha!: "In such-and-such land is a tree that shouts!" meaning it has grown so tall that it is as if it is shouting and calling people toward itself.

"يخصمون" is from the root khusumah, meaning dispute and fighting.

But what are they disputing about? It is not mentioned in the verse, but it is evident that it refers to disputes over worldly matters and material life. However, some have interpreted it as disputing over the matter of "Resurrection," though the first meaning seems more appropriate, although it is not unlikely that a comprehensive meaning including both—and every kind of dispute and quarrel—is intended.

It is noteworthy that the multiple pronouns in the verse all refer to the polytheists of Mecca who doubted the Resurrection and mockingly asked: "When will the Resurrection occur?"

However, it is certain that the reference is not to them personally, but to their kind (the type of humans who are heedless and unaware of the Resurrection), for they died and never saw this celestial cry (reflect on this).

In any case, the Quran warns them with this brief and decisive expression that, firstly, the Resurrection will occur suddenly and unexpectedly, and secondly, it is not a complex subject for them to rise in debate and dispute over its possibility; with one cry, everything ends and the world reaches its conclusion.

Therefore, in the next verse, it says: This matter is so swift, lightning-fast, and sudden that "they will not even be able to make a will, nor will they have the chance to return to their families and homes!" (فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلَا إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ).

Usually, when an accident happens to a person and they feel the end of their life is near, they try to reach their home and shelter wherever they are, to be among their spouse and children, and then entrust their unfinished business and the fate of their survivors to others through a will and recommendation.

But does the cry of the world's end give anyone a chance? Or, assuming there were a chance, would anyone remain alive to hear a person's recommendations? Or, for example, could a wife and child sit at the bedside of a husband and father and hold his head in their arms so he may pass away in peace? None of these things are possible.

The fact that "توصية" (will/recommendation) appears in the indefinite form (nakirah) indicates that they will not find the opportunity for even a single small recommendation.

Then it points to the next stage, which is the stage of life after death, saying: "And the Trumpet will be blown; then suddenly from the graves they will hasten toward their Lord" (وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ الْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ).

Dust and decayed bones, by the command of the Lord, will put on the garment of life and rise from the grave, appearing for trial and accounting in that extraordinary court. Just as they all died with one "Cry" (Sayhah), they will gain soul and come to life with one "Blast"

(Nafkh—blowing into the Trumpet). Neither their death is difficult for Allah, nor their revival; it is exactly like a bugle sounded to gather and alert an army, and in an instant, everyone wakes from sleep, runs out of tents, and stands in line. Reviving the dead is just as simple and swift for Allah! "اجداث" is the plural of "جدث" (rhyming with qafas), meaning grave. This expression clearly shows that the Resurrection has a physical aspect in addition to a spiritual one, and the new body is fashioned from the same previous materials.

The expression "نفخ" (was blown) in the past tense is because the Arabs usually express certain future matters in the past tense, indicating that there is no doubt or hesitation in it, as if it has already occurred.

"ينسلون" is from the root "نسل" (rhyming with fasl), meaning to walk swiftly. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat that this word originally means separating from something, and the reason human offspring are called "نسل" is that they have separated from their parents (therefore, when a person moves away and separates with speed, this expression is used).

The expression "ربهم" (their Lord) likely points to the fact that Allah's Lordship, Ownership, and Nurturing require that there be an accounting, a book, and a Resurrection.

In any case, it is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that the end of this world and the beginning of the next both occur with a revolutionary and sudden movement, and each is referred to as the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (Nafkh al-Sur), the full explanation of which will come, Allah willing, under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar.

Then it adds: "At this time, the deniers of the Resurrection and the Hereafter will say: 'Woe to us! Who has raised us from our place of sleep?'" (قَالُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا مَن بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا).

"[They will be told:] 'This is what the Most Merciful (Allah) had promised, and the Messengers spoke the truth!'" (هَٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ).

Yes, the scene is so eloquent and terrifying that man forgets all false and superstitious matters and finds no way but explicit confession to the realities. He compares the graves to a place of sleep and the Resurrection to waking up from sleep, as stated in the famous Hadith: "As you sleep, you shall die, and as you wake, you shall be raised."

Here, they first panic and cry out: "Woe to us! Who woke us from this sleep and raised us from our place of sleep?!" But they soon realize and remember that the true Prophets from Allah had promised them this day in the world. They answer themselves:

This is the promise of the Most Merciful Allah—the God whose general mercy encompasses everyone—and His Prophets spoke the truth and informed us of this day, but alas, we took it all in mockery and derision.

Therefore, the sentence "هَٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ" is a continuation of the speech of those same deniers of Resurrection. However, some have considered it the speech of angels or believers, which is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse and there is no necessity for it, as the confession of the deniers to the truths on that day is not something mentioned only in this verse. As stated in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 97: "And the true promise (Resurrection) draws near. Then behold! The eyes of the disbelievers will stare in horror [and they will say]: 'Woe to us! We were indeed heedless of this; nay, but we were wrongdoers.'"

In any case, the expression "مرقد" which means "place of sleep" or "sleep" [1] expresses the reality that in the world of Barzakh (the Purgatory), they fall into a state similar to sleep. As we stated under verse 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, Barzakh for the majority of people—who are in an intermediate state of faith and disbelief—is not unlike a state of sleep, while the foremost believers and the exceptionally wicked disbelievers are fully conscious there, enjoying blessings or suffering various punishments. [2] Some have also suggested that the horror and terror of the Resurrection are so great that the punishment of Barzakh in comparison is like nothing more than a peaceful sleep.

Then, to explain the speed of the occurrence of this Blowing of the Trumpet, He says: "It will be but a single Blast, and behold! They will all be brought before Us" (إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ).

Therefore, for the revival of the dead, their rising from the graves, and their presence in the court of the Lord's justice, not much time is needed, just as a long time was not needed for the death of humans. The first cry is the cry of death, and the second cry is the cry of life, existence, and presence in the court of the Lord's justice!

[1]: In the first case it is a "noun of place" and in the second case it is a "mimic infinitive."

[2]: Regarding Barzakh and the state of people there, we have spoken in Volume 14, page 321 onwards.

The expression "صيحة" (a cry) and its emphasis with "واحدة" (single), and then the use of "اذا" (behold/suddenly) which in such cases reports the sudden occurrence of something, and the expression "هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ" as a nominal sentence, are all evidence of the rapid occurrence of this stage of Resurrection.

The decisive tone of these verses and their penetrating rhythm affect the hearts of humans such that it is as if they hear this cry with the ear of the soul: "O sleeping humans! O scattered dust! And O decayed bones! Arise, arise and prepare for the accounting and recompense!" How beautiful are the verses of the Quran and how eloquent are its warnings!

Ayahs

فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٤)
‘Today no soul will be wronged in the least, nor will you be requited except for what you used to do.’ (54)
إِنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى شُغُلٍ فَـٰكِهُونَ (٥٥)
Indeed today the inhabitants of paradise rejoice in their engagements (55)
هُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُمْ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ مُتَّكِـُٔونَ (٥٦)
—they and their mates, reclining on couches in the shades. (56)
لَهُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَلَهُم مَّا يَدَّعُونَ (٥٧)
There they have fruits, and they have whatever they want. (57)
سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْلًا مِّن رَّبٍّ رَّحِيمٍ (٥٨)
‘Peace!’—a watchword from the all-merciful Lord. (58)

Commentary

The People of Paradise Immersed in Material and Spiritual Bounties

At this point, the discussion regarding the nature of the reckoning in the Gathering is left concise, and the text moves on to describe the ultimate fate of the righteous believers and the wicked disbelievers, stating: "This Day, no soul shall be wronged in the least" (فَالْيَوْمَ لا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً).

Neither shall anyone's reward be diminished, nor shall anyone's punishment be increased; there exists not even a needle's point of fluctuation, injustice, or oppression.

Then, it provides an explanation that is, in fact, a clear and vivid reason for the absence of injustice in that Great Court, saying: "and you shall not be recompensed except for what you used to do" (وَ لا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

The literal implication of this expression—without assuming any hidden words—is that the recompense for all of you is your very own deeds. What justice could be better or higher than this? In other words: the deeds, good or bad, that you perform in this world will be with you there; those very deeds will be personified (embodied) and will be your companion and associate in all the stations of the Gathering and after the conclusion of the reckoning. Is handing over the product of someone's own deeds to them contrary to justice? And is giving form to the essence of the deeds and making them one's companion an act of oppression?

From this, it becomes clear that "oppression" fundamentally has no meaning in that scene. If justice and oppression sometimes exist among humans here, it is because they lack the ability to return the exact deeds of every individual back to them.

A group of commentators has imagined that this latter sentence applies specifically to the evildoers and disbelievers—who are punished exactly according to their deeds—and does not include the believers, because Allah rewards them far beyond their deeds.

However, this misunderstanding is resolved by noting one point: the discussion here is about justice in reward and punishment and receiving the deserved recompense. This does not contradict Allah adding thousands upon thousands more for the believers out of His grace and mercy; for that is a matter of "favor" (tafaddul), while this is a matter of "entitlement" (istihqaq).

Then, it addresses a portion of the believers' rewards, and before anything else, it points to the issue of peace of mind, saying: "Indeed, the dwellers of Paradise on that Day are so occupied with the blessings of Allah that they are removed from every distressing thought" (إِنَّ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ الْيَوْمَ فِي شُغُلٍ). "And they are in a state of ultimate joy and happiness" (فاكهون).

"Shughl" (rhyming with shotor) and "Shughl" (rhyming with ghofl) both mean events and states that occur to a person and keep them occupied, whether they are joyful or sorrowful.
