However, since it is immediately followed by the word "fakihun"—which is the plural of "fakih," meaning joyful, happy, and laughing—it can refer to matters that, due to extreme joy, occupy a person so much that they become completely oblivious to worrisome matters. They will be so immersed in delight and vigor that grief and sorrow will not overcome them. They will even forget the terror and dread they experienced during the rising of the Resurrection and their presence in the Court of Divine Justice; for if it were not truly forgotten, the shadow of anxiety and grief would always weigh heavily on their hearts. Therefore, one of the effects of this mental occupation is forgetting the horrors of the Gathering. [^1]

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Fakihah" means every kind of fruit, and "Fikah" refers to speech that makes a person feel at home and occupied. Ibn Manzur says in Lisan al-Arab: "Fikah" means joking, and "Fakih" refers to a good-humored and jovial person.

In any case, after the blessing of peace of mind—which is the essence of all blessings and the condition for utilizing all bounties—it proceeds to describe other blessings, saying:

"They and their spouses are in pleasant shades, reclining on thrones in private retreats" (هُمْ وَ أَزْواجُهُمْ فِي ظِلالٍ عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ مُتَّكِؤُنَ). [^1] "Azwaj" means heavenly spouses, or the believing spouses they had in this world. The possibility suggested by some that it means "peers" (as in Surah As-Saffat, verse 22: "Gather those who wronged and their peers") seems very unlikely here. Especially since "ara'ik" is the plural of "arikah," which, according to many commentators and lexicographers, means thrones located in a bridal chamber. [^2] The expression "zhilal" (shades) refers to the shades of the trees of Paradise, among which the thrones of the people of Paradise are placed, or the shades of the palaces of Paradise. All of this indicates that there is a sun there as well, but not a distressing sun; indeed, they have a different kind of vigor and joy in the pleasant shade of the trees of Paradise.

Furthermore, "For them therein is fruit [of every kind], and they shall have whatever they call for" (لَهُمْ فِيها فاكِهَةٌ وَ لَهُمْ ما يَدَّعُونَ).

It is well-understood from other verses of the Quran that the food of the people of Paradise is not limited to fruit, but the expression in the above verse shows that fruit—specifically those special fruits that are very different from the fruits of this world—is the most superior food of Paradise. Even in this world, according to nutritionists, fruit is the best and most suitable food for humans.

"Yadda'un" is derived from the root "di'ayah," meaning to request; that is, whatever they request and desire is available to them, and they have no wish in their hearts that remains unfulfilled.

The late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: The Arabs use this expression regarding "desire" (tamanni); one says: Idda'i 'ala ma shi'ta, meaning: "Ask of me and desire whatever you wish."

Thus, whatever can be contained in human thought today, and whatever does not even cross one's mind, is prepared and ready there in the form of various bounties and blessings. Allah's hospitality for His guests is carried out at the highest possible level.

But more important than all of these are the spiritual bounties mentioned in the final verse under discussion, which says: "Peace!—a word from a Merciful Lord" (سَلامٌ قَوْلًا مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ). [^1] This soul-refreshing and invigorating call, full of love and affection from Him, so immerses the human soul that it grants a pleasure, joy, and spirituality incomparable to any other blessing. Yes, hearing the call of the Beloved—a call mixed with affection and brimming with grace—immerses the people of Paradise in joy from head to toe, a single moment of which is superior to the entire world and all that is in it!

In a narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "While the people of Paradise are immersed in heavenly blessings, a light appears above them; it is the light of Allah's grace shining upon them. A call arises: 'Peace be upon you, O people of Paradise,' and this is what is mentioned in the Quran: 'Peace!—a word from a Merciful Lord.' At this point, the gaze of Allah's grace so attracts them that they become oblivious to everything except Him, and they forget all the blessings of Paradise in that state. It is then that the angels enter upon them from every gate saying, 'Peace be upon you.'" [^1] Indeed, the attraction of witnessing the Beloved and the sight of the Friend's grace is so pleasurable and soul-stirring that a single moment of it is not equal to any blessing, even the entire world. The lovers of meeting Him are such that if this spiritual effusion were cut off from them, they would perish, as stated in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام:

"If I were veiled from Him for a single hour, I would die!" [^2]

Interestingly, the literal sense of the verse is that this greeting from the Lord, bestowed upon the believing people of Paradise, is a direct greeting without an intermediary. It is a greeting from the Rabb (Lord), and furthermore, a greeting originating from His special mercy—that is, His station of Rahimiyyah—in which all graces and honors are gathered. Oh, what a blessing!

[^1]: There are many possibilities regarding the grammatical position of "qawlan" among commentators, but the most appropriate is that it is a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq) for a hidden verb, as if it were "yaqulu qawlan" (He says a word).

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 23, p. 35, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 416.

Key Points The Types of Greetings Bestowed Upon the People of Paradise

Fundamentally, Paradise is the "Abode of Peace" (Dar al-Salam), as we read in Surah Yunus, verse 25: "And Allah invites to the Abode of Peace." The people of Paradise, who are the inhabitants of this land, sometimes encounter the greeting of the angels who enter upon them from every gate upon their arrival in Paradise, saying: "Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured. And excellent is the final home" (Ar-Ra'd: 24).

Sometimes the inhabitants of the Heights (A'raf) call out to them, saying: "Peace be upon you" (Al-A'raf: 46).

Sometimes, after entering Paradise, they encounter the greeting and salutation of the angels, and sometimes at the moment of the soul being taken, this greeting is bestowed upon them by the angels of death, who say: "Peace be upon you; enter Paradise for what you used to do" (An-Nahl: 32).

And sometimes they send greetings and peace upon one another, and fundamentally, "their greeting therein will be: Peace" (Ibrahim: 23).

And finally, superior and higher than all of these is the greeting of the Lord: "Peace!—a word from a Merciful Lord."

In short, "They will not hear therein any ill speech or commission of sin—only a saying: 'Peace, peace'" (Al-Waqi'ah: 25-26).

But this is not a greeting that exists only in words; rather, it is a greeting whose tranquilizing and health-giving effect penetrates the depths of the human soul and spirit, immersing everyone in peace and well-being.

Ayahs

وَٱمْتَـٰزُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٥٩)
And ‘Get apart today, you guilty ones!’ (59)
۞ أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٦٠)
‘Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. (60)
وَأَنِ ٱعْبُدُونِى ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦١)
Worship Me. That is a straight path”? (61)
وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا ۖ أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٢)
Certainly, he has led astray many of your generations. Have you not exercised your reason? (62)

Commentary

Why Do You Worship Satan?!

A portion of the inspiring and proud journey of the people of Paradise was mentioned in the preceding verses; the verses under discussion now point to a part of the fate of the people of Hell and the servants of Satan.

First, on that Day, they will be addressed with a humiliating call and told: "And get aside today, O you criminals!" (وَ امْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ).

It was you who, in the world, forced yourselves into the ranks of the believers, sometimes taking on their appearance and exploiting their prestige and credibility. Today, separate your ranks from theirs and appear in your true form! This is, in fact, the realization of the Divine promise mentioned in Surah Sad, verse 28:

"أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ": "Shall We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like those who spread corruption on earth? Or shall We treat the righteous like the wicked?" In any case, the apparent meaning of the verse under discussion is the separation of the ranks of the criminals from the believers, although commentators have suggested several other possibilities, including:

  • The separation of the ranks of the criminals from one another, with each group being placed in a specific category.
  • Or their separation from their intercessors and the objects of their worship.
  • Or the separation of every single individual from one another, such that in addition to the immense pain and suffering of Hell, the pain and suffering of separation and parting from everyone and everything casts a heavy and ominous shadow over them.

However, the inclusiveness of the address to all of them and the content of the phrase "And get aside" (وَ امْتازُوا) strengthens the first meaning we mentioned.

The next verse points to the meaningful reproaches and rebukes of Allah toward the criminals on the Day of Resurrection, saying: "Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam, that you should not worship Satan—for indeed, he is your clear enemy?" (أَ لَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ).

This Divine covenant and pledge was taken from man through various means, and this point has been reminded to him repeatedly:

First, on the day the children of Adam began to grow and multiply on earth, this address was made to them:

"يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ كَما أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ يَنْزِعُ عَنْهُما لِباسَهُما لِيُرِيَهُما سَوْآتِهِما إِنَّهُ يَراكُمْ هُوَ وَ قَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا تَرَوْنَهُمْ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ أَوْلِياءَ لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ": "O children of Adam, let not Satan tempt you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their clothing to show them their private parts. Indeed, he sees you, he and his tribe, from where you do not see them. Indeed, We have made the devils allies to those who do not believe" (Al-A'raf: 27).

Then, this same warning was repeatedly issued through the tongues of the Divine Messengers, as we read in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 62: "وَ لا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ": "And let not Satan avert you. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy."

And in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 168, we read: "وَ لا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانُ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ": "And do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy!"

On the other hand, this covenant was also taken in the "existential" (takwini) realm through the granting of intellect to man, for intellectual proofs clearly testify that man should not obey the command of one who, from the very first day, set out to be his enemy, caused him to be expelled from Paradise, and swore to beguile his children.

Thirdly, through the Divine nature (fitrah) of all human beings—which is based on monotheism and the exclusivity of obedience to the Pure Essence of the Lord—this covenant was also taken. Thus, this Divine recommendation has been realized and this fateful covenant signed not through one language, but through several.

It is also noteworthy that "worship" (عبادت) in the phrase "do not worship Satan" means "obedience," because worship does not always mean ritual adoration, bowing, and prostration; rather, one of its forms is obedience. As we read in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 47, that Pharaoh and his associates said after the appointment of Moses عليه السلام and Aaron عليه السلام: (أَ نُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنا وَ قَوْمُهما لَنا عابِدُونَ). "Should we believe in two men like ourselves while their people are for us worshippers (servants in obedience)?"

And in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 31, we read regarding the Jews and Christians: "اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَ رُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَ ما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلهاً واحِداً": "They have taken their scholars and monks as lords besides Allah, and [also] the Messiah, the son of Mary. And they were not commanded except to worship one God; there is no deity except Him."

Interestingly, in a narration from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام regarding this verse, we read: "By Allah, they (the scholars and monks) did not call them to worship themselves, and had they called them, the Jews and Christians would never have answered them. However, they made lawful for them what was forbidden and forbade what was lawful (and they accepted it), and thus they worshipped them without even realizing it!" [1]

Similar meanings with slight variations have been recorded in other narrations: Among them, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever obeys a man in disobedience to the Lord has worshipped him." [2]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated: "Whoever listens to a speaker (and accepts his words) has worshipped him! If the speaker conveys from Allah, he has worshipped Allah, and if the speaker conveys from Satan, he has worshipped Satan." [3]

In the next verse, for further emphasis and to state the duty of the children of Adam, He says: "And that you worship Me? This is the straight path." (وَ أَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ).

On one hand, He took a covenant that they should not obey Satan, for he revealed his enmity and hostility from the first day; what rational person would act upon the command of his ancient and manifest enemy?

In contrast, He took a covenant that they should obey Him, and the reason He gives is that "this is the straight path." This is, in fact, the best motivator for humans. For example, if someone is trapped in the middle of a dry and scorching desert and sees his life, his spouse, children, and property in danger from thieves and wolves, the most important thing he thinks about is finding the straight path to his destination—a path that will lead him most quickly and easily to the place of salvation.

Furthermore, it is understood from this expression that this world is not a permanent residence, for a path is shown to someone who is passing through a transit point and has a destination ahead.

Again, to further identify this dangerous old enemy, He adds: "And he has indeed led astray a great multitude of you. Did you not then understand?" (وَ لَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنْكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيراً أَ فَلَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْقِلُونَ).

Do you not see what miseries Satan has brought upon his followers? Have you not studied the history of those before you to see what an ominous and painful fate his servants met? The ruins of their disaster-stricken cities are before your eyes, and their tragic end is clear to anyone who possesses the slightest intellect or reflection.

So why do you not take seriously an enemy who has proven his hostility time and time again? Why do you still plot friendship with him, and even choose him as your leader, guardian, and guide?

"Jibill" (جِبِلّ) as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means a congregation or group which, in terms of its greatness, is likened to "Jabal" (mountain). The term "kathira" (many) is for further emphasis regarding the followers of Satan, who constitute a vast segment in every society.

Some have written the number of "Jibill" to be ten thousand or more, and did not consider this term appropriate for less than that [1], while others do not consider these specific numbers necessary [2]. In any case, sound intellect dictates that man should be strictly wary of such a dangerous enemy who shows mercy to no human and whose victims lie in the dust of destruction in every corner. As the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that aware and vigilant leader, addressed the people in one of his sermons to draw attention to this reality, saying:

"So beware, O servants of Allah! of the enemy of Allah, lest he infect you with his disease (pride and arrogance), and lest he unsettle you with his call, and lest he bring against you his cavalry and infantry. By my life, he has placed the arrow of threat on the bowstring for you, drawn it with great power and intensity to the limit of his ability, and aimed at you from a nearby place! He is the one who said: 'My Lord, because You have sent me astray, I will surely make [disobedience] fair-seeming to them on earth, and I will surely mislead them all' (while Allah was not the cause of his straying, rather his own whims led him astray)." [3]

And truly, it is strange that we choose such an enemy as a friend; as the poet says: Where shall I escape this shame and disgrace, That we are at peace with him and at war with the Truth?

Ayahs

هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (٦٣)
This is the hell you had been promised! (63)
ٱصْلَوْهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٦٤)
Enter it today, because of what you used to deny. (64)
ٱلْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلَىٰٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنَآ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٦٥)
Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness concerning what they used to earn.’ (65)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَطَمَسْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَٱسْتَبَقُوا۟ ٱلصِّرَٰطَ فَأَنَّىٰ يُبْصِرُونَ (٦٦)
Had We wished We would have blotted out their eyes: then, were they to advance towards the path, how would have they seen? (66)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَمَسَخْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِهِمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مُضِيًّا وَلَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٦٧)
And had We wished We would have deformed them in their place; then they would neither have been able to move ahead nor to return. (67)
وَمَن نُّعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ ۖ أَفَلَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٨)
And whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation. Then, will they not exercise their reason? (68)

Commentary

The Day When the Tongue Fails and the Limbs Bear Witness!

The previous verses recounted a portion of Allah’s reproaches and His dialogue with the criminals on the Day of Resurrection; the verses under discussion continue this same theme in another section.

Indeed, on that Day, while the scorching and blazing fire of Hell is placed before the eyes of the criminals, He points to it and addresses them, saying: "This is the Hell which you were promised!" (هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ)

Divine Prophets came one after another and warned you of such a day and such a fire, but you took them all as a joke and mockery. "Enter it this day and burn with its searing heat, for this is the recompense for the disbelief you used to hold." (اصْلَوْهَا الْيَوْمَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ) [1]

Then He refers to the witnesses of the Day of Resurrection—witnesses who are part of the human being's own body, leaving no room for the denial of their words. He says: "This day We set a seal

[1]: "Aslawha" (اصْلَوْهَا) is from the root "Salā" (صلى), meaning to kindle a fire, to burn in fire, to roast, or to enter the fire and remain constant in it.

upon their mouths, and their hands speak to Us, and their feet bear witness to what they used to earn." (الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى‌ أَفْواهِهِمْ وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ تَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ).

Indeed, on that Day, man's limbs are no longer subservient to his desires; they separate their account from the person's entire being, submit to the Lord, bow before His sacred threshold, and reveal the truths through their testimony. What a strange court it is where the witnesses are the very limbs of the person's own body—the very tools with which the sin was committed! Perhaps the testimony of the limbs is because when these criminals are told that their punishment for the deeds they performed is Hell, they rise in denial, imagining it to be like a worldly court where truths can be denied through deception. Then the testimony of the limbs begins, and wonder and terror seize their entire being as all paths of escape are closed to them.

Regarding how the limbs speak, commentators have offered several possibilities:

  1. On that Day, Allah creates perception, consciousness, and the power of speech in each individual limb, and they truly speak. And what is there to wonder at? The One who created this power in a piece of flesh called the tongue or in the human brain is capable of creating it in other limbs as well.

  2. They do not possess independent perception or consciousness, but Allah makes them speak; in reality, the limbs will be the locus for the manifestation of speech, revealing truths by Allah's command.

  3. The limbs of every human being will certainly carry the traces of the deeds performed throughout their entire life, for no action is lost in this world.

Certainly, its effects remain on each limb of the body and in the surrounding environment. On that Day, which is the day of exposure and manifestation, these traces appear on the hands, feet, and other limbs, and the appearance of these traces is equivalent to their testimony.

This expression is also frequent in everyday speech and literary metaphors. For example, they say: ‘Aynuka tashhadu bi-saharik: "Your eye bears witness to your sleeplessness!" Or we say: Al-hītānu tabkī ‘alā sāhib al-dār: "The walls weep for the master of the house!" A Persian poet also says: "The color of the face gives news of the secret within!" In any case, the testimony of the limbs on the Day of Resurrection is certain. But does each limb recount only the specific deed it performed, or all deeds? Undoubtedly, the first possibility is more appropriate; hence, in other verses of the Quran, the testimony of the ears, eyes, and skin is mentioned.

As we read in Surah Fussilat, verse 20: حَتَّى إِذا ما جاؤُها شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَ أَبْصارُهُمْ وَ جُلُودُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ: "Until, when they reach it, their hearing and their eyes and their skins will bear witness against them as to what they used to do."

And in Surah An-Nur, verse 24: يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "On the Day when their tongues, their hands, and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do."

It is also noteworthy that in one place it says: "their tongues will bear witness" (as in the verse from Surah An-Nur), while in the verses under discussion it says: "We set a seal upon their mouths."

This expression may be because first, a seal is set upon the human tongue and the limbs begin to speak; when the person sees the testimony of the limbs,

his tongue is opened, and since there is no room for denial, the tongue also confesses.

There is also the possibility that the testimony of the tongue does not refer to normal speech, but rather a speech like that of the other limbs, arising from within rather than without! (Regarding the number of witnesses in that great court and the nature of their testimony, we will discuss this in more detail, Allah willing, under verses 19-23 of Surah Fussilat).

A final point is that the testimony of the limbs pertains to the disbelievers and criminals; otherwise, the account of the believers is clear. Thus, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

"The limbs do not bear witness against a believer; they only bear witness against one for whom the word of punishment has become due. As for the believer, he is given his record in his right hand. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'So whoever is given his record in his right hand—those will read their record, and they will not be wronged [even as much as] a hair on a date seed.'" [1]

In the next verse, He points to one of the punishments that Allah might afflict this criminal group with in this very world—a painful and terrifying punishment. He says: "And if We willed, We could have obliterated their eyes!" (وَ لَوْ نَشاءُ لَطَمَسْنا عَلى‌ أَعْيُنِهِمْ) [2]

[1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses discussed.

[2]: "Tamasna" (طمسنا) is from the root "Tams" (طمس), meaning to obliterate and destroy the traces of something. Here it refers to obliterating the light of the eye or the eye itself such that nothing remains of it and it is completely wiped out.

In this state, an extraordinary terror seizes them: "They would struggle to find the path they usually took and try to outstrip one another, but how could they see?" (فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ فَأَنَّى يُبْصِرُونَ).

They would be unable even to find the way to their own homes, let alone find the path of truth and walk upon the Straight Path!

Another painful punishment is: "And if We willed, We could have transformed them in their places (into lifeless, motionless statues or paralyzed animal forms) so that they could neither proceed forward nor return back." (وَ لَوْ نَشاءُ لَمَسَخْناهُمْ عَلى‌ مَكانَتِهِمْ فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مُضِيًّا وَ لا يَرْجِعُونَ) [1] The phrase "فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ" might mean racing one another to find the path they usually traveled, or it might mean deviating from the path and failing to find it, as some linguists have said the phrase means "they passed it and left it until they went astray." [2] In any case, according to this interpretation—which most Islamic commentators have accepted—the two verses above relate to worldly punishments and are a threat to the disbelievers and criminals that God can afflict them with such painful fates in this very world, yet He has not done so out of His grace and mercy, so that perhaps these stubborn ones might wake up and return to the path of truth.

[1]: "Makanah" (مكانة) means the place of standing or staying. Here it indicates that God can take them out of their human form right where they stand, such that they both change appearance and lose the ability to move, exactly like lifeless statues.

[2]: Lisan al-Arab, Qutr al-Muhit, Al-Munjid (root: s-b-q).

However, another possibility exists: that these verses refer to divine punishments on the Day of Resurrection, not in this world. In fact, following the previous verse which said We set a seal on their mouths on that Day, these verses point to two other punishments that God will implement if He wills:

First, that He blinds their eyes so they cannot find the "Sirat," meaning the path to Paradise. Second, that these individuals, who in the world lacked movement on the path of felicity, are turned into lifeless statues on that Day, remaining bewildered in the arena of gathering, having no way forward and no way back. Of course, the context of the verses supports this interpretation, even though most commentators have preferred the former. [1]

In the final verse under discussion, He points to the state of man at the end of his life in terms of the weakness and frailty of mind and body. This serves as a warning to those who procrastinate in choosing the path of guidance, an answer to those who blame their shortcomings on the shortness of life, and a proof of Allah's power—that just as He can return a strong man to the weakness and frailty of an infant, He is capable of the Resurrection, as well as blinding the criminals and halting their movement. He says: "And whomever We grant long life, We reverse him in creation; do they not then understand?" (وَ مَنْ نُعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِي الْخَلْقِ أَ فَلا يَعْقِلُونَ).

To explain, "nunakkishu" (نُنَكِّسْهُ) is from the root "tankis," meaning to overturn

[1]: Fi Zilal mentions this as the sole interpretation, while Majma' al-Bayan, al-Tibyan, al-Mizan, al-Safi, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Ruh al-Bayan, Qurtubi, and the Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi have chosen the former.

something such that the head is where the feet should be and the feet where the head should be. Here, it is a metaphor for man's complete return to the states of infancy.

For man is weak from the beginning of creation and gradually moves toward growth and perfection. During the fetal stage, every day witnesses a new creation and new growth. After birth, he continues his evolutionary path in body and spirit rapidly, and the God-given powers and talents hidden within his being blossom one after another. The period of youth arrives, followed by maturity, and man stands at the peak of physical and spiritual perfection. At this point, sometimes the spirit and body part ways; the spirit continues its perfection, while the physical regression begins. Eventually, however, the intellect also begins its downward journey, gradually—and sometimes rapidly—returning to the stages of childhood. Actions become childish, and thinking and even making excuses become like those of children, with physical weakness harmonizing with it. The difference is that these actions and temperaments are sweet and attractive in children, a promise of a hopeful and joyful future blossoming, and for this reason, they are entirely tolerable. But from the elderly, they are jarring, ungraceful, and sometimes repulsive or pitiable.

Truly, days arrive that are very painful, the depth of whose distress can hardly be imagined. The Holy Quran also refers to this meaning in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 5, saying: وَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلى‌ أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ لِكَيْلا يَعْلَمَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْئاً: "And among you is he who is returned to the most abject age so that he will not know, after [having had] knowledge, a thing." (He will not even recognize the closest members of his family!).

Thus, in some narrations, seventy-year-old individuals are mentioned as Asir-ullah fil-ard (the captives of Allah on earth). [1] In any case, the phrase "Do they not then understand?" gives a striking warning in this regard, saying to humans: If this power and ability you possess were not borrowed, it would not be taken from you so easily. Know that another hand of power is above you, which is capable of all things.

Realize your worth before you reach that stage, and before vitality and beauty turn to witheredness, pluck flowers from this meadow and take provisions for the long journey of the Hereafter from this world; for in the season of weakness, old age, and helplessness, you will be capable of nothing! Therefore, one of the five things the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله advised Abu Dharr was to seize the period of youth before old age:

"Take advantage of five before five: your youth before your old age, your health before your sickness, your wealth before your poverty, your life before your death, and your free time before your preoccupation." [2] Or as the poet says: A youth once said to an old man, "How is life with your old age?" He replied, "In this letter is an ambiguous word, Whose meaning you shall not know until the time of old age! Better that you speak of your own strength; Why ask of the period of weakness? The commodity that I gave away for free— If you can, do not give it away for free!"

[1]: This phrase appears in a Prophetic hadith (Safinah, root: ‘-m-r), while in some other narrations, ninety years is mentioned.

[2]: Bihar al-Anwar, vol. 77, p. 77.

Ayahs

وَمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ ٱلشِّعْرَ وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْءَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (٦٩)
We did not teach him poetry, nor does it behoove him. This is just a reminder and a manifest Quran, (69)
لِّيُنذِرَ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٠)
so that anyone who is alive may be warned, and that the word may come due against the faithless. (70)

Commentary

He is not a poet; He is a warner to the living!

We have stated that in this Surah, lively and comprehensive discussions regarding the fundamental beliefs of Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and Prophethood (Nubuwwah) are presented, shifting the discourse from one to another at various intervals.

In the preceding verses, various discussions regarding Monotheism and the Resurrection were raised. In the two verses above, the discourse returns to Prophethood, addressing one of the most common accusations leveled against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and providing a crushing and instructive response to it. That accusation is the claim of poetry and being a poet. It says: "We did not teach him poetry, nor is it fitting for him to be a poet" (وَ ما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَ ما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ).

Why did they accuse the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of such a thing when he had never composed poetry? This was because the attraction and influence of the Quran on hearts were perceptible to everyone, and the beauty of its words and meanings, as well as its eloquence and clarity, were undeniable. Even the polytheists themselves were so captivated by the rhythm and expression of the Quran that they would sometimes come secretly at night near the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله dwelling to listen to the murmur of his recitation in the heart of the night.

How many people became infatuated and enamored by hearing just a few verses of the Quran, accepting Islam in that very gathering and seeking refuge in the embrace of the Quran!

It was here that, to justify this great phenomenon and to deceive the people regarding this heavenly revelation, they spread the rumor of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله poetry and poetic nature everywhere—which was itself an implicit admission of the extraordinary influence of the Quran! But why is it not fitting for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to be a poet? Because the path of "Revelation" is completely separate from the path of "Poetry," for:

1- Usually, the source of poetry is imagination and fancy; a poet mostly rides and flies on the wings of imagination, whereas Revelation originates from the Source of Existence and revolves around realities.

2- Poetry gushes from changing human emotions and is constantly in a state of flux, whereas Revelation expresses fixed heavenly truths.

3- The charm of poetry in many instances lies in its exaggerations and hyperboles, to the extent that it has been said: Ahsan al-shi'r akdhabuhu! ("The best poetry is the most untruthful of it"), whereas in Revelation, there is nothing but honesty.

4- In many cases, for the sake of verbal beauty, a poet is forced to surrender to words and follow them, and many truths may be trampled upon in the process.

5- Finally, in the beautiful expression of one of the commentators, "Poetry" is a collection of longings that fly from the earth to the sky, but "Revelation" is a collection of truths that descend from the sky to the earth, and these two paths are completely different! Again, here it is necessary to maintain a separate account for poets who walk the path of sacred goals and keep themselves free from the undesirable side effects of poetry, and we must not forget the value of their status and art; however, the general nature of poetry is as described.

For this reason, the Holy Quran says at the end of Surah al-Shu'ara: وَ الشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ ("As for the poets, it is the erring who follow them")! Then, in a short and meaningful phrase, it mentions the reason for this, saying:

أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي كُلِّ وادٍ يَهِيمُونَ وَ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ ما لا يَفْعَلُونَ ("Do you not see that they wander distracted in every valley [always immersed in their poetic fancies and metaphors, surrendering to the waves of emotions and leaps of imagination] and that they say what they do not do?") (Surah al-Shu'ara, verses 224-226).

Of course, at the end of those same verses, it excludes the believing and righteous poets whose art is in the service of their goals, honoring them and separating their account from others.

But in any case, the Prophet cannot be a poet, and when it says: God did not teach him poetry, it means he is far removed from poetry, because all teachings return to the Pure Essence of Allah.

Interestingly, it has been repeatedly narrated in histories and traditions that whenever the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to cite a poem as an example or as a witness to his speech, he would break its meter so that no excuse would fall into the hands of the enemy. For instance, one day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to recite this famous Arabic poem:

ستبدى لك الايام ما كنت جاهلا / و ياتيك بالاخبار من لم تزود "Soon the days will reveal to you truths of which you were ignorant—and news will be brought to you by those for whom you provided no sustenance."

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to recite the above poem, he said:

"ياتيك من لم تزود بالاخبار"

And he rearranged the sentence [1]. The Quran, in addition to denying poetry for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, adds: "These verses are nothing but a means of awakening and a clear Quran" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَ قُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ).

"The purpose of it is to warn those who are alive, and that the word [of punishment] may be proved true against the disbelievers" (لِيُنْذِرَ مَنْ كانَ حَيًّا وَ يَحِقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ) [2]. Yes, these verses are a "Dhikr" (Reminder) and a source of recollection and a means of awakening; these verses are a "Clear Quran" that expresses the truth with decisiveness and clarity without any concealment, and for this reason, it is a factor for awakening, life, and vitality.

Once again, we see here that the Quran regards faith as "life," the believers as "the living," and the faithless as "the dead." On one side, the title "living" (Hayy) is placed, and on the opposite side, the title "disbelievers" (Kafirin). This is that spiritual life and death that goes far beyond physical death and life, and its effects are more extensive and broader. If life were merely "breathing," "eating," and "walking," this is something in which all animals share; this is not human life.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

[2]: The phrase "To warn..." (Liyundhira...) relates to "Dhikr" in the previous verse, and some have considered it related to "We taught" or "We sent down" which is implied, but the first possibility seems more appropriate.

Human life is the blossoming of the flowers of intellect, wisdom, and outstanding faculties in the human soul, as well as piety, altruism, sacrifice, self-control, virtue, and ethics; and the Quran is the nurturer of this life within human beings.

In any case, humans are divided into two groups in the face of the Quran's call:

One group is alive and awake, responding to its call and paying heed to its warnings. The other group consists of the dead-hearted disbelievers who never show a positive reaction to it, but this warning serves as a completion of the proof (itman al-hujjah) against them and the realization of the decree of punishment upon them.

Key Points The "Life" and "Death" of Hearts!

Man possesses several types of life and death:

First is "vegetative" life and death, which is the manifestation of growth, development, nutrition, and reproduction, and in this regard, he is aligned with all plants.

Another is "animal" life and death, the prominent signs of which are "sensation" and "movement," and in these two characteristics, he is also accompanied by all animals.

But there is a third type of life that is specific to humans and separates them from plants and other animals, and that is "human and spiritual" life. This is what is referred to in Islamic traditions as "Life of the Hearts" (Hayat al-Qulub), where "heart" here refers to the "soul, intellect, and emotions" of man.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 18, p. 442

The words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in the sermons and short sayings of Nahj al-Balagha place great emphasis on this issue. In a sermon regarding the Quran, he says:

تفقهوا فيه فانه ربيع القلوب "Reflect upon the Quran, for in it is the life-giving spring of the hearts." [1]

And elsewhere regarding wisdom and knowledge, he says:

هى حياة للقلب الميت "It is the life for the dead heart." [2]

Sometimes he contrasts the sickness of the heart with the sickness of the body, saying:

و اشد من مرض البدن مرض القلب "Worse than the sickness of the body is the sickness of the heart." [3]

And at times he says: "Whoever's spirit of piety (wara') decreases, his heart dies":

و من قل ورعه مات قلبه [4]

And many other similar expressions.

On the other hand, the Holy Quran posits a specific type of vision, hearing, understanding, and consciousness for man other than the apparent vision, hearing, and consciousness. As it says regarding the disbelievers: صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ فَهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ ("Deaf, dumb, and blind—so they do not understand!") (Baqarah-171).

Elsewhere, it calls the hypocrites "those in whose hearts is a disease" and Allah increases their disease: فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً (Baqarah-10).

And those who have no fear of God within them are described as hard-hearted ones whose hearts are harder than solid rock: ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ (مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ) فَهِيَ كَالْحِجارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً ("Then your hearts became hardened [after that], being like stones or even harder") (Baqarah-74).

And regarding a group of disbelievers, it uses an expression that introduces them as "unclean-hearted," saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ ("Those are the ones whose hearts Allah does not intend to purify") (Ma'idah-41).

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 110.

[2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 133.

[3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, 388.

[4]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, 349.

Elsewhere it says: "Only the living who have a listening ear respond to your call, not the dead!": إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَ الْمَوْتى‌ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ ("Only those who hear will respond. As for the dead, Allah will resurrect them in the Resurrection, then to Him they will be returned") (An'am-36).

From the sum of these expressions and many other similar ones, it becomes clearly evident that the Quran considers the axis of life and death to be the human and rational axis, because the entire value of man lies in this very part.

In reality, life, vitality, perception, sight, hearing, and the like are summarized in this part of human existence, although some commentators have considered these expressions metaphorical. However, they have not harmonized here with the spirit of the Quran, because from the Quran's perspective, this is the reality, and animal life and death are nothing more than a metaphor! The factors of spiritual life and death are many, but what is certain is that "hypocrisy," "arrogance," "pride," "prejudice," "ignorance," and major sins kill the "heart," as we read in the Munajat al-Ta'ibin (The Prayer of the Penitent) from the fifteen whispered prayers of Imam Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام:

و امات قلبى عظيم جنايتى "My great crime has killed my heart." [1]

The verses under discussion are also an emphasis on this reality.

Are those who are satisfied with life only to live in a world of ignorance and constant indulgence—neither hearing the cry of the oppressed, nor responding to the call of the heralds of truth, nor being troubled by the oppression of the tyrant, nor being moved by the deprivation of the downtrodden—thinking only of themselves and being strangers to others and even to their own true selves, truly alive?! Is this life, the product of which is only consuming a certain amount of food, wearing out a number of clothes, and repetitive sleeping and waking?

If life is this, what is the difference between the animal and the human world?

Therefore, it must be accepted that beyond this outward appearance of life, there is a core and a reality upon which the Quran emphasizes and of which it speaks.

Interestingly, the dead whose death possesses the effects of human life are, in the view of the Quran, alive; but the living in whom none of the effects of human life are seen are, in the logic of the Quran, dead—an agonizing and pathetic death.

[1]: The Fifteen Whispered Prayers of Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام), First Prayer (The Whispered Prayer of the Penitent).

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا فَهُمْ لَهَا مَـٰلِكُونَ (٧١)
Have they not seen that We have created for them—of what Our hands have worked—cattle, so they have become their masters? (71)
وَذَلَّلْنَـٰهَا لَهُمْ فَمِنْهَا رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْهَا يَأْكُلُونَ (٧٢)
And We made them tractable for them; so some of them make their mounts and some of them they eat. (72)
وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَمَشَارِبُ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
There are other benefits for them therein, and drinks. Will they not then give thanks? (73)
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٧٤)
They have taken gods besides Allah, [hoping] that they might be helped [by the fake deities]. (74)
لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُندٌ مُّحْضَرُونَ (٧٥)
[But] they cannot help them, while they [themselves] are an army mobilized for their defence. (75)
فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٦)
So do not let their remarks grieve you. We indeed know whatever they hide and whatever they disclose. (76)

Commentary

The Abundant Benefits of Cattle for You

Once again, the Holy Quran returns to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and Polytheism (Shirk) in these verses. While enumerating some of the signs of Allah's greatness in human life and His fulfillment of their needs, it points to the weakness, inability, and helplessness of idols. Through a clear comparison, it reveals the truth of the path of Monotheism and the falsehood of the path of Polytheism.

First, it says: "Do they not see that We have created for them, from what Our hands have fashioned, cattle of which they are masters?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا أَنْعاماً فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ‌) [1]

And so that they may properly benefit from these cattle, "We have subdued them for them" (وَ ذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ‌). "So some of them they use for riding, and some of them they eat" (فَمِنْها رَكُوبُهُمْ وَ مِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ‌).

Their benefits do not end here; rather, "And they have [other] benefits in them and [various] drinks" (وَ لَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ وَ مَشارِبُ‌).

[1]: The phrase "أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا..." (Do they not see...) is a sentence coordinated with the previous sentence by the conjunction "waw" (and). However, since the interrogative "hamza" always takes precedence in position, it precedes the conjunction "waw." "Vision" here may mean "knowing" or "looking."

"Will they not then give thanks?" (أَ فَلا يَشْكُرُونَ‌). Gratitude is a means of attaining Ma'rifat Allah (knowledge of Allah) and recognizing the Provider of blessings.

Several points are noteworthy here

  1. Among the various blessings in which man is immersed, the blessing of the existence of cattle is highlighted here. This is because they are constantly present in man's daily life; human life is so intertwined with them that if they were removed from the scene of his life, matters would truly become difficult and complicated for him. Yet, due to their constant presence, they are seldom given due attention.

  2. The phrase "عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا" (Our hands have fashioned/done) is a metaphor for the direct exercise of the Lord's power. This is because the hands are the most important organs through which a human exercises power; therefore, "hand" (yad) is a metaphor for power. For example, someone might say, "Such a region is in my hand," meaning it is under the sway of my power. The Holy Quran says: يَدُ اللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ‌: "The hand of Allah is over their hands" (Al-Fath, 10). In any case, the mention of "hands" (aydi) in the plural form refers to the various manifestations of the Lord's power.

  3. The phrase "فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ‌" (and they are masters/owners of them), using the "fa" of consequence (fa al-tafri'), indicates that We created the cattle by Our power but granted their ownership to humans. This expresses the ultimate grace of the Lord. Therefore, the objection raised by some commentators regarding the "fa" of consequence here is misplaced. It is exactly like telling someone, "We have developed this garden, but you enjoy its fruits," which is a sign of ultimate love, altruism, and generosity.

  4. The phrase "ذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ‌" (We subdued them for them) refers to the important matter of cattle being tamed for man. These strong and powerful animals—which occasionally, by Allah's command, forget the "subservience" and rise in rebellion and fury—become so dangerous that dozens of people are helpless against them. Yet, in a normal state, sometimes a train of camels is tied to a rope and handed to a child of a few years, and "it goes wherever his heart desires!" It is truly wondrous: humans are neither capable of creating a fly, nor can they even tame a fly for themselves, yet Allah, the Capable and Gracious, has created millions upon millions of different cattle and made them subservient to humans, constantly in their service.

  5. The phrase "فَمِنْها رَكُوبُهُمْ وَ مِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ‌" (considering that rakub is an adjective meaning "that which is ridden") indicates that humans choose some cattle as mounts and some for food. Although the meat of all common cattle is permissible (halal) in Islam, in practice, only a portion of them is used for food; for instance, no one uses donkey meat except in cases of necessity. This applies if we take "minha" (from them) in both phrases to mean "partitive" regarding individuals. However, if the first is partitive regarding individuals and the second is partitive regarding parts, the meaning would be that you make some cattle your mounts and eat from some parts of their bodies (since bones and the like are not edible).

  6. The phrase "لَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ‌" (they have benefits in them) refers to the many other advantages humans derive from cattle, including their wool for various types of clothing, carpets, and tents; and their hides for clothing, shoes, hats, and various life utilities. Even today, when industries have completely transformed the face of human life, the dire need of humans for cattle—both for food and for clothing and other life tools—remains strong. Even today, various serums and vaccines, which are the most effective means for fighting or preventing diseases, are prepared using cattle and substances taken from their blood. Even the most seemingly worthless things in the life of cattle, which are their droppings, are utilized; lands are cultivated and trees are made fruitful by them.

  7. The expression "مَشارِبُ‌" (drinks) refers to the milk obtained from various cattle. A significant portion of human nutritional needs is met by it and its derivatives, such that the dairy industry and milk products in the world today constitute a major part of countries' exports and imports. This same milk, which is a complete food for man, emerges from between "excrement" and "blood" as "pure milk, palatable" (labanan sa'ighan) for those who drink it—a source of pleasure for the drinkers and a source of strength for the weak. [1]

  8. The phrase "أَ فَلا يَشْكُرُونَ‌" (Will they not then give thanks?), presented as a rhetorical question, is intended to arouse human nature and emotions toward gratitude for the endless blessings, a fraction of which was mentioned in the above verses. As we know, the "necessity of thanking the Benefactor" is a foundation for "knowing Allah" (Ma'rifat Allah), because gratitude is impossible without recognizing the Giver of the blessing. Furthermore, studying these blessings and the fact that idols have no involvement or influence in them serves as a means to invalidate polytheism.

Therefore, in the subsequent verses, it describes the state of the polytheists, saying: "And they have taken besides Allah gods, hoping that they might be helped" (and be supported by the idols) (وَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ آلِهَةً لَعَلَّهُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ‌). What a naive fantasy and false thought! To place these weak beings—who have no power to defend themselves, let alone others—alongside the Creator of the heavens and the earth and the Giver of all those bounties, and to seek help from them in the difficult events of life? Yes, they sometimes turned to idols so that they might be a source of honor for them: "And they have taken besides Allah gods that they might be for them a source of honor" (Maryam, 81). And sometimes they imagined them to be intercessors before Allah: "And they worship besides Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them, and they say, 'These are our intercessors with Allah'" (Yunus, 18). In any case, all these imaginations are naught. As the Quran states in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 192: "These idols can neither help their worshippers, nor can they even help themselves" (وَ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْراً وَ لا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَنْصُرُونَ‌).

Thus, in the next verse, it adds: "They are not able to help them, and they [the worshippers] will be for them a host brought forth [for punishment]" (لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَ هُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ‌). How painful it is that these followers will be placed as a host behind the idols on that Day, and all shall be brought before the court of Allah's justice, and thereafter all shall be sent to Hell, without being able to untie any knot for their "army." In principle, the expression "مُحْضَرُونَ‌" (brought forth/presented) everywhere is a sign of humiliation; bringing individuals forth without their own desire is a sign of their insignificance. According to this interpretation, the first pronoun "hum" (they) in the phrase "وَ هُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ‌" refers to the "worshippers," and the second pronoun refers to the "deities." However, some commentators have also suggested the opposite possibility: that the deities and idols will be the "army" of their worshippers on that Day, yet despite being an army, they are capable of the least help! But the first interpretation is more appropriate. In any case, these expressions apply only to deities with consciousness, such as devils and the arrogant among jinn and men. However, the possibility also exists that on that Day, Allah will create intellect and consciousness even in the idols made of stone and wood so that they may reproach their worshippers; meanwhile, these stones and woods will be alongside them as fuel for Hell, as the Quran says in Al-Anbiya, verse 98: "Indeed, you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of Hell. You will [surely] come to it."

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, as a consolation to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to strengthen his morale against all these obstructions, provocations, and superstitious thoughts and actions, it says: "So let not their speech grieve you. Indeed, We know what they conceal and what they declare" (فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ قَوْلُهُمْ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَ ما يُعْلِنُونَ‌). Neither are their intentions hidden from Us, nor their secret conspiracies, nor their overt denials and mischief. We know it all and We keep their account for the Day of Reckoning, and We shall protect you from their evil in this world as well. Not only the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but every believer can be heartened by this Divine word: that everything in this universe is in the presence of Allah, and nothing of the enemies' schemes will be hidden from Him. He will not leave His friends alone in difficult moments and will always be their Supporter and Protector.

Key Point

The monotheistic vision creates a specific course of action in life for God-worshippers, which separates them from polytheistic lines based on choosing idols or seeking refuge in weak humans like themselves. To speak more clearly: in today's world, which has become bipolar and is dominated by two great powers of East and West, the thought usually occurs to many small and medium-sized countries that to preserve themselves, they must seek refuge in one of these two "idols" and come under its sphere of protection. However, experience has shown that during the occurrence of severe events, problems, and crises, these seemingly great powers can neither untie a knot for themselves nor for their satellites and followers. How beautifully the Quran says: "They are not able to help them, nor can they help themselves." This is a warning to all Muslims and followers of pure Monotheism to break away from all these idols and seek refuge in the shadow of Divine grace. They should rely only on themselves and the power of faith and spirituality of Islamic societies. They must never allow these polytheistic thoughts—that they must seek help from these powers for a rainy day—into their minds. In principle, they should purge the culture of Islamic societies of such thoughts and know that they have suffered many blows thus far—both in confronting the usurping Israel and against other enemies—because of this. Had this fundamental Quranic principle governed them, they would never have been afflicted by these painful defeats. We hope for the day when we all renovate our thoughts under the shadow of this Quranic teaching, rely on ourselves, seek refuge in the shadow of Allah's grace, and live honorably and free.

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated in most commentaries that a man from the polytheists named "Ubayy ibn Khalaf," "Umayyah ibn Khalaf," or "As ibn Wa'il" found a piece of decayed bone and said: "With this firm evidence, I shall rise to dispute with Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and invalidate his words regarding the Resurrection." He took it and came to the Prophet of Islam (perhaps crushing some of it in the Prophet's presence and scattering it on the ground) and said: "Who can give life to these decayed bones (and what intellect would believe such a thing)?"

The above verses and the four verses following them, totaling seven verses, were revealed, providing a logical and crushing response to him and those who think like him.

Commentary

The First Creation is Decisive Proof of Resurrection

We have mentioned that discussions regarding the Origin (Allah), the Resurrection, and Prophethood are presented in various segments in Surah "Ya-Sin," which is the heart of the Quran. This Surah of the Holy Quran began with the issue of Prophethood and concludes with seven cohesive verses containing the most powerful statements regarding the Resurrection.

First, it takes the hand of man and leads him back to the beginning of his own life, to that day when he was nothing more than an insignificant drop of fluid (nutfah), and compels him to reflect. It says: "Does man not see that We created him from a drop of fluid? Then behold, he became a clear adversary, strong, powerful, possessing intellect and speech, even rising to argue against his Lord and becoming an open disputant!" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَ الْإِنْسانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ) [^1] What a vivid and expressive description! First, it emphasizes the title "man" (al-insan), meaning every human being, regardless of their belief, school of thought, or level of knowledge, can perceive this truth.

Then it speaks of the "nutfah," which linguistically originally means a small, insignificant amount of water, so that this arrogant and self-satisfied human might reflect a little

[^1]: "Khasim" (خصیم) refers to one who persists in hostility and argumentation, and "ru'yah" (seeing) here means knowing.

and realize what he was on the first day. Furthermore, that entire insignificant drop of water was not the origin of his growth; rather, a tiny living cell, invisible to the eye, from among thousands of cells swimming in that drop of water, combined with a tiny living cell located in the woman's womb, and man stepped into the realm of existence from that microscopic entity! He traversed the stages of evolution one after another, six of which, according to the Quran at the beginning of Surah Al-Mu'minun, take place within the womb (the stage of nutfah, then the clinging clot, then the chewed-like lump, then the appearance of bones, then the clothing of bones with flesh, and finally the emergence of the soul, meaning sensation and movement).

After birth, when he was a very weak and helpless infant, he also rapidly moved past the stages of development until he reached the limit of physical and intellectual maturity.

Yes, this weak and helpless creature became so strong and powerful that he allowed himself to rise in aggression against the call of "Allah," consigning his past and future to oblivion, and becoming a clear manifestation of a "clear adversary" (khasim mubin).

Interestingly, the expression "clear adversary" (khasim mubin) possesses both an aspect of strength and an aspect of weakness, both of which the Quran apparently alludes to here.

On one hand, such an act is only possible for a human being who possesses intellect, thought, consciousness, independence of will, choice, and power. (And we know that the most important matter in human life is speech—words whose content is first prepared in thought, then placed into the mold of sentences, and ejected from the mouth like bullets fired from a machine gun toward a target. This is a feat achieved by no living creature except man).

In this way, He visualizes God's display of power in this great force He has given to an insignificant drop of water.

But on the other hand, he is a forgetful and arrogant being, using these blessings bestowed upon him by his Benefactor against Him, rising to argue and dispute. What ignorance and stubbornness!

For his ignorance, it is enough that "he presents an example for Us and, in his own imagination, thinks he has found a crushing argument, while he has forgotten his own creation, saying: 'Who can give life to these bones when they are decayed?'" (وَ ضَرَبَ لَنا مَثَلًا وَ نَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَ هِيَ رَمِيمٌ) [^1] The meaning of "striking an example" here is not an ordinary proverb, metaphor, or allusion; rather, it means presenting an argument and mentioning a specific instance to prove a general point.

Yes, he (Ubayy ibn Khalaf, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, or As ibn Wa'il) finds a piece of decayed bone in the desert—a bone whose owner was unknown.

Did they die a natural death? Or were they killed in a gruesome manner in one of the wars of the Age of Ignorance? Or did they perish from hunger? In any case, he thought he had found a crushing reason to negate the Resurrection. With anger mixed with joy, he picked up the piece of bone, saying: "I shall surely dispute with Muhammad" (with this very evidence) such that he will not be able to answer! He hurried to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and shouted: "Tell me, who

[^1]: "Ramim" (رمیم) is from the root "ramm" (رم). According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, the root "ramm" (rhyming with "dhamm") means to repair or restore something old and decayed. "Rimmah" (with a 'h' sound) specifically refers to decayed bone, and "rummah" (rhyming with "qubbah") refers to a decayed rope.

has the power to clothe this decayed bone with the garment of life?" Then he crushed part of the bone and scattered it on the ground, thinking the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) would have no answer to this logic.

Interestingly, the Holy Quran has provided his entire answer with the short phrase "وَ نَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ" (and he forgot his own creation), even though it mentions further explanations and more evidence afterward.

It says: If you had not forgotten your own creation, you would never have resorted to such a hollow and weak argument. O forgetful man! Look back and behold your own creation—how you were an insignificant drop of fluid, and every day He clothed you in a new garment of life. You are constantly in a state of death and resurrection; from being inanimate matter you died and became a growing plant, and from the world of plants you died and emerged as an animal, and from the animal world you died and became a human. But you, the forgetful one, have cast all of this into the vault of oblivion, and now you ask: "Who will give life to this decayed bone?!" When this bone completely decays, it simply becomes dust; were you not dust on the first day?!

Therefore, He immediately commands the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to "Say to this stubborn, arrogant, and forgetful one: He will give them life Who produced them the first time" (قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

If today a decayed bone remains as a relic of him, there was a day when even this decayed bone did not exist, and not even dust existed. Yes, He Who created him from the depths of non-existence finds it easier to renew the life of a decayed bone.

And if you think: "When this decayed bone becomes dust and is scattered everywhere, who can recognize those parts and gather them from different places?"

The answer to that is also clear: "He is Knower of every creation" and He knows all their characteristics (وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ).

One who possesses such "knowledge" and such "power" will face no difficulty in the matter of the Resurrection and the reviving of the dead.

If we move a magnet through tons of soil in which small particles of iron are scattered, it immediately collects all those particles, even though it is nothing more than an inanimate object. Allah can easily gather all the particles of every human's body, wherever they may be on any corner of the earth, with a single command.

Not only is He aware of the origin of man's creation, but He is also aware of their intentions and deeds, and their reckoning is clear before Him.

Therefore, the calculation of deeds, intentions, and inner beliefs also poses no problem for Him, as stated in verse 284 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "وَ إِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللَّهُ" (Whether you show what is within yourselves or conceal it, Allah will bring you to account for it). For this reason, Moses (عليه السلام) was commanded to say in response to Pharaoh—who doubted the Resurrection and expressed amazement at the reviving of previous generations and their reckoning: "The knowledge thereof is with my Lord in a Record. My Lord neither errs nor forgets" (قالَ عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتابٍ لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَ لا يَنْسى) (Taha: 52).

Ayah

ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ ٱلْأَخْضَرِ نَارًا فَإِذَآ أَنتُم مِّنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ (٨٠)
He, who made for you fire out of the green tree, and, behold, you light fire from it! (80)

Commentary

The Resurrection of Energies!

Following the previous verses which discussed the Hereafter and provided meaningful and vivid indications regarding the possibility of the Resurrection and the removal of any perceived improbability, the verses under discussion—which are the final verses of Surah Ya-Sin, the "Heart of the Quran"—present a more detailed and eloquent explanation of this issue, pursuing it through three or four fascinating paths.

First, He says: "The same God who produced for you fire from the green tree, so that with it you kindle [fire], is capable of clothing these decayed bones with the garment of life once again" (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الشَّجَرِ الْأَخْضَرِ ناراً فَإِذا أَنْتُمْ مِنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ).

What a strange and fascinating expression! The more we reflect upon it, the deeper the meanings it reveals to us. In principle, many verses of the Quran possess several layers of meaning: some are simple for the general public to understand in every time and place; some are deep for the elite; and finally, some are profoundly deep for the foremost of the elite, or for other eras, centuries, and the distant future.

At the same time, these meanings do not contradict one another; they are gathered simultaneously within a single meaningful expression.

The above verse is exactly like this:

The first interpretation mentioned by many earlier commentators—a simple and clear meaning understandable to the general public—is as follows:

In ancient times, it was common among the Arabs to use the wood of specific trees called "Markh" and "'Afar," which grew in the deserts of Hijaz, to ignite fire.

"Markh" and "'Afar" were two types of "fire-striking" wood; the former was placed underneath and the latter was struck against it, producing sparks like a flint stone. In fact, they used them instead of modern-day matches.

The Quran says: "The God who can bring fire out of these green trees has the power to clothe the dead with the garment of life."

"Water" and "fire" are two opposites. He who can place them side by side has the power to place "life" next to "death" and "death" next to "life"! Praise be to the Creator of Existence who keeps "fire" in the heart of "water" and "water" in the heart of "fire"; surely, for Him, clothing the frames of dead humans with the garment of life is not a difficult task.

If we take a step beyond this meaning, we reach a more precise interpretation: the property of igniting fire through tree wood is not exclusive to "Markh" and "'Afar" wood; rather, this property exists in all trees and all physical bodies in the world (although the two aforementioned woods, due to their specific substances and state, are more prepared for this task).

In short, all tree wood will produce sparks if struck firmly together, even "the wood of green trees."

For this reason, sometimes vast and terrifying fires occur in the heart of forests without any human agent; only the blowing of strong winds and storms that strike tree branches firmly against each other, causing a spark to fall among dry leaves—followed by the wind fanning that fire—is the primary cause.

This is the same electrical spark that appears due to friction and rubbing.

This is the same fire that lies hidden within all the particles of the world's beings, revealing itself during friction and rubbing, creating "fire" (nar) from the "green tree" (shajar akhdar)! This is a broader interpretation that expands the horizon of the union of opposites in creation and shows "permanence" within "annihilation" more clearly.

However, there is a third interpretation here that is even deeper, which we have grasped with the help of modern sciences, and we have named it "The Resurrection of Energies."

To explain: one of the important functions of plants is the process of "carbon-fixing" from the air and the building of "plant cellulose" (cellulose is the mass of trees, whose main components are carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen).

Now let us see how this cellulose is made: the cells of trees and plants take "carbon dioxide" from the air and decompose it, releasing the oxygen and keeping the carbon within themselves, combining it with water to build the wood of the trees.

But the important point is that, according to the testimony of natural sciences, every chemical synthesis that takes place must either be accompanied by the absorption of a specific energy or the release of it (pay close attention).

Therefore, when trees are engaged in the process of carbon-fixing, according to this law, they require the presence of an energy; here, they use the heat and light of the sun as an active energy.

In this way, during the formation of tree wood, a portion of the sun's energy is stored within them. When we "burn" the wood, so to speak, that same stored solar energy is released, because once again "carbon" combines with the "oxygen" of the air to form carbon dioxide, and oxygen and hydrogen (a quantity of water) are released.

Leaving aside these technical terms, in very simple words: this pleasant light and heat that warms and illuminates a villager's hut or a city dweller's charcoal heater in winter is the same solar light and heat that has been stored within the wood of these trees over several years or decades. What the tree gradually took from the sun throughout its life, it now gives back in full! And when it is said that all energies on Earth return to solar energy, this is one of its facets.

This is where we reach the "Resurrection of Energies." We see that the light and heat scattered in this space, which nurtured and caressed the leaves and wood of the trees, was never destroyed; rather, it changed its form and was hidden from our human eyes within the particles of the wood, branches, and leaves. When a flame reaches the dried wood, their "resurrection" begins, and all that was hidden of the sun's energy in the tree appears in that moment of "gathering and rising," without it having decreased by even the amount of a candle's light for a short duration! (Again, pay close attention).

Undoubtedly, this meaning was not clear to the masses at the time of the verse's revelation, but as we said, this poses no problem, for the verses of the Quran possess multi-layered meanings at different levels and for varying capacities.

One day they understood something from this verse, and today we understand more, and perhaps future generations will go even further and understand more. At the same time, all these meanings are correct, fully acceptable, and gathered within the meaning of the verse.

Key Points

1- Why "Green Tree"?

It might occur to the mind: why did the Quran use the expression "shajar akhdar" (green tree) here? While igniting fire with wet wood is very difficult, would it not have been better to say "al-shajar al-yabis" (dry wood) to be consistent with this context? But the fascinating point here is that only green trees can perform the process of carbon-fixing and storing solar light. If dry trees are exposed to sunlight for hundreds of years, not a single bit is added to their store of thermal energy; they are only capable of this important task when they are green and alive.

Therefore, it is only the "green tree" that can create "fuel" (waqud) for us and keep heat and light in a mysterious way within its cold and moist wood. As soon as they dry up, the process of carbon-fixing and storing solar energy ceases.

Based on this principle, the above expression is both a beautiful depiction of the Resurrection of Energies and an eternal scientific miracle of the Holy Quran! Furthermore, if we return to the other interpretations mentioned above, the expression "green tree" is still appropriate and beautiful, because the wood of green trees, when subjected to strong friction, produces sparks—sparks that can be the origin of igniting a fire. Here we realize the greatness of God's power, who has preserved fire in the heart of water and water in the heart of fire.

2- The Difference Between Fuel and Igniter

"Tuqidun" is derived from the root "waqud" (on the pattern of qubur), meaning the lighting of a fire. "Iqad" means to kindle a fire, and "waqud" (on the pattern of thamud) means the firewood used to kindle the fire.

Therefore, the phrase فَإِذا أَنْتُمْ مِنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ (so that from it you kindle fire) refers to the firewood with which they light the fire; in other words, it refers to the "fuel" (fire-taker) rather than the "igniter" (fire-striker).

To explain: in Persian, we call firewood "atash-gireh" (fuel) and matches or lighters "atash-zaneh" (igniter). In Arabic, firewood is called "waqud" and matches or lighters are called "zand" or "zinad" 1.

Therefore, the Quran says: "The God who made fire for you from the green tree, and you prepare fuel from it (He does not say igniter), He is capable of bringing the dead back to life." This expression is perfectly consistent with the Resurrection of Energies (pay close attention). [2] In any case, the issue of igniting fire with tree wood, though it seems a simple matter to us, is revealed upon reflection to be among the most wondrous matters. For the substances of which a tree is composed are largely water and some terrestrial components, none of which are flammable. What power is this that has created from water and earth (and air) this energy-producing substance with which human life has been closely linked for thousands of years?!

[2]: Unless we take "min" in the phrase "minhu tuqidun" to mean "with," so that it aligns with the other interpretations.
