Commentary

He is the Owner and Sovereign over all things!

After mentioning the proofs for the Resurrection by drawing attention to the first creation and the creation of fire from the green tree in the preceding verses, the first verse under discussion pursues this issue through a third path: the infinite power of Allah.

It says: "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth, with all their greatness, wonders, and astonishing systems, able to create the like of these [humans] who have turned to dust? (And return them to a new life?) Yes, He can, and He is the Knowing Creator" (أَ وَ لَيْسَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِقادِرٍ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ بَلى‌ وَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

This sentence, which begins with a "rhetorical question," in reality poses a question to awakened consciences and alert minds: Do you not look at this vast heaven with all its amazing fixed stars and planets, and with all its solar systems and galaxies, each corner of which is a vast world?! How could One who is capable of creating such extraordinarily great and orderly realms not be capable of reviving the dead?! And since the answer to this question is ready in the heart and soul of every awakened human, He does not wait for an answer, but immediately says: "Yes, He has such power," and following that, He emphasizes two great attributes of Allah that must be considered in relation to this issue—namely, His attribute of Creativity and His infinite Knowledge—which is, in fact, a proof for the preceding statement: if your doubt stems from His power over creation, He is the Khallaq (the All-Creator—note that Khallaq is an intensive form).

And if gathering these particles requires knowledge and science, He is All-Knowing and Aware in every respect.

Regarding what the pronoun "their like" (mithlahum) refers to, commentators have offered several possibilities, but the most famous is that it refers to "humans," meaning the Creator of the heavens and the earth is capable of creating the like of humans.

Here, the question arises: why did He not say, "He is able to create them again," but instead said, "the like of them"?

Several answers have been proposed to this question. What seems closer [to the truth] is that when the human body turns to dust, it loses its form, and when it returns on the Day of Resurrection, those same previous materials take on a form like the first form; that is, the matter is the same matter, but the form is similar to the past form, because that exact [instance of] form, especially considering the constraint of time, cannot return. This is particularly true since we know that in the Resurrection, humans are not resurrected with all their past conditions; for example, the elderly will be in the form of youth, and the disabled will be in a healthy form.

In other words: the human body is like a brick that has crumbled; its dust is gathered, turned into clay once more, poured into a mold, and a new brick is made from it.

This new brick is, from one perspective, the very same thing, and from another perspective, the like of it (its matter is the same matter and its form is like that form—reflect on this). [1]

The next verse is an emphasis on what passed in the previous verses, an emphasis on the fact that any kind of creation is easy and simple before His will and power. Creating

[1]: Some commentators have referred the pronoun "their like" (mithlahum) back to the heavens and the earth, stating that the choice of the rational plural pronoun here is because there are many rational beings in the earth and heaven. Others have taken the expression "like" as evidence that the return of the exact same body and materials that were in the world is not necessary, because the human personality is defined by the soul, and this soul, to whatever matter it attaches, is "like" the human. However, it must be noted that this statement is not at all consistent with the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses—even the verses under discussion—because the Quran explicitly says in these very verses: Allah revives the same decayed bones and clothes them in the garment of life, not other materials (reflect on this).

the great heavens and the terrestrial globe is the same for Him as creating a small insect. He says: "His command is only that when He intends a thing, He says to it, 'Be,' and it is, exactly as Allah willed" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌).

Everything depends on a single sign and command from Him, and is there any room for doubt in the reviving of the dead by One who possesses such power?

It is obvious that the Divine command and decree here do not mean a verbal command; likewise, the phrase "Be" (Kun) is not a phrase that Allah expresses in verbal form, for He has no speech [in the human sense] nor is He in need of words. Rather, what is meant is the attachment of His will to the creation and innovation of something, and the expression with the word "Be" is because no expression shorter, smaller, or faster than this can be imagined.

Yes, the attachment of His will to something is one and the same as its becoming existent! In other words: when Allah wills something, it is immediately realized such that there is no gap between His "will" and the "existence of things." Therefore, the expressions "command," "saying," and the phrase "Be" are all explanations for the issue of creation and origination, and as we said, there is no verbal command, saying, speech, or the letters "Kaf" and "Nun" involved here. All of these describe the rapid realization of things after the attachment of the Divine will. What need does He have for words and phrases? Fundamentally, after His volition is attached to the creation of something, the mediation of words is meaningless.

To put it even more clearly, there are no more than two stages in the actions of Allah:

The stage of Will, and the stage of Creation, the latter of which is expressed in the above verse as command, saying, and the phrase "Be."

A group of early commentators imagined that there is a saying and speech involved here, and they consider it one of the unknown secrets. In reality, they became trapped in the twists and turns of the word and remained unaware of its content and meaning, measuring Divine acts by the scale of their own existence.

How beautifully the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha:

"He says to what He intends to bring into being, 'Be,' and it is [1], not with a sound that strikes [the ear], nor with a cry that is heard. Rather, the speech of the Sublimely Purified is an act of His which He originated, and its like did not exist before that; had it been eternal, it would have been a second god." [2] Furthermore, if a word were involved, two problems would present themselves:

First, this word itself is a creature among creatures, and to create it, another word "Be" would be necessary, and this statement would be repeated for the second "Be," leading to an infinite regress.

Second, every address requires an addressee, and when something has not yet come into existence, how can Allah address it with the phrase "Be"? Can the non-existent be addressed?

[1]: In some editions of Nahj al-Balagha, such as the "Minhaj al-Bara'ah" edition, the expression is "to what He intended" (lima arada). The Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn also quotes it this way from Nahj al-Balagha, but in other editions like those of Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Ibn Maytham, and Subhi Salih, it appears as "to whom He intended" (liman arada), though the former is more appropriate.

[2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 186.

In other verses of the Quran, this same meaning appears with different expressions, such as verse 117 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (وَ إِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌). And like verse 40 of Surah Al-Nahl: "Our word for a thing when We intend it is only that We say to it, 'Be,' and it is" (إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْ‌ءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌). [1]

The last verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah "Ya-Sin," ends this discussion beautifully as a general conclusion regarding the Origin and the Resurrection. It says: "So glory be to Him in whose hand is the dominion of all things, and to Him you will be returned" (فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌). Considering that Malakut (dominion) comes from the root Mulk (sovereignty/ownership) and the addition of "waw" and "ta" to it is for emphasis and hyperbole, the concept of the verse becomes: the unconditional sovereignty and ownership of everything is in the hand of Allah's power, and such a God is sanctified and free from any kind of inability or weakness. In this case, reviving the dead and clothing decayed bones and scattered dust in the garment of life will pose no problem for Him. Since this is so, certainly all of you will return to Him, and the Resurrection is the truth!

[1]: Another discussion regarding the interpretation of the phrase "Be, and it is" (Kun fa-yakun) appears in Volume 1 under verse 117 of Al-Baqarah, page 418.

Key Points

We have repeatedly promised in this Tafsir to provide a concise discussion on the issues of the Resurrection at the end of Surah Ya-Sin. Now, to fulfill this promise and to pay a portion of the due to this issue, we draw the attention of the dear readers to the following six topics:

1- Belief in the Resurrection is an Innate (Fitri) Matter

If man were created for annihilation, he should be in love with "annihilation" and find pleasure in death—even timely death—at the end of life. However, we see that the face of death, in the sense of non-existence, has never been pleasant for man at any time; rather, he flees from it with his entire being! The effort to preserve the bodies of the dead through mummification and the building of eternal tombs like the Pyramids of Egypt, and the pursuit of the water of life, the elixir of youth, and whatever leads to longevity, is clear evidence of man's burning passion for the issue of survival.

If we were created for annihilation, what meaning could this desire for survival have? Other than being a troublesome and, at the very least, useless and futile desire! Do not forget that we pursue the discussion of the Resurrection after accepting the existence of the All-Wise and All-Knowing Allah. We believe that whatever He has created in our being is based on a purpose; therefore, the love for survival must also have a purpose, and that is harmony with creation and the world after this realm.

In other words, if the mechanism of creation produced thirst in our being, it is proof that water exists externally. Likewise, if the sexual instinct and the attraction to the opposite sex exist in man, it is a sign that the opposite sex exists externally; otherwise, attraction and pull without something to be attracted to would not be consistent with the wisdom of creation.

On the other hand, when we examine human history from distant times and the most ancient days, we find numerous signs of man's firm belief in life after death.

The remains of past humans—even prehistoric humans—available to us today, especially the manner of burying the dead, the quality of constructing graves, and even burying objects along with the dead, testify that belief in life after death was hidden within their subconscious conscience.

A famous psychologist says: "Careful research shows that the earliest tribes of humanity possessed a kind of religion, for they buried their dead in a special manner and placed their tools beside them, and in this way, they proved their belief in the existence of another world." [1] These show that these peoples had accepted life after death, even if they went astray in its interpretation and imagined that life to be exactly like this life.

In any case, this ancient, deep-rooted belief cannot be considered simple or merely the result of suggestion and habit.

Thirdly, the existence of an internal court called "conscience" is another witness to the innate nature of the Resurrection.

Every human feels a sense of peace within their conscience upon performing a good deed, a peace that sometimes cannot be described by any speech or pen.

Conversely, in the face of sins, especially great crimes, one feels distress, to the point that it is often seen that one commits suicide or surrenders to punishment and the gallows, considering it a release from the torture of conscience.

Nevertheless, man asks himself: how is it possible for a small world like my existence to have such a court and tribunal, yet the great world be void of such a conscience and court?

Thus, the innate nature of the issue of the Resurrection and life after death becomes clear to us through various paths:

Through the universal love of humans for survival.

Through the existence of this faith throughout human history.

And through the existence of a small sample of it within the human soul.

[1]: Sociology by Samuel Koenig, page 192 (slightly summarized).

2- The Reflection of the Resurrection in Human Life

Belief in the world after death, the permanence of the effects of human actions, and the immortality of one's deeds—whether good or evil—leaves a very powerful impact on the thoughts, nerves, and muscles of humans and can act as an effective factor in encouraging virtues and fighting against vices.

The effects that faith in life after death can have on reforming corrupt and deviant individuals and encouraging self-sacrificing, struggling, and altruistic individuals are far greater than the effects of ordinary courts and punishments, because the characteristics of the Court of Resurrection are very different from ordinary courts. In that court, there is no appeal, nor do gold or force affect the minds of its observers; neither is the presentation of false documents of any use, nor do its formalities require a long time.

The Holy Quran says: "And fear a Day when no soul will suffice for another soul at all, nor will intercession be accepted from it, nor will compensation be taken from it, nor will they be aided" (Al-Baqarah: 48).

And it also says: "And if each soul that had wronged possessed all that is on earth, it would [offer to] ransom itself with it. And they will confide regret when they see the punishment; and they will be judged in justice, and they will not be wronged" (Yunus: 54).

And we also read: "That Allah may recompense every soul for what it earned. Indeed, Allah is swift in account" (Ibrahim: 51).

His accounting is so swift and decisive that according to some narrations: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty settles the account of all creatures in the duration of a blink of an eye!" [1] For this reason, in the Holy Quran, the source of many sins is mentioned as forgetting the Day of Recompense. In verse 14 of Surah Al-Sajdah, it says: "So taste [the punishment] because you forgot the meeting of this, your Day."

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under verse 202 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

It is even understood from some expressions that if a person has even a suspicion of the Resurrection, they will refrain from many wrong actions, as He says regarding those who give short measure: "Do they not think that they will be resurrected for a tremendous Day?" (Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 4).

The eternal epics created by the warriors of Islam in the past and present in the fields of Jihad, and the great self-sacrifice and altruism shown by many people in defending Islamic lands and supporting the deprived and oppressed, are all reflections of the belief in the eternal life of the other realm. Scientific studies and various experiences have shown that such phenomena on a vast and extensive scale are not possible except through a belief in which life after death holds a special place.

A soldier whose logic is: "Say, 'Do you await for us except one of the two best things?'" (meaning either victory over you or reaching the honor of martyrdom) (At-Tawbah: 52) is certainly an invincible soldier.

The face of death, which is terrifying for many people of the world—who even flee from its name and anything that evokes it—is not only not unbeautiful for those who believe in life after death, but is a window to a great world, the breaking of the cage and the liberation of the human soul, the opening of the doors of the prison of the body, and the reaching of absolute freedom.

Fundamentally, the issue of the Resurrection, after the Origin, is the line of demarcation between the culture of God-worshippers and materialists, because two different viewpoints exist here:

A viewpoint that sees death as absolute annihilation and destruction, and flees from it with all its being, because everything ends with it.

And a viewpoint that considers death a new birth and stepping into a vast, wide, and bright world, and spreading wings in the boundless sky.

Naturally, the followers of this school not only do not allow fear or terror of death and martyrdom in the way of their goal to enter their hearts, but rather, inspired by the school of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who says:

"By Allah, the son of Abu Talib is more intimate with death than an infant is with its mother's breast," [1] they welcome death in the way of their goal.

And for this reason, when the sword strike of the criminal of the age, Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam, landed on his blessed brain, he said:

"I have succeeded, by the Lord of the Kaaba."

In short, faith in the Resurrection creates from a fearful and aimless person a brave, courageous, and purposeful individual whose life is full of epics, sacrifices, purity, and piety.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 5.

3- Rational Proofs for the Resurrection

Aside from the numerous traditional proofs for the Resurrection mentioned in the Holy Quran, which include hundreds of verses in this field, there are also clear rational proofs for this matter, a part of which is summarized here:

A- The Proof of Wisdom-

If we consider the life of this world without the other world, it would be futile and meaningless. It is exactly like assuming the life of the fetal period without the life of this world.

If the law of creation were such that all fetuses were suffocated and died at the moment of birth, how meaningless the fetal period would appear! Likewise, if the life of this world is imagined as severed from the other world, this confusion will exist.

For what necessity is there for us to struggle amidst difficulties for seventy years, more or less, in this world? To be raw and inexperienced for a time, and "by the time the rawness is cooked, life is over"! To pursue the acquisition of science and knowledge for a time, and when we reach a point in terms of information, the snow of old age has settled on our heads! For what do we live, after all? To eat some food, wear a few sets of clothes, sleep and wake up repeatedly, and continue this tiring, repetitive program for decades?! Is this vast sky, this wide earth, all these preliminaries and conclusions, all these teachers and educators, all these great libraries, and these intricacies used in our creation and that of other beings, really all for that eating, drinking, wearing, and material life?

This is where those who do not accept the Resurrection admit to the futility of this life, and a group of them consider committing suicide and escaping from this futile and meaningless life as permissible or a source of pride! How is it possible for someone to believe in Allah and His infinite wisdom and yet consider the life of this world significant without it being a preliminary for the eternal life of the other world?

The Holy Quran says: "Did you then think that We created you in vain and that you would not be returned to Us?" (Al-Mu'minun: 115). That is, if there were no return to Allah, the life of this world would be vain and futile.

Yes, the life of this world finds meaning and becomes consistent with Divine wisdom only if we consider this world a farm for the other world (The world is the farm of the Hereafter) and a passage to that vast realm (The world is a bridge) and a preparatory class and university for the other world and a marketplace for that abode, just as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said in his profound words:

"Indeed, the world is an abode of truth for him who is truthful with it, an abode of safety for him who understands it, an abode of richness for him who takes provision from it, and an abode of admonition for him who takes heed from it. It is the mosque of the lovers of Allah, the place of prayer for the angels of Allah, the place of descent for the revelation of Allah, and the marketplace for the friends of Allah." [1] In short, the study and examination of the state of this world clearly testify that there is another world behind it: "And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not remember [that another world follows it]?" (Al-Waqi'ah: 62).

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 131.

B- The Proof of Justice-

Scrutiny of the system of existence and the laws of creation shows that everything in it is calculated.

In the organization of our bodies, such a just system reigns that whenever the slightest change or disproportion occurs, it causes illness or death. The movements of our hearts, our blood circulation, the membranes of our eyes, and every single cell of our bodies are subject to the same precise system that governs the entire universe: "And by justice the heavens and the earth were established." [1] Can man be a misfit in this vast world?! It is true that Allah has given man freedom of will and choice to test him and so that he may traverse the path of perfection in its shadow, but what happens if man misuses this freedom? If the oppressors and tyrants, the misguided and the misguiders, continue their path by misusing this Divine gift, what does the justice of Allah require?

It is true that a group of evildoers are punished in this world and reach the recompense of their deeds—or at least a part of it—but certainly, it is not the case that all criminals see all their punishment, nor that all the pure and good reach the reward of their deeds in this world. Is it possible for these two groups to be equal in the scale of the Lord's justice? As the Holy Quran says: "Then shall We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?" (Al-Qalam: 35-36).

And elsewhere it says: "Or should We treat those who fear Allah like the wicked?" (Sad: 28).

In any case, the difference between humans in obeying the command of Truth is beyond doubt, just as the inadequacy of the "court of worldly retribution," the "court of conscience," and the "reactions to sins" for establishing justice alone does not seem sufficient. Therefore, one must accept that for the implementation of Divine justice, it is necessary for there to be a general court and tribunal of justice where the smallest good and bad deed is accounted for; otherwise, the principle of justice will not be fulfilled.

Therefore, it must be accepted that accepting the justice of God is equivalent to accepting the existence of the Resurrection. The Holy Quran says: "And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection" (Al-Anbiya: 47).

And it also says: "And it will be judged between them in justice, and they will not be wronged" (Yunus: 54).

[1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under verse 7 of Surah Ar-Rahman.

C- The Proof of Purpose-

Contrary to the imagination of materialists, in the Divine worldview, there was a purpose for the creation of man, which in philosophical terms is expressed as "perfection" and in the language of the Quran and Hadith is sometimes expressed as "closeness to Allah" or "worship and servitude": "And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me" (and to become perfect in the shadow of worship and servitude and find a way to the sanctuary of My proximity) (Adh-Dhariyat: 56).

If death were the end of everything, would this great goal be fulfilled?

Undoubtedly, the answer to this question is negative.

There must be a world after this world, and the line of human perfection must continue in it, and he must reap the product of this farm there, and even as we have said elsewhere, this journey must continue in the other world as well until the final goal is fulfilled.

In summary, fulfilling the purpose of creation is not possible without accepting the Resurrection, and if we sever the connection of this life from the world after death, everything takes the form of an enigma and we will have no answer for the "whys."

D- The Proof of Negating Disagreement-

Undoubtedly, we all suffer from the disagreements that exist among schools of thought and religions in this world, and we all wish for the day these disagreements are removed. While all evidence shows that these disagreements have permeated the nature of life in this world, and it is even understood from proofs that after the uprising of Imam Mahdi عليه السلام—the establisher of the single global government—although many disagreements will be resolved, the disagreement of schools will not be completely removed, and as the Holy Quran says, the Jews and Christians will remain in their disagreements until the threshold of the Resurrection: "So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection" (Al-Ma'idah: 14).

But Allah, who leads everything toward unity, will eventually end the disagreements, and since this is not fully possible in this world due to the thick veils of the material realm, we know that in the other world, which is the realm of manifestation and appearance, this matter will finally be realized, and truths will become so sunny that disagreement of school and belief will be completely removed.

Interestingly, this issue is emphasized in numerous verses of the Quran. In one place it says: "So Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ" (Al-Baqarah: 113).

And elsewhere it says: "And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths [that] Allah will not resurrect one who dies. But yes, [it is] a true promise upon Him, but most of the people do not know. [It is] so He may make clear to them that wherein they differ and so those who have disbelieved may know that they were liars" (Al-Nahl: 38-39).

4- The Quran and the Issue of the Resurrection

After the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid), which is the most fundamental issue in the teachings of the Prophets, the issue of the Resurrection, with its characteristics and educational and cultural effects, holds the primary rank. Therefore, in Quranic discussions, it occupies the most verses after Monotheism and the knowledge of God.

Quranic discussions of the Resurrection sometimes take the form of logical arguments and sometimes the form of rhetorical discussions and effective, striking suggestions that sometimes make the hair on one's body stand up upon hearing them, and the sincere tone of the speech is such that, like arguments, it penetrates the depths of the human soul and spirit.

In the first part, namely logical arguments, the Quran mostly emphasizes the subject of the possibility of the Resurrection, because deniers often imagined it to be impossible and believed that the Resurrection—especially in the form of "bodily resurrection" which necessitates the return of decayed and dusted bodies to a new life—is not possible.

In this section, the Quran enters through completely diverse and varied paths, all of which end at one point: the "rational possibility of the Resurrection."

Sometimes it visualizes the first life for man and says in a short, expressive, and clear phrase: "As He began you, you will return" (Al-A'raf: 29).

Sometimes it visualizes the life and death of plants and their resurrection, which we see every year with our eyes, and at the end of it says: your resurrection is just like this:

"And We have sent down from the sky blessed water and produced thereby gardens and grain for harvest... and We give life thereby to a dead land. Thus is the resurrection" (Surah Qaf: 9-11).

Elsewhere it says: "And it is Allah who sends the winds, and they stir the clouds, and We drive them to a dead land and give life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness. Thus is the resurrection!" (Fatir: 9).

Sometimes it raises the issue of Allah's power in the creation of the heavens and the earth, saying: "Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not tired by their creation, is able to give life to the dead? Yes, indeed, He is over all things competent" (Al-Ahqaf: 33).

And sometimes it raises the resurrection of energies and the leaping of fire from within the green tree as an example of His power, and the placement of fire in the heart of water, saying: "He who made for you from the green tree, fire" (meaning that God who clothes the dead in the garment of life created fire for you from the green tree!) (Ya-Sin: 80).

Sometimes it visualizes fetal life for man and says: "O people, if you should be in doubt about the Resurrection, then [consider that] indeed, We created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then from a clinging clot, then from a lump of flesh, formed and unformed—that We may show you [Our power]. And We settle in the wombs whom We will for a specified term, then We bring you out as a child" (Al-Hajj: 5).

And finally, it sometimes shows the specter of the Resurrection in long sleeps—sleeps that are the brother of death, or rather, in some respects, death itself—such as the 309-year sleep of the People of the Cave, and after a beautiful and interesting explanation regarding their sleep and waking, it says: "And thus We caused them to be discovered that they [the people] might know that the promise of Allah is true and that of the Hour there is no doubt" (Al-Kahf: 21).

These are six different paths raised within the verses of the Quran to explain the possibility of the Resurrection.

In addition to this, the story of the four birds of Ibrahim (Al-Baqarah: 260), the account of Uzair (Al-Baqarah: 259), and the story of the slain man of the Children of Israel (Al-Baqarah: 73)—each of which is a historical example for this issue—are other evidences and proofs that the Quran mentions in this field.

In short, the depiction that the Holy Quran provides of the Resurrection, its faces, its preliminaries, and its results, and the expressive proofs it has raised in this field, are so vivid and convincing that anyone with the slightest share of an awakened conscience will be deeply affected by them.

As some have said: about 1,200 verses of the Quran discuss the Resurrection, which, if collected and interpreted, would themselves become a thick book, and we hope that after finishing the writing of this Tafsir, when we proceed to the "Thematic Interpretation" by the will of Allah, we will make this collection available to those interested.

5- Bodily Resurrection

The meaning of bodily resurrection is not that only the body returns in the other world; rather, the goal is that the soul and body are resurrected together. In other words, the return of the soul is certain; the discussion is about the return of the body.

A group of earlier philosophers believed only in spiritual resurrection and considered the body a vehicle that is with man only in this world, and after death, he becomes independent of it, leaves it, and hastens to the world of spirits.

However, the belief of the great scholars of Islam is that the Resurrection takes place in both spiritual and bodily aspects. Here, some are not restricted to the specific previous body and say: Allah places a body at the disposal of the soul, and since the human personality is defined by his soul, this body is considered his body! While researchers believe that the same body that turned to dust and disintegrated is gathered by the command of Allah, and He clothes it in a new garment of life, and this is a belief taken from the texts of the verses of the Holy Quran.

The evidences for bodily resurrection in the Holy Quran are so numerous that it can be said with certainty: those who limit the Resurrection to the spiritual have not done the slightest study of the many verses on the Resurrection; otherwise, the bodily nature of the Resurrection is so clear in the verses of the Quran that there is no room for any doubt.

These very verses we read at the end of Surah Ya-Sin clearly express this truth, for the desert Arab's wonder was: who can revive this decayed bone he holds in his hand?

And the Quran explicitly says in response to him: "Say, 'He will give them life who produced them the first time.'"

The entire wonder of the polytheists and their opposition to the issue of the Resurrection centered on this very point: how, when we have become dust and our dust is lost in the earth, do we wear the garment of life again? "And they say, 'When we are lost within the earth, will we indeed be in a new creation?'" (Al-Sajdah, verse 10).

They used to say: "How does this man promise you that when you have died and become dust and bones, you will be brought forth?" (Al-Mu'minun, verse 35).

They wondered at this issue so much that they considered expressing it a sign of madness or a lie against Allah: "Those who disbelieve say, 'Shall we direct you to a man who informs you that when you have been disintegrated in total disintegration, you will [then] be in a new creation?'" (Surah Saba, verses 7-8).

For this reason, generally, the Quran's arguments regarding the possibility of the Resurrection revolve around this very bodily resurrection, and the sixfold statements mentioned in the previous section are all witnesses and evidence for this claim.

Furthermore, the Quran repeatedly points out that you will come out of the graves on the Day of Resurrection (Ya-Sin: 51, Al-Qamar: 7); graves pertain to bodily resurrection.

The story of Ibrahim's four birds, as well as the story of Uzair and his revival after death, and the event of the slain man of the Children of Israel, which we referred to in previous discussions, all speak explicitly of bodily resurrection.

The many descriptions the Holy Quran provides of the material and spiritual blessings of Paradise all show that the Resurrection is realized both in the stage of the body and in the stage of the soul; otherwise, Houris, palaces, various heavenly foods, and material pleasures alongside spiritual blessings would have no meaning.

In any case, it is not possible for someone to have the slightest knowledge of the logic and culture of the Quran and deny bodily resurrection; in other words, denying bodily resurrection from the Quranic perspective is equivalent to denying the principle of the Resurrection! In addition to these traditional proofs, there are also rational evidences in this field, which would lead to a lengthy discussion if we were to enter into them.

Of course, belief in bodily resurrection raises some questions and objections, such as the "eater and the eaten" (akil wa ma'kul) paradox, which Islamic researchers have answered, and we have provided a comprehensive and concise explanation in this regard under verse 260 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [1]

6- Paradise and Hell

Many imagine that the world after death is exactly like this world, only in a more perfect and interesting form.

However, we have many indications showing that there is a great distance in terms of quality and quantity between this world and that world, to the extent that even if we compare this distance to the difference between the small world of the fetus and this vast world, it still does not seem to be a complete comparison.

According to explicit narrations, there are things there that no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and have not even crossed the human mind. The Holy Quran says:

"And no soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for eyes" (As-Sajdah, verse 17).

The systems governing that world also differ completely from what governs this world. Here, individuals appear as witnesses in court, but there, the hands...

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 228 onwards.

...and the feet and even the skin of the body shall bear witness.

"That Day, We shall set a seal on their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will bear witness to what they used to earn." (Ya-Sin, Verse 65). "And they will say to their skins, 'Why have you testified against us?' They will say, 'Allah has caused us to speak, He Who causes all things to speak...'" (Fussilat, Verse 21). In any case, whatever is said regarding the next world only visualizes a distant shadow for us; fundamentally, our alphabet and our intellectual culture in this world are incapable of truly describing it. From here, many questions regarding Paradise and Hell, and the nature of their blessings and punishments, will be answered.

We know this much: Paradise is the center of all kinds of Divine favors, both material and spiritual, and Hell is a center of the severest punishments in both aspects.

However, regarding the details of these two, the Quran provides indications in which we believe, but no one knows the full details until they see them! Regarding the current existence of Paradise and Hell and their location, we had a relatively detailed discussion in Volume 2, under Verse 133 of Surah Al-Imran (page 92 onwards).

Similarly, regarding reward and punishment in the realm of Resurrection and the issue of the "Embodiment of Deeds" and the "Record of Deeds," we had a discussion in Volume 2, page 378 (under Verse 30 of Al-Imran) and Volume 12, page 455 (under Verse 49 of Surah Al-Kahf).

In addition to all these, various other discussions regarding the specifics of the Hereafter will come, God willing, under the appropriate verses, especially in the surahs toward the end of the Quran.

O Lord! On that day of fear and danger, in that Great Resurrection and Court of Justice, grant us security and tranquility through Your grace! O Allah! If the judgment is based on the standard of deeds, our hands are empty; weigh our insignificant good deeds with the scale of Your favor and generosity, and cast a veil over our sins with Your mercy and forgiveness.

O God! Make it such that in the end, You are pleased with us and we are among the successful in Your presence—Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Volume 18 of Tafsir Nemouneh 8th of Ramadan al-Mubarak, 1404 (June 8, 1984)
